question: A 49-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-year history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with movement throughout the day. Recently, she has noticed small subcutaneous nodules on her forearms. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. Her mother had similar symptoms and was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist complaining of worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She also notes swelling in her fingers and difficulty grasping objects. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand pain and morning stiffness that lasts for about 1 hour. She reports swelling in her fingers and difficulty performing daily tasks like buttoning her shirt. Her mother had a history of similar joint problems. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are also detected. X-rays of the hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of firm, painless nodules on her elbows and forearms. She reports significant fatigue, occasional low-grade fevers, and a 5-pound weight loss over the past month. She denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. Her medical history is notable for recurrent bouts of pharyngitis as a child. Vital signs are: temperature 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, pulse 78/min, respirations 16/min, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. On physical examination, several subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her arms. There is no synovial thickening or joint effusion. Laboratory workup reveals an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 85 mm/hr and normocytic anemia. A chest X-ray shows hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain, redness, and swelling in her right big toe, which have occurred intermittently over the past year. Each episode lasts for several days before resolving spontaneously. She describes the pain as sharp and throbbing, often waking her at night. She denies any recent injury or fever. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with hydrochlorothiazide. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and tender right first metatarsophalangeal joint, with a palpable nodule on the helix of her ear. Laboratory tests show normal renal function and elevated serum uric acid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because of a rash on her legs and blood in her urine for the past 4 days. She reports a history of sore throat approximately 2 weeks ago. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower legs. Laboratory studies show hematuria with red blood cell casts and mild proteinuria. Her blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She notices morning stiffness lasting over an hour, especially in her hands and wrists, which improves as the day progresses. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/h and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 12 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is 35 IU/mL (normal <14 IU/mL), and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following conditions is associated with this patient's diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers over the past year. She reports that the ulcers have been occurring every few months and typically heal after several weeks, leaving behind hyperpigmented scars. Additionally, she has been experiencing episodic joint pain and swelling, particularly in her knees and ankles. There is no significant medical history, and she is not on any regular medications. The physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers in the oral mucosa and erythematous nodules on her legs. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP levels, but rheumatoid factor is negative. HLA-B51 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman visits the clinic due to recurrent painful oral ulcers which have been recurring every few months over the past year. She also reports experiencing two episodes of genital ulcers, which she describes as painful and bothersome. The patient mentions experiencing joint pain in her knees and ankles, especially in the morning, which improves as the day progresses. She denies any ocular symptoms, fever, or weight loss. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies, and she is not on any regular medication. On physical examination, there are shallow ulcers on her buccal mucosa and signs of old healed ulcers in the genital region. Her laboratory findings show normal levels of complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain for the past two years. She notes that the pain is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. On examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, and an X-ray of the hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to your clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and morning stiffness. She reports that the stiffness lasts for about an hour after she wakes up. The pain is primarily in her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. She also notes feeling generally fatigued. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. On examination, swelling and tenderness are noted in multiple small joints of the hands and wrists, with decreased grip strength. Her laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent sinusitis and persistent nasal congestion. She also reports a 2-month history of dry cough, occasional hemoptysis, and generalized fatigue. She denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals bilateral nasal polyps and decreased breath sounds in the right upper lung field. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL (previously 0.8 mg/dL). A chest X-ray reveals multiple bilateral lung nodules, and urinalysis reveals red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Perform high-resolution CT of the chest

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists that has been persistent for over a year. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing tasks that require gripping or pinching. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals synovitis in several metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of both forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 1-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes the pain as persistent, with morning stiffness lasting for over an hour. She notes swelling in the joints and the presence of firm, subcutaneous nodules near her elbows. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism and hypertension. She denies any recent infections, trauma, or rashes. Family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as the wrists. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor test returns positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs) are also present. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most appropriate treatment option for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain in her hands and feet. She describes the pain as worse in the mornings, lasting over an hour before easing slightly. She also reports difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars and buttoning shirts. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling noted in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches that have persisted for the past two weeks. She reports that the pain began shortly after recovering from a viral illness characterized by fever, sore throat, and general malaise. The aches are accompanied by fatigue and morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She denies any significant joint swelling or redness. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals tender points over the neck, shoulders, and hips but no synovitis or deformities. Laboratory studies show a mild elevation in C-reactive protein and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman with a history of hypothyroidism presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She has difficulty performing daily tasks due to the pain and notes that rest does not alleviate her symptoms. She denies fever or weight loss. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/h, and normal thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and feels fatigued throughout the day. Upon examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are positive. Which of the following is most likely associated with her condition?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of fatigue, low-grade fevers, and painful subcutaneous nodules on her elbows and forearms. She reports experiencing joint stiffness in the morning that lasts for about an hour and has developed over the past few months. She denies any recent travel, insect bites, or new medications. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there are palpable, firm nodules over the extensor surfaces of the arms and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but normal complete blood count and liver function tests. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.D: Test for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.E: Perform a skin biopsy of the nodules
options.F: Arrange for an MRI of the affected joints
options.G: Refer to a rheumatologist for further evaluation

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She describes stiffness in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning, which lasts for about an hour before improving. She has also noticed swelling in her fingers and difficulty performing daily activities. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, she has symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness. She mentions that her symptoms are most severe in the morning and tend to improve as the day progresses. This has been ongoing for the past six months. She has difficulty using her hands in the morning due to stiffness and pain in her wrists and fingers. Her family history is significant for autoimmune conditions. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past few months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for more than an hour each day. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for the pain. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg, pulse is 80/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal anemia of chronic disease, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Additionally, she is positive for rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of symmetric pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and finds it difficult to grip objects. On examination, there are tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, and subcutaneous nodules near her elbows. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraines. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of her hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She reports feeling fatigued and has noticed swelling and tenderness in her hands and wrists. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any regular medications. On physical examination, a malar rash is evident, sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in her wrists and fingers for the past 3 years. The stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day. She reports difficulty performing daily activities due to the pain and decreased range of motion. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Several subcutaneous nodules are palpable on her elbows. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to recurrent oral ulcers that have been occurring for the past 3 months. She also reports painful genital ulcers that have appeared intermittently over this period. She mentions experiencing joint pain in her knees and ankles, but denies morning stiffness. The patient has had episodes of fever and fatigue. She has no significant past medical history and is not taking any medications. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcerations in the oral cavity and erythematous lesions in the genital region. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of worsening pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history includes hypertension and she takes lisinopril. She has no history of trauma to her hands. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, weight loss, and joint pain primarily affecting her knees and elbows. She also notes several firm, painless nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She has been experiencing dry eyes and mouth, but denies any fever or night sweats. Family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including lupus and rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is no joint swelling or erythema, but there are multiple subcutaneous nodules on her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but normal serum calcium and phosphate levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care clinic due to increasing pain and stiffness in her hands for the last 6 months. She reports that the pain is worst in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma or injury to the hands. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and seasonal allergies. She takes lisinopril and loratadine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL, White blood cell count: 7,200/mm^3, Platelets: 250,000/mm^3, Rheumatoid factor: positive, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of burning sensations and tingling in her feet, which have progressed to her hands in the last week. She reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has experienced mild joint pain, primarily in her wrists and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies alcohol use and has no significant family history. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Muscle strength is 5/5 in all extremities, but deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the ankles. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL and a mean corpuscular volume of 105 fL. The rest of her complete blood count is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 3 months. She also reports episodes of lightheadedness and palpitations. Her medical history is significant for occasional joint pain in her hands. Physical examination shows pallor and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 74 fL, and reticulocyte count of 1.2%. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. She has no history of gastrointestinal bleeding or recent dietary changes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to determine the cause of her anemia?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 weeks. She describes the joint pain as bilateral and primarily affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She notes her mother had similar symptoms in her 40s. On examination, her wrists and knees are swollen and tender bilaterally with decreased range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 85 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her wrists and small joints of her hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 2 hours. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for a history of smoking, which she quit 5 years ago. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Autoantibody testing shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She describes the joint pain as affecting her wrists, knees, and fingers, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Family history is significant for autoimmune diseases in her mother and sister. On examination, her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 122/80 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals a red, maculopapular rash across the malar region sparing the nasolabial folds, mild swelling of the wrists and knees, and tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is most likely to be found in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint swelling and stiffness that has been progressively worsening over the last three months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before subsiding. She also notes difficulty in performing daily activities such as opening jars or turning door knobs. She has no history of trauma and denies fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently on any medications. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 37-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, particularly affecting her wrists and the small joints of her hands. She reports the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and can last for over an hour, improving somewhat with activity. She denies any fever or recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On physical examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild swelling. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr), and a C-reactive protein (CRP) of 24 mg/L (reference range: 0–10 mg/L). X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her hands and wrists, which has been worsening over the past six months. She reports difficulty in the mornings, with stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed effectively with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On examination, there are noticeable deformities in the fingers with swelling, tenderness, and decreased range of motion in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, a positive rheumatoid factor, and high titers of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-ray imaging of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and morning stiffness in her joints. She reports that the stiffness lasts for over an hour each morning and affects her wrists, hands, and knees symmetrically. She also notes occasional low-grade fevers and has lost about 5 pounds unintentionally over the past few months. Her family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There is no skin rash, and her cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 85 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of difficulty walking and a noticeable foot drop. She reports experiencing some weakness in her right leg and occasionally stumbles when walking. Her medical history is notable for occasional back pain, which she attributes to her work as a warehouse manager. She denies any recent injury or trauma. On examination, she has weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the right leg and dorsum of the foot. Her gait shows a high-stepping pattern with the right foot. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and normal inflammatory markers. MRI of the lumbar spine shows no significant abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 6 months. She has also noted pale skin and frequent headaches. Her medical history is significant for osteoarthritis managed with ibuprofen as needed. She denies any gastrointestinal bleeding, changes in bowel habits, or recent infections. She reports a balanced diet with no known food allergies. On physical examination, she appears pale with mild tachycardia, but the exam is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), mean corpuscular volume of 105 fL (reference range: 80-100 fL), and a reticulocyte count of 0.5% (reference range: 0.5-2.5%). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's condition?

options.A: Testing for celiac disease with tissue transglutaminase antibodies
options.B: Investigating for rheumatoid arthritis with serum rheumatoid factor
options.C: Vitamin B12 and folate levels assessment
options.F: Evaluation for lead poisoning with blood lead levels
options.G: Colonoscopy to rule out gastrointestinal bleeding

question: A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain and stiffness. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She denies any trauma but mentions that the back pain began insidiously. Her medical history is significant for irritable bowel syndrome. She also reports occasional pain in her hips and shoulders. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. HLA-B27 is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with worsening joint pain and stiffness over the past 3 years, particularly in the mornings lasting over an hour. She reports difficulty using her hands for daily activities and has noticed deformities developing in both hands. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She takes lisinopril and atorvastatin daily. She denies alcohol use and smokes occasionally. On physical examination, there are swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of intermittent fevers and night sweats. She also reports fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lb) over the past two months. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, she has multiple palpable, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. She denies any joint pain or stiffness. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. An excisional biopsy of a cervical lymph node is performed. Which of the following is most likely to be found on further investigation?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, recurrent sinusitis, and a persistent cough producing blood-tinged sputum over the past 3 months. She reports occasional joint pain but attributes it to her long hours as a computer programmer. She also notes episodes of hematuria. Her medical history is significant for recurrent upper respiratory tract infections, and she has been on multiple courses of antibiotics with little improvement. Physical examination reveals mild nasal crusting and tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles at the bases. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count: 12,000/mm3, Platelet count: 300,000/mm3, Serum creatinine: 2.1 mg/dL, and Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. A chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.F: Refer to a pulmonologist for bronchoscopy

question: A 51-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness affecting her hands. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving. She also reports fatigue and difficulty performing daily tasks, such as opening jars. Her medical history is significant for hypertension. She denies any recent infections or travel. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. No skin rashes or nail changes are noted. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of worsening joint pain, fatigue, and easy bruising over the past 2 months. She reports a decreased appetite and unintentional weight loss of 6 kg (13 lb) in the same period. She has difficulty buttoning her clothes due to stiffness and pain in her wrists and fingers, particularly in the morning. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. She denies any recent infections, travel, or new medications. Physical examination reveals conjunctival pallor, ecchymoses on her arms, and synovitis of the wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 8.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,800/mm3, and platelet count of 85,000/mm3. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 75 mm/h, and a peripheral blood smear shows normocytic, normochromic anemia. Further evaluation indicates a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 31-year-old female returns from a 3-week trip to Southeast Asia, presenting with a sudden onset of joint pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She reports low-grade fevers and a generalized feeling of fatigue. There was no history of trauma or previous joint issues. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes loratadine as needed. She does not report any skin rashes or gastrointestinal symptoms. Vital signs reveal temperature 100.2°F (37.9°C), blood pressure 120/78 mmHg, pulse 96/min, and respiratory rate 20/min. Physical examination shows synovitis of the affected joints without erythema. Laboratory findings show elevated ESR and CRP with a negative rheumatoid factor. A mosquito-borne viral infection is suspected. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe pain in her left wrist after slipping on ice and falling on her outstretched hand. She describes the pain as sharp and worsening with movement. She denies any numbness or tingling in her fingers. Her past medical history is significant only for seasonal allergies. She takes loratadine as needed. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness over the distal radius with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no visible deformity, and her capillary refill is less than 2 seconds. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a one-week history of rash, joint pain, and oral ulcers. She notes that the rash is particularly worsened by sun exposure and describes it as appearing on her cheeks and nose. In addition, she has experienced fatigue and joint stiffness, particularly in the morning, which lasts for over an hour. She denies recent infections or travel. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has a malar rash and oral ulcers. There is tenderness and swelling of the small joints of her hands. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory results reveal anemia and positive antinuclear antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of a rash that worsens with sun exposure, joint pain, and oral ulcers that have persisted for several weeks. She reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. The rash is predominantly on her face, resembling a butterfly distribution across the cheeks and nose. She denies any significant hair loss, Raynaud's phenomenon, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, the facial rash is evident, and there is tenderness and mild swelling in her wrists and small joints of the hands. Laboratory tests reveal:

CBC:
Hb: 11.8 g/dL
WBC: 4,200/mm3
Platelets: 190,000/mm3

Serum:
Na+: 138 mEq/L
K+: 4.0 mEq/L
Cr: 0.9 mg/dL
ANA: Positive
Anti-dsDNA: Elevated

What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty grasping objects. Her past medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or rashes. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with several subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a positive rheumatoid factor. Her anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody is also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as symmetrical and more pronounced in the morning, lasting for several hours. She denies any recent trauma or overuse of her joints. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, which is well-managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. X-rays of the hands show no erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that improves with activity and worsens with rest. She also reports occasional hip pain and fatigue. Her symptoms are more pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour. She denies any significant trauma or injury to the back. She has a family history of arthritis. On physical examination, there is limited flexion of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory studies show:

Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 45 mm/hr
C-reactive protein: 14 mg/L

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of joint pain and stiffness over the past six months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about 1 hour before improving throughout the day. The pain primarily affects her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. She has a past medical history of mild asthma and is otherwise healthy. She denies any recent infections or trauma. She takes only albuterol as needed for asthma. On examination, her wrists and fingers are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory results show a positive anti-citrullinated protein antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman visits the clinic due to joint pain and swelling that began 3 months ago. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands due to swelling of the joints. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. A hand X-ray shows marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts over an hour. Her symptoms initially began in her fingers and wrists and have now progressed to her knees. She works as a typist and reports difficulty typing in the mornings due to the stiffness and pain. She denies any fevers, rash, or recent travel. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following findings is most likely to be associated with this condition?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to ongoing stiffness and swelling in her joints for the past 8 months. She reports that the symptoms are most prominent in the morning and gradually improve throughout the day. Her medical history includes hypertension and mild depression. On examination, there are ulnar deviations of the fingers and swelling of the bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows erosions at the joint margins. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in her legs. She describes her symptoms as starting with mild fatigue and occasional tingling in her feet, which has now progressed to noticeable weakness that makes climbing stairs challenging. She denies any significant joint pain but notes some stiffness in her fingers in the morning that resolves after an hour. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, her vital signs are normal. Neurological examination reveals decreased muscle strength in the lower extremities bilaterally, rated 4/5. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk, and there is a positive Babinski sign. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,400/mm3
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
ESR 25 mm/h
Serum
Electrolytes within normal limits
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Thyroid function tests normal
MRI of the lumbar spine is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red, nodular lesions on her shins that have developed over the past week. She also reports a low-grade fever and joint pain in her knees and ankles. Her medical history is significant for recurrent upper respiratory tract infections, and she denies any recent travel or new medications. She does not smoke or use alcohol. On examination, she has tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior aspects of her legs and mild effusion in both knees. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 88/min, blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/h
C-reactive protein 12 mg/L
A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending over to lift a small box. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her mid-back. Her past medical history is significant for osteoporosis and hypothyroidism. She denies any leg weakness or bowel or bladder incontinence. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the thoracic vertebrae and decreased range of motion due to pain. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 18/min. A lateral X-ray of the thoracic spine reveals a wedge-shaped deformity in the T8 vertebra. Which of the following diagnoses is most likely associated with this patient's presentation?

A 47-year-old female presents to the clinic with progressive swelling and pain in her hands and feet for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty using her hands for daily activities. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, trauma, or fever. Family history reveals her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints as well as ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated at 40 mm/hr. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent pain and stiffness in her wrists and hands, particularly in the morning, lasting over an hour. She also notes swelling and difficulty in using her hands for daily activities. Over the past few months, she has noticed firm nodules over her elbows. She has a history of hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and is allergic to penicillin. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including lupus in her mother. Physical examination reveals tenderness and synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of worsening bilateral hip pain. She reports that the pain is dull in nature and tends to worsen with activity, particularly after long walks or standing for extended periods. She denies any recent trauma or significant falls. She has also experienced occasional morning stiffness lasting about 20 minutes, which improves throughout the day. Her past medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril and intermittent migraines treated with sumatriptan. Her family history is significant for osteoarthritis in her father. On examination, there is limited range of motion in both hips, and mild tenderness is noted over the greater trochanteric regions. There is no noticeable swelling or erythema. Her laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

options.A: Hip x-ray to evaluate for osteoarthritis
options.C: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the hips

question: A 55-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of progressively worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for over an hour. She also notes the presence of firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination shows synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and subcutaneous nodules on her elbows and forearms. She notes these nodules have been present for several months and appear to be firm and non-tender. Additionally, she has experienced intermittent fevers and unintended weight loss over the past few months. Her medical history is significant for a thyroid disorder, which is currently treated with levothyroxine. On physical examination, the nodules are visible on the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and C-reactive protein (CRP). The rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and generalized joint pain for the past several months. She reports developing nodules under her skin on the back of her arms, which have been painful. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She has no known drug allergies. On examination, the patient is noted to have multiple firm, tender subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of both elbows. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,800/mm3
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
ESR 45 mm/hr
CRP elevated
Rheumatoid factor negative
Anti-citrullinated peptide antibodies negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain for the past week. She reports that the pain is migratory, initially starting in her knees, then moving to her elbows and wrists. She also had a sore throat two weeks ago that resolved without treatment. She feels fatigued but denies any rash, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. On examination, her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling of the elbows and wrists. There is no erythema or warmth over the joints. Heart sounds reveal a soft systolic murmur best heard at the apex. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
C-reactive protein elevated
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of difficulty walking and a noticeable dragging of her right foot. She works as a teacher and notes increasing fatigue by the end of the day. Her medical history is significant for occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, but she has not sought treatment for it. She denies any recent trauma or infections. On examination, she exhibits a high-stepping gait and weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot. Sensory and reflex examinations are normal. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following conditions might best explain her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue, low-grade fever, and painful nodules on her elbows and forearms for the past 2 weeks. She reports occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, which worsens in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies any recent infections or changes in medications. She has a history of hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there are multiple firm subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and elbows. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 95/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/h and a C-reactive protein level of 5.2 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past six months. She describes the stiffness as most pronounced in the morning, lasting at least 90 minutes, and is accompanied by swelling in the joints. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory findings show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibody. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 8 months. She describes morning stiffness that lasts over an hour, improving somewhat with movement. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are noted over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory results reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and weight loss over the past 3 months. She reports a noticeable swelling in her neck and under her arms along with joint pain, which is particularly severe in the mornings. She denies any recent infections or travel history. Her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, multiple cervical and axillary lymph nodes are palpable, non-tender, and firm. Several joints in her hands and wrists are swollen and tender with some warmth. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia and elevated C-reactive protein levels. An excisional biopsy of a cervical lymph node is ordered. Assuming this biopsy is performed, which of the following is the most expected finding?

options.G: High levels of Epstein-Barr virus DNA

question: A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a seated position. She has also experienced muscle aches and fatigue. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, which are well-controlled with medication. She denies any joint pain, swelling, or morning stiffness. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On physical examination, there is symmetrical weakness of the proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs. There are no skin rashes, and the joints appear normal. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and normal thyroid function tests. An electromyography shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes the pain as being most severe in the morning, affecting both hands and wrists symmetrically, and improving throughout the day. She notes swelling and stiffness in her fingers and has difficulty making a fist in the morning. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, she is noted to have tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman, currently 30 weeks pregnant, presents to her obstetrician with complaints of increasing lower back and pelvic pain over the past month. She describes the pain as a dull ache, exacerbated by prolonged standing and walking, and relieved somewhat by rest. She has no significant past medical history and this is her first pregnancy. She denies any fever, urinary symptoms, or recent trauma. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the sacroiliac joints, and her gait appears to be slightly waddling. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. ESR is mildly elevated. 

What is the most appropriate management step for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She describes the stiffness lasting for more than an hour every morning, improving as the day progresses. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. She is otherwise healthy, taking only an over-the-counter multivitamin daily. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with warmth and tenderness. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Further testing shows positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent bone pain over the past several months. She reports the pain is most pronounced in her hips and lower back, but she has not experienced any recent falls or injuries. The patient is postmenopausal, and her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She takes lisinopril and atorvastatin daily. She denies fever, weight changes, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On physical examination, she has tenderness to palpation over the spine and pelvic region. Laboratory studies show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, normal calcium and phosphate levels, and a C-reactive protein of 1.1 mg/L. A recent DEXA scan showed decreased bone density. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past 4 months. She describes the pain as a deep ache that is worse in the morning and after prolonged activity. She also reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has noticed occasional stiffness in her hands, especially in the mornings. She denies any trauma or recent infections. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the greater trochanteric region bilaterally, but no erythema or swelling. Her gait is slightly antalgic. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of the hips reveal narrowing of the joint space. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.G: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 37-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies alcohol or tobacco use. Her family history reveals that her mother had "arthritis problems." On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody tests are positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers. She reports that the ulcers tend to heal and then recur every few weeks. Additionally, she has experienced joint pain in her knees and ankles, which are more pronounced after periods of rest. She denies any significant weight loss or fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any sexual activity in the past year. On physical examination, multiple shallow ulcers with erythematous borders are noted in the oral cavity and genital region. There is mild swelling and tenderness in the bilateral knees. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 25 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and knees. She notes that the stiffness is most severe in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving. She has difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint swelling and pain. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR of 45 mm/hr and a positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands reveals marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of recurrent painful oral ulcers and new-onset genital ulcers over the past three months. She also reports episodic joint pain in her knees and wrists, which tend to swell and become stiff in the mornings. She denies any fever, weight loss, or rash. Past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On physical exam, the oral mucosa reveals multiple shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and tongue, and there are similar lesions observed on the labia majora. The joints are tender but not deformed. Laboratory tests from today show elevated inflammatory markers but a normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 8 months. She also notes bilateral swelling in front of her ears and occasional joint pain in her hands and wrists. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent weight loss or fever. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and positive ANA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her right great toe. She reports these episodes have occurred three times in the past six months, each lasting about a week and resolving spontaneously. She describes the pain as excruciating, preventing her from walking. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril and denies alcohol use. On examination, there is erythema and tenderness over the right first metatarsophalangeal joint with noticeable tophi on the ear helix. Her vital signs are stable. Serum uric acid levels are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance for the past 6 months. She reports feeling extremely tired even after a full night's sleep, and her coworkers have noticed she appears more sluggish at work. She denies any recent changes in exercise habits or diet. She has a history of irregular menstrual cycles but is currently not on any medications. On physical examination, her skin appears dry, and there is periorbital puffiness. Her pulse is 58/min, and her blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level with low free thyroxine (T4). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive pain and stiffness in multiple joints. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities due to hand deformities. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, her bilateral wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender. There are visible deformities at the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. She has positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic with a sudden onset of joint pain in her knees, wrists, and ankles. She recently returned from a vacation in the Caribbean two weeks ago. She reports associated fatigue and a low-grade fever. She denies any previous history of joint pain, recent infections, or trauma. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the affected joints, but no deformities. Her temperature is 38.1°C, pulse is 88/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Joint aspiration from the knee reveals clear fluid without crystals or bacteria on Gram stain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-year history of persistent fatigue and weight gain. She reports feeling cold all the time, even during warm weather. Her hair has become brittle and started falling out in the last few months. Additionally, she complains of joint stiffness, especially in the mornings, but denies any significant joint swelling or redness. She has a history of depression, for which she takes sertraline. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. Her vital signs show a temperature of 97.8°F (36.6°C), blood pressure of 122/80 mmHg, pulse of 60/min, and respirations of 14/min. Physical examination reveals dry, coarse skin and a delayed relaxation phase of deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory workup shows a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, TSH level of 8.2 mIU/L, and a free T4 level of 0.7 ng/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to ongoing joint pain and stiffness. She reports that her symptoms have persisted for the last six months and are most pronounced in the morning, lasting more than an hour before subsiding. She describes the pain as affecting her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints symmetrically. She denies any recent injuries or new medications. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies and a tonsillectomy in childhood. She takes no regular medications and denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, she appears well-nourished with a normal body mass index. Her joints exhibit mild swelling and tenderness, particularly over the wrists and hands, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 12.8 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 48 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has persisted for over two years. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and reports difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt. On physical examination, her wrists and several metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender, with visible subcutaneous nodules near the elbows. Her mother was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis several years ago. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is most pronounced in her wrists and knuckles and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, currently managed with levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, the affected joints are swollen and tender bilaterally, with a reduced range of motion. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her knees and ankles. She notes that the joint pain has been intermittent over the past two weeks and is associated with morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is significant for asthma, for which she uses an inhaler as needed. On physical examination, there are several tender, erythematous nodules on both lower legs. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest x-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, low-grade fevers, and nodules on her elbows and forearms. She reports joint stiffness in her hands that eases with movement throughout the day. On examination, multiple firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her elbows, along with mild swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and morning stiffness for the past 4 months. She reports that the stiffness lasts for over an hour each morning and has been progressively worsening. The patient also notes that her fingers and wrists have become increasingly swollen and tender. She has a 10-year history of hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine, and her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. She denies any recent trauma. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Testing reveals positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past year. She reports frequent use of artificial tears and mouthwash, which offer minimal relief. She also notes swelling around her jawline that seems to come and go, which she attributes to her parotid glands, and occasional joint pain in her fingers and wrists. She denies fever, weight loss, or changes in vision. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any regular medications. On examination, her vital signs are stable. The oral mucosa appears dry, and mild bilateral parotid gland enlargement is noted. Her eye examination shows reduced tear production. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting about 1 hour before improving throughout the day. She also notes occasional fatigue and low-grade fevers. She denies any significant past medical history or recent infections. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies are significant for a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving throughout the day. She works as a librarian and mentions difficulty with tasks such as typing or turning book pages. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or skin rash. Examination reveals swelling and tenderness at her bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with limited range of motion. Her laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibody levels. An X-ray of her hands reveals marginal erosions and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of right shoulder pain and stiffness. She reports the pain is worse with movement and has difficulty raising her arm above her head. She denies trauma, fever, or recent illness. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, there is decreased range of motion in all planes of the right shoulder, with tenderness upon palpation. Laboratory tests reveal normal ESR and CRP levels, and rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance over the past six months. She reports joint stiffness, particularly in the morning, but it improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is notable for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, she appears slightly overweight with dry skin, and her thyroid gland is palpable but not tender. Her vital signs are temperature 97.8°F (36.6°C), blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, pulse 58/min, and respirations 16/min. Laboratory tests show: TSH 8.5 μIU/mL (normal 0.4-4.0), free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal 0.8-1.8), and ESR 30 mm/hr (elevated). Which of the following is most likely contributing to her lab abnormalities and symptomatology?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and pain in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She has also noticed swelling and stiffness in her joints. Her medical history includes mild hypertension, which is well-controlled on lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.4 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 52 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), and C-reactive protein of 18 mg/L (reference range: 0-10 mg/L). What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 50-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of persistent fatigue, unintentional weight loss, and night sweats. She also reports swollen lymph nodes in her neck and armpits, which have been progressively enlarging. She does not drink alcohol or smoke. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, she appears pale and has multiple palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes that are non-tender. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests show anemia with a hemoglobin concentration of 9.5 g/dL, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 85 mm/h. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with fatigue, unintentional weight loss of 10 lbs over the past 3 months, and generalized weakness. She also describes episodes of dizziness and has noticed easy bruising. She denies any recent infections, fever, or changes in bowel habits. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she appears pale. Her vital signs are: temperature 98.2°F (36.8°C), pulse 88/min, respirations 14/min, and blood pressure 118/70 mmHg. Physical examination shows conjunctival pallor and mild splenomegaly.
