Laboratory tests reveal: hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 80 fL, leukocyte count 3,200/mm³, and platelet count 110,000/mm³. A peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia with reticulocytopenia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and pain in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting both hands equally, and notes swelling in her fingers. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, well-managed on levothyroxine. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain. She notes that the pain began in her knees and has since moved to her wrists and elbows. She experienced a sore throat approximately 3 weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Her mother mentions that she has been more fatigued than usual. She denies any fever, rash, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, heart rate is 72/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the affected joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and mild leukocytosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain that is particularly severe in the mornings, lasting over an hour before improvement. She reports pain and stiffness in her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for mild seasonal allergies. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the aforementioned joints, but no erythema. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care office with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. She recently recovered from a viral upper respiratory infection about one month ago. She describes the pain as generalized and affecting her muscles and joints, particularly in the morning. She also reports feeling unusually tired and having difficulty sleeping. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On physical examination, there is no synovitis or joint swelling, but there is generalized tenderness to palpation over multiple muscle groups. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, a negative rheumatoid factor, and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 52-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of symmetric swelling and pain in her wrists and small joints of the hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and describes the pain as impacting her ability to perform daily activities. Her family history is significant for autoimmune disease, as her sister has systemic lupus erythematosus. She denies recent infections, trauma, or new medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detected at high titers. X-rays of the hands show juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of fatigue and pain in her joints for the past several months. She mentions that the pain is worse in the morning and tends to improve throughout the day. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medication. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman visits the clinic due to a three-month history of stiffness and pain in her hands and wrists, which is most severe upon waking and improves throughout the day. She also reports occasional swelling and warmth in the affected joints. She denies fever, weight loss, skin rash, or photosensitivity. Her family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. She works as a graphic designer and denies any recent injuries or changes in physical activity.

On physical examination, there is tenderness and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. No skin rashes or oral ulcers are noted. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of severe lower back and pelvic pain that is making it difficult for her to walk and perform daily activities. She denies any prior history of back problems or trauma. She also reports mild morning stiffness in her hands and knees which resolves after an hour, but is not experiencing any swelling in her joints. Her pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated. On examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and the lower lumbar region, with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no peripheral joint swelling or redness. Her vital signs are normal, and laboratory tests show normal white blood cell count and elevated C-reactive protein. What is the most likely diagnosis or next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.A: Physical therapy for pelvic girdle pain
options.C: Magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine
options.E: Bed rest with pelvic support belt

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has worsened over the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. She notes difficulty in performing tasks requiring hand dexterity due to swelling and is concerned about subcutaneous nodules developing over her elbows. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Vital signs include a temperature of 98.8°F (37.1°C), blood pressure of 112/76 mm Hg, heart rate of 78/min, and respiratory rate of 14/min. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender, with symmetric synovitis. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory findings reveal anemia of chronic disease and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute right wrist pain after falling onto an outstretched hand while walking her dog. She reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist. She denies any previous trauma to the area or chronic joint issues. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, but she denies any history of arthritis or autoimmune conditions. On examination, her right wrist is swollen and tender with limited range of motion. There is no deformity noted, and her hand's neurovascular status is intact. An X-ray of the wrist is pending. Laboratory results show a mild elevation in ESR and normal CRP levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past year. She describes having difficulty with tasks such as opening jars and buttoning shirts due to decreased hand strength. Morning stiffness lasts for over an hour before improvement. She has no significant past medical history and takes over-the-counter ibuprofen with partial relief. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with noticeable ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers, positive rheumatoid factor (RF), and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate juxta-articular osteopenia and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of worsening lower back pain over the past year. The pain is dull, bilateral, and improves with physical activity but worsens with rest. She reports morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour and has been feeling more fatigued over the past few months. She denies any significant trauma, and over-the-counter pain relievers only provide minimal relief. She has no significant medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her family history is notable for an uncle with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination shows tenderness and decreased range of motion in the lower lumbar region. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of the spine show sacroiliac joint inflammation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and describes the pain as symmetrical, affecting her hands, wrists, and knees. She has noticed swelling in her fingers and difficulty bending them, especially in the mornings. She denies any recent trauma or injuries. Her past medical history is significant for occasional migraines. She works as an accountant and denies smoking or alcohol use. On examination, her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. There is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to a 2-month history of morning stiffness and swelling in her hands and wrists that improves throughout the day. She reports no significant previous medical history and takes only a multivitamin regularly. Her mother had lupus, and her grandmother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody levels are significantly elevated. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain that radiates to her right arm, accompanied by tingling and numbness in her right hand for the past four months. She reports difficulty gripping objects and occasional weakness in her arm. She denies any significant trauma but mentions that she works long hours in front of a computer. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch in the right C6 dermatome, and weakness is noted in the right biceps muscle. Spurling's test is positive, and reflexes are slightly diminished in the right biceps. An MRI of the cervical spine is ordered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient's condition?

options.D: Consult rheumatology for suspected rheumatoid arthritis

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. She has a past medical history of hypertension controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of intermittent joint pain and swelling in her hands and knees. She also reports a worsening rash that becomes more pronounced with sun exposure and was recently noted to have small, painless ulcers on her oral mucosa. Her family history is significant for an aunt with systemic lupus erythematosus. She denies any morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. Her vital signs are: temperature 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure 115/74 mmHg, pulse 76/min, and respirations 18/min. Physical examination reveals an erythematous malar rash and mild swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and a positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and unintended weight loss of 10 pounds. She also notes occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, but denies any morning stiffness. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has difficulty concentrating at work. On physical examination, her conjunctivae are pale, and she has mild tenderness in her wrists without swelling. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 72 fL, and a ferritin level of 8 ng/mL. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

A 54-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with gradually worsening joint pain and swelling in her hands and feet over the past 3 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her family history is significant for an older sister with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past six months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, with stiffness that is most pronounced in the mornings and after long periods of inactivity. The stiffness generally improves with movement throughout the day. She denies any recent trauma, but mentions feeling extremely fatigued over the past few months. Her medical history is significant for vitiligo and a family history of autoimmune diseases. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and reduced range of motion in the hips. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A pelvic x-ray is performed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.B: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the sacroiliac joints

question:
A 56-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressive worsening of leg weakness over the past 3 months. She describes difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a seated position, accompanied by mild fatigue. She denies any numbness or tingling. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. Family history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is 3/5 strength in the proximal muscles of both lower extremities, with no muscle atrophy. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetrical. There is no joint swelling or tenderness. Recent laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin (Hb): 13.5 g/dL
Creatine kinase (CK): 850 U/L
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 30 mm/hr

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given this patient's presentation?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute left knee pain and swelling, which began after a hiking trip two days ago. She recalls feeling a 'pop' in her knee while descending a steep trail, followed by immediate pain and difficulty bearing weight. She has a history of occasional joint pain in her hands and morning stiffness lasting about an hour, which she attributes to stress and overuse. Her vital signs are normal. On examination, the left knee is warm, swollen, and tender with an effusion. There is limited range of motion due to pain. Lachman test is positive, and there is mild joint line tenderness. Laboratory tests show mildly elevated inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of diffuse body aches and fatigue. She states that she had a viral illness with fever and sore throat about a month ago, which resolved spontaneously. The body aches started shortly after and have persisted since. She denies any joint swelling but mentions some morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She has also been feeling more tired than usual and has difficulty concentrating. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well, with normal vital signs. She has diffuse tenderness on palpation of the muscles in her upper arms and thighs but no joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.D: Refer to rheumatology for potential rheumatoid arthritis
options.F: Conduct a sleep study for apnea

question:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 3 weeks of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes the sensation as 'pins and needles', which sometimes wakes her up at night. She has a history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. She denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. On physical examination, there is diminished sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Her deep tendon reflexes are reduced in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show:
HbA1c 9.2%
Fasting glucose 172 mg/dL
Her current medications include metformin and lisinopril. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

options.C: Improved glycemic control through dietary changes

question: A 17-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of migratory joint pain affecting her knees, wrists, and ankles. She recalls having a sore throat about a month prior, which resolved without any specific treatment. She reports feeling fatigued and has noticed some swelling in her joints. She denies any recent fever or skin rashes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is tenderness and mild swelling of the wrists and knees, with no erythema. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rapid streptococcal antigen test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her knees and ankles. She denies any fever or weight loss. She mentions that she had a sore throat a few weeks ago but did not seek medical attention. On physical examination, tender, erythematous nodules are observed on her anterior shins. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's current presentation?

A 45-year-old woman is evaluated for persistent nasal congestion, occasional nosebleeds, and a cough producing blood-tinged sputum for the last 4 months. She mentions increased fatigue and joint pain, particularly in the knees and elbows, which she initially attributed to her busy lifestyle. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies recent travel or sick contacts. On examination, her blood pressure is 138/84 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, respirations are 20/min, and temperature is 37.2°C (99°F). There is tenderness over the sinuses and bilateral crackles at the lung bases. Laboratory studies show a creatinine level of 2.0 mg/dL (increased from a baseline of 0.9 mg/dL), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) of 36 mg/dL, and urinalysis revealing hematuria and red blood cell casts. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. An immunologic workup is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, primarily affecting her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has no history of trauma or recent infections. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An autoantibody panel shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show mild periarticular osteopenia but no erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving throughout the day. She denies any fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 12.5 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 8,500/mm3, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain affecting her wrists, knees, and fingers. She notes that her joint stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for about an hour. She denies any fevers, chills, or recent infections. She has a personal history of mild hypertension, controlled with losartan. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including her sister with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a normal white blood cell count. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions at the joints. Which of the following findings is most likely to be present in this patient?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of diffuse bone pain and fatigue. She reports that the pain is most intense in her hips and lower back, and it worsens with activity. She has a history of hypertension, well-controlled with lisinopril, and takes calcium supplements daily. She experiences mild stiffness in the morning lasting about 20 minutes and denies any joint swelling or redness. She is postmenopausal and has been for 12 years. On examination, she appears tired but in no acute distress. There is tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine and the iliac crests. Laboratory studies show a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL, serum phosphate of 2.4 mg/dL, and parathyroid hormone (PTH) level that is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman is evaluated in the hospital for persistent fever over the past 3 weeks. She reports generalized malaise, arthralgias, and weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism. She takes lisinopril and levothyroxine. On examination, her temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 128/74 mm Hg. There is tenderness in the small joints of her hands without significant swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 85 mm/hr and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 15 mg/L. Blood cultures are negative. A chest x-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her evaluation?

options.F: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain, periorbital edema, and stiffness in her joints, primarily in the morning, for the past 3 months. She also describes feeling unusually tired and has noticed that her face appears rounder. Her menstrual cycles have become irregular. She denies any changes in diet or exercise habits. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 145/95 mmHg, moon facies, and central adiposity. There is mild swelling over the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Her laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but thyroid function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 2-year history of progressively worsening pain in her hands and wrists. The pain is typically worse in the mornings and tends to improve as the day goes on. She has also noticed swelling and stiffness in her fingers. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she is taking lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma to her hands. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg, and heart rate is 75/min. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm³
Platelet count: 250,000/mm³

Serum:
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-CCP antibodies: Positive
ESR: 55 mm/h

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, weight loss, and tender lumps on her forearms. She reports that the nodules have gradually increased in size and are painful to touch. She also notes occasional joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings. She denies any recent infections or travel. On physical examination, several firm, nontender subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show:
Hematocrit 35%
Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3
C-reactive protein 2.4 mg/dL
ESR 50 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman at 28 weeks of gestation comes to the clinic because of worsening lower back and pelvic pain for the past 2 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that intensifies with prolonged standing or walking and is relieved by resting. She denies any trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her pregnancy has been uneventful, and she takes prenatal vitamins regularly. On examination, she has an antalgic gait. There is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, and her lumbar range of motion is mildly restricted. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 35 mm/h. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.A: Initiate physical therapy with pelvic stabilization exercises
options.B: Prescribe acetaminophen as needed for pain relief
options.C: Order an MRI of the lumbar spine
options.F: Initiate a course of oral corticosteroids

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with gradually worsening weakness in her shoulders and hips over the last 6 months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. She also experiences occasional joint pain and morning stiffness in her hands. She denies any rash or skin changes. Her medical history includes hypertension, which is controlled with lisinopril. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, she has 4/5 strength in her proximal muscles, with normal strength distally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatinine kinase level and mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 6 kg (13 lbs). She reports feeling feverish, but her temperature records have been inconsistent. Additionally, she has noticed swelling in her neck and underarm areas. She denies any recent infections or travel history. On examination, multiple firm, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Her joint examination shows mild tenderness in her wrists without swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with a normal complete blood count. 

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of morning stiffness and swelling in her fingers and wrists for the past 2 months. The stiffness lasts for about 2 hours each morning before improving. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Her mother had a history of 'arthritis' and her sister has hypothyroidism. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Tests for anti-nuclear antibodies are negative, but there is a high titer of rheumatoid factor and antibodies to cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She describes the stiffness as most severe in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving slightly with movement. She reports difficulty gripping objects and occasional swelling in her knees. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism and hypertension, managed with levothyroxine and lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. There are no subcutaneous nodules. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-month history of symmetric swelling and morning stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness lasts about 2 hours each morning before improving. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detected. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of persistent bilateral hand and wrist pain for over 6 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour and difficulty grasping objects. She has noticed several nodules under the skin on her elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or falls. She occasionally takes ibuprofen, which provides minimal relief. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Firm, mobile subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and intermittent fatigue for the past two weeks. She mentions recovering from a viral illness a month ago, characterized by fever and sore throat. She now experiences muscle soreness and joint stiffness, especially in the mornings, which improves as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma or previous episodes of similar symptoms. Her medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. On examination, she appears well-nourished and in no acute distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Musculoskeletal examination reveals tenderness over the proximal muscle groups but no joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of progressive fatigue and joint pain over the past 3 months. She reports swelling and stiffness in her fingers and knees, especially in the morning, but also feels tired throughout the day. She has no notable past medical history and takes no regular medications. On physical examination, she appears pale. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 3,200/mm3, Platelet count 110,000/mm3, ESR 42 mm/hr, Serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL. A peripheral blood smear shows normocytic, normochromic anemia with no abnormal cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of skin tightening, particularly around her fingers, and color changes in her fingers upon exposure to cold. She describes episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red. These episodes are often painful. She also reports fatigue and some joint stiffness, which has been worse in the morning. Her medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease. Physical examination reveals shiny, taut skin on the fingers and telangiectasias on the face. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past several months. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting both her wrists and fingers. The pain is worse in the morning but improves with movement throughout the day. She also notes that she feels generally unwell and has unintentionally lost 5 pounds over the last 3 months. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin of 10.8 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein of 2.3 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent joint pain and swelling. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with her daily activities. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal, proximal interphalangeal, and wrist joints. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/hr and C-reactive protein of 20 mg/L. Her rheumatoid factor is positive at 80 IU/mL, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been persistent for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning and affecting both her hands and knees symmetrically. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is warmth and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of generalized bone pain and fatigue. She reports that the pain is particularly severe in her lower back and hips. She also notes occasional episodes of nausea and constipation. Her medical history includes hypertension and she has been postmenopausal for 15 years. Her medications include lisinopril. On physical examination, she appears fatigued but in no acute distress. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Initial laboratory studies reveal serum calcium of 11.8 mg/dL and a phosphorus level of 2.4 mg/dL. Alkaline phosphatase is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to increasing difficulty using her hands over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has noticed that her fingers are becoming more deformed. She has a 20-year history of rheumatoid arthritis and is currently on methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine. On examination, the patient has bilateral swan neck and boutonnière deformities in her fingers and visible nodules over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is also elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate management step for this patient?

options.H: Begin calcium and vitamin D supplementation

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain. She reports the nodules appeared about a week ago and have been progressively more painful. She mentions worsening fatigue and low-grade fevers over the past month. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Her medical history is significant for mild asthma, managed with occasional use of albuterol. She works as a school teacher and does not smoke or use illicit drugs. On physical examination, there are multiple erythematous, tender nodules on both shins. Her joints show mild swelling, but no significant deformities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely clinical diagnosis for this patient?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She denies any significant trauma or injury. She has also noted occasional swelling in her knees and ankles, along with fatigue. Over-the-counter NSAIDs provide some relief. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her family history is significant for psoriasis in her father. On examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited lumbar spine flexion. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of widespread muscle pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a constant ache that worsens with physical activity and is associated with morning stiffness lasting for several hours. Despite sleeping for more than 8 hours at night, she wakes up feeling unrefreshed and fatigued. She denies any swelling of the joints. Her medical history is significant for irritable bowel syndrome and migraine headaches. She takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. Physical examination reveals tenderness at multiple soft tissue points but no synovitis or joint deformities. Laboratory tests show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. A complete blood count and metabolic panel are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving with activity. She has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies demonstrate a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing in the affected joints.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 2-month history of a facial rash and joint pain. The rash, which worsens with sun exposure, is characterized by erythematous plaques on her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. She reports joint pain in her wrists and knees, which is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. Additionally, she has experienced fatigue and hair loss over the past few weeks. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. On examination, there is a notable malar rash, and her wrists are mildly swollen and tender. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 10-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve somewhat throughout the day. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands and the left wrist. There is also mild ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated levels of rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of persistent pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning, often lasting more than an hour. She also mentions noticing firm lumps under her skin near her elbows. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. She works as a librarian and denies any recent travel or trauma. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of hand pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and improves throughout the day. She reports that both her wrists and the small joints of her hands are affected. On examination, there is synovial thickening of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with tenderness and warmth but no erythema. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports pain and swelling in her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. She has been experiencing fatigue and unintended weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies smoking and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the aforementioned joints, with limited grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of increasing difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head over the past few months. She reports occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting approximately 30 minutes. She denies any recent viral illnesses or changes in medication. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness, particularly in the shoulders and hips, with no significant joint swelling or tenderness. Laboratory evaluation shows elevated creatine kinase levels and mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, headaches, and new-onset hypertension. She also reports a noticeable decrease in urine output over the past 2 weeks and episodes of joint discomfort primarily in her knees and wrists. Her medical history includes occasional migraines but is otherwise unremarkable. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/98 mmHg, heart rate is 82/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine at 2.2 mg/dL, BUN of 45 mg/dL, and a urinalysis shows 2+ proteinuria with granular casts. Antinuclear antibody testing returns positive at 1:160. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes the pain as aching and reports it is worse in the mornings, improving somewhat with activity throughout the day. The pain predominantly affects her hands and wrists bilaterally. She also notes swelling and stiffness in these joints. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or travel. She smokes one pack of cigarettes per day. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and positive rheumatoid factor. A hand X-ray displays joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her physician with fatigue, weight gain of approximately 8 kg over the past four months, and swelling around her eyes. She also reports joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings, and occasional difficulty with buttoning her clothes. She denies any fever, skin rash, or recent travel. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, and she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, she has bilateral periorbital edema and mild pitting edema in her lower extremities. Her thyroid is not palpable. Laboratory results show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Urinalysis reveals proteinuria of 3+ with no hematuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent sinus congestion and a cough producing yellow sputum. She reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands, but she dismisses it as minor. Her medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections over the past year. She denies fevers or weight loss. Vital signs are normal, and physical examination reveals nasal crusting and mild tenderness over the sinuses. Laboratory tests show an elevated serum creatinine level of 1.8 mg/dL (normal 0.6-1.2 mg/dL) and proteinuria on urinalysis. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling that began two weeks ago after recovering from a sore throat. The pain initially started in her knees and ankles but has since spread to her elbows and wrists. She describes the pain as migrating and on some days, the pain appears in new joints while the previous ones improve. She denies any morning stiffness. Her past medical history is significant for frequent episodes of pharyngitis during childhood. On physical examination, her vital signs are stable, and no fever is noted. There is mild swelling and tenderness in the elbows and wrists, but no joint deformities are observed. Heart sounds are normal with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of painful, blotchy, purplish skin lesions that have progressively worsened. She reports episodes of swelling and pain in her wrists and knees, as well as recurrent nosebleeds. Over the past month, she has experienced two episodes of deep vein thrombosis despite being on oral contraceptive pills. Her past medical history is significant for migraines. Her family history reveals that her mother had lupus. On examination, she has reticular, lace-like purplish discoloration on her thighs and arms. Laboratory results indicate:

Hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL
Platelet count 150,000/mm3
PT 13 seconds (normal range: 11-13.5 seconds)
INR 1.1
aPTT 38 seconds (normal range: 30-40 seconds)

Further testing reveals the presence of lupus anticoagulant and anticardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is the next best step in this patient's management?

options.G: Screen for SLE with an ANA panel

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about two hours before improving throughout the day. She also reports fatigue and occasional fever. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. The physician also observes subcutaneous nodules on her elbows. Laboratory studies reveal a high rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to her physician’s office complaining of chronic lower back pain for the past year. She describes the pain as dull and persistent, often worse in the mornings and improving with activity as the day progresses. She occasionally experiences stiffness in her lower back and hips, especially after prolonged inactivity. Her medical history is significant for psoriasis and mild hypertension. She denies any numbness or tingling in her legs. Her medications include amlodipine and ibuprofen as needed. She has a family history of rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On physical examination, there is reduced lumbar spine mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of fatigue and joint pain in her hands and wrists for the past 2 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and that the pain improves slightly throughout the day with activity. She denies any previous joint issues or family history of arthritis but notes she has been feeling unusually tired. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and occasional muscle cramps over the past 8 months. She notes that she feels cold more often than usual and has experienced increased hair loss. On examination, she has a slightly puffy face and skin that appears dry. Her temperature is 97.2°F (36.2°C), blood pressure is 122/80 mmHg, pulse is 60/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show the following:

TSH: 15 mU/L (normal 0.4-4.0 mU/L)
Free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (normal 0.8-1.8 ng/dL)
Hemoglobin: 12 g/dL
Hematocrit: 36%

Given her presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and recent weight gain. She mentions having cold intolerance and difficulty concentrating over the past few months. Her medical history is significant for mild depression treated with sertraline. On examination, the patient appears fatigued and has dry skin. Her thyroid gland is firm to palpation without any discrete nodules. The rest of her physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal a TSH level of 12 mU/L (elevated), free T4 of 0.8 ng/dL (low), and ESR of 35 mm/hr (elevated). The patient asks about her symptoms and possible conditions. Which of the following is the most likely condition accounting for the majority of her current symptoms?

A 37-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of persistent fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as affecting her wrists, knees, and fingers symmetrically. She also experiences morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has no significant medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for the pain, which provides minimal relief. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, her joints are swollen, tender, and warm, but no nodules are observed. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.3 g/dL, leukocytes at 7,500/mm3, platelets at 200,000/mm3, an ESR of 78 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic complaining of fatigue and joint pain in both hands and knees for the past 8 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and describes the pain as worsening after periods of rest. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling, warmth, and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL (normal 12-16 g/dL) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (normal <20 mm/hr). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain that has been shifting from her knees to her elbows and wrists over the past two weeks. She reports feeling fatigued and had a sore throat about a month ago that resolved without treatment. She denies any recent trauma or fevers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 120/76 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the left wrist and right knee. There are no cutaneous rashes. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
C-reactive protein 18 mg/L
Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 4 months. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting her hands, wrists, and knees, and notes that it is particularly severe in the morning, improving throughout the day. She has had difficulty performing daily activities, such as dressing and cooking, due to stiffness and swelling in her joints. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show normocytic anemia, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. She describes the pain as primarily affecting her hands, wrists, and knees, and notes that it is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She reports feeling unusually tired and has been struggling to keep up with her daily activities. She has no significant past medical history and takes only acetaminophen occasionally for pain relief. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 87 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 42 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain, fatigue, and a rash on her arms and legs. She recalls being bitten by a tick about a month ago during a hiking trip. Since then, she has experienced intermittent fevers and headaches. On physical examination, she has mild swelling and tenderness in her knees and wrists, as well as an erythematous, maculopapular rash on her extremities. Her vital signs show a temperature of 38°C (100.4°F), blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse of 78/min, and respirations of 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal a mild leukocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers. An ELISA test for Borrelia burgdorferi is ordered. Considering her presentation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling in her hands and feet that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She notes morning stiffness lasting about 2 hours and difficulty grasping objects. She has a history of hypertension and has been on lisinopril for the past 3 years. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-week history of joint pain and swelling. She has just returned from a trip to Southeast Asia, where she spent time in rural areas. She reports pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles, with morning stiffness that lasts for about 30 minutes. She also notes occasional fevers and fatigue. She denies any skin rashes, eye pain, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. On physical examination, she has synovitis of the wrists and mild swelling of the knees. Her vital signs include a temperature of 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse of 88/min, respirations of 16/min, and blood pressure of 118/74 mm Hg. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and an elevated ESR of 45 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 49-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of gradually worsening pain and stiffness in both hips, especially in the morning and after sitting for long periods. She reports no significant swelling or redness in the joints. She has a history of obesity and mild hypertension. She denies fever, rashes, or any recent infections. Her medications include lisinopril. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in both hips, but no warmth or erythema. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count, normal C-reactive protein, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 28 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr). X-ray of the pelvis shows narrowing of joint spaces in both hip joints. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain that seems to move from one joint to another. Initially, she had pain in her right ankle, which improved after a few days, but then her left knee became swollen and painful. She also mentions a sore throat that resolved without antibiotics around a month ago. She denies fever, rash, or any recent travel. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, her left knee is swollen and tender, but there is no redness or increased warmth. A throat culture performed today is negative. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 14/min. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports joint pain and swelling in her fingers and knees, along with mouth ulcers that have recently developed. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Her oral mucosa shows multiple shallow ulcers, and there is tenderness and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal anemia, leukopenia, and proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past week. She describes her pain as migratory, initially affecting her wrists and then her knees. She mentions that she had a sore throat two weeks ago but did not seek medical attention, as it resolved on its own. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 118/72 mm Hg. There is tenderness and mild swelling in her knees and wrists. No skin rashes are observed. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a positive rapid antigen detection test for group A streptococcus. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive muscle weakness. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects over her head. She denies any joint pain, morning stiffness, or significant fatigue. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, she has 4/5 strength in the proximal muscles of the arms and legs, but distal strength is preserved. Her reflexes are normal, and there is no muscle tenderness. Laboratory results reveal elevated creatine kinase and mildly elevated inflammatory markers. Electromyography shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes stiffness in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning, lasting for about an hour. She has noticed swelling and tenderness in her fingers and wrists. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. On examination, the patient has symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7,000/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/h, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of fatigue, generalized malaise, and joint pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists and small joints of her hands bilaterally. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Family history is notable for her mother having an unspecified autoimmune disorder. On examination, she has warmth and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Hand X-rays show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as bilateral, affecting her wrists, hands, and knees, and states that it is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for approximately 1 hour before improving as she moves around. She also reports feeling fatigued and having difficulty performing daily tasks. She denies any recent injuries, fever, or rashes. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with limited range of motion. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain, stiffness, and deformity, particularly affecting her hands and wrists. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve as the day progresses. She has also experienced fatigue and low-grade fevers. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory findings reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse bone pain and fatigue, which have progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and occasional headaches but denies any fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with amlodipine and atorvastatin respectively. She does not consume alcohol and has never smoked. Recently, she noticed her ring size has increased. On physical examination, there is mild bowing of the lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, normal calcium, and normal phosphorus levels. A plain X-ray of the pelvis and lumbar spine demonstrates cortical thickening of the femur and pelvis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 50-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the pain is most severe in the morning and tends to improve as the day progresses. She has noticed swelling in her wrists and fingers, which has been symmetrical. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her past medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 3-month history of pain and swelling in her fingers and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She denies any recent infections or injuries. Her medical history is significant for mild asthma controlled with inhalers. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of joint pain in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as persistent and worse in the morning, lasting more than an hour before she can move easily. She also reports fatigue and has noticed small, firm lumps under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history is unremarkable except for a family history of rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On physical examination, her hands show bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are noted on examination of the elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of pain in her wrists and fingers. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Over the last few months, she has noticed swelling in both wrists and some small lumps under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any rash, fever, or weight loss. Vital signs are stable. Physical examination reveals bilateral wrist synovitis and subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-CCP antibody is also positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of chronic neck pain and intermittent numbness in her right hand and forearm, which she has been experiencing for the past 6 months. She mentions that her symptoms worsen after prolonged typing at her desk job and improve slightly with rest. She denies any trauma or previous neck injuries. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, there is tenderness over the cervical paraspinal muscles, and Spurling's test is positive. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch over the right C6 dermatome and mild weakness in wrist extension on the same side. An X-ray of the cervical spine shows mild degenerative changes. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Start physical therapy for cervical spine stabilization
options.B: Obtain an MRI of the cervical spine
options.D: Refer to a neurosurgeon for possible decompression
options.E: Prescribe a short course of oral corticosteroids
options.F: Recommend ergonomic modifications at her workplace
options.G: Order electromyography and nerve conduction studies

question: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to joint pain. The mother reports that the pain started a few weeks ago following a sore throat, and now her daughter is experiencing pain in different joints, including the knees, wrists, and ankles. The patient describes the pain as migratory and is associated with mild swelling and redness. She feels fatigued but denies fever or weight changes. Her medical history is unremarkable. She is not on any medications and has no known allergies. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination is normal. Examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling of the affected joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer, ESR of 40 mm/hr, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purpura on her lower extremities and hematuria. She describes the purpura as small, painless, non-blanching spots primarily on her legs. She recently experienced a sore throat, but it resolved without treatment. She denies any joint pain or swelling. There is no history of abdominal pain, fever, or recent travel. Physical examination reveals scattered petechiae on the lower legs and mild swelling of the ankles. Urinalysis shows red blood cell casts and moderate proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive joint deformity predominantly affecting her hands and wrists over the last 3 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with activity. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender, with ulnar deviation of her fingers. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is 55 mm/hr. Radiographs of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic neck pain and intermittent numbness and tingling in her right arm that has been worsening over the past six months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that sometimes radiates down her arms, accompanied by a feeling of weakness in her grip. She has a long-standing history of migraines and anxiety, for which she takes sumatriptan and sertraline. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is limited range of motion in her cervical spine, and Spurling's maneuver reproduces her symptoms. Strength in the upper extremities is 4/5, and there is a mild decrease in sensation along the C7 dermatome on the right. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric in the upper extremities. MRI of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes with disc osteophyte complex at C5-C6, causing mild foraminal stenosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. She also reports occasional swelling of her parotid glands and mild joint pain, particularly in her hands and knees. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with medication. She denies any recent travel, fever, or weight loss. On physical examination, there is notable bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands and decreased salivary pooling under the tongue. Schirmer's test shows decreased tear production. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits your office with complaints of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past two years. She notes the pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She has difficulty making a fist in the morning due to stiffness but finds relief with over-the-counter NSAIDs. She denies any trauma or recent illness. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with notable subcutaneous nodules on the olecranon process of both elbows. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are markedly elevated. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty walking and tripping over her left foot. She reports that it feels as if her foot is dragging, particularly when she walks on uneven surfaces. She denies any numbness or tingling in her feet but admits to some generalized weakness and intermittent joint pain in her hands. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, there is a noticeable foot drop on the left side and weakness of the ankle dorsiflexors.
