Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An ultrasound of the nodules shows solid, hypoechoic structures without fluid collections. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressively worsening hand deformities over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day with activity. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She denies tobacco or alcohol use. On physical examination, there is notable ulnar deviation of the fingers and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Her hemoglobin is 12 g/dL, leukocyte count is 8,000/mm^3 with normal differential, and ESR is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and palpable subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She reports that the nodules are not painful but have gradually increased in size. The joint pain is primarily in her wrists and ankles, and she notes morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. The patient reports occasional low-grade fevers and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the past month. She denies any skin rashes, hair loss, or photosensitivity. On examination, there are firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and mild swelling of the wrist joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, a rash that appears on her face after sun exposure, and oral ulcers for the past two weeks. She describes the joint pain as aching, involving both wrists and small joints of her hands, and it frequently wakes her in the early morning. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, she has a well-demarcated erythematous rash over her cheeks and nasal bridge, sparing the nasolabial folds. Her temperature is 99.0°F (37.2°C), blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show elevated anti-nuclear antibodies and low complement levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, primarily affecting her wrists, fingers, and knees. She notes fatigue and some weight loss recently. She denies recent infections or trauma. Past medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism. Her medications include lisinopril and levothyroxine. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 122/80 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory studies reveal a positive anti-citrullinated protein antibody and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 32-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking. She notes weakness in her lower extremities and has difficulty climbing stairs. She describes a sensation of heaviness in her legs, and occasional numbness in her feet. The patient also reports joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes, as well as intermittent fatigue. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, her strength is 4/5 in both lower extremities, with decreased deep tendon reflexes at the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests reveal normal creatine kinase levels and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of fatigue and diffuse body aches for the past two weeks following a viral upper respiratory infection. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has joint stiffness in her hands and knees, which is worse in the morning and gradually improves as the day goes on. She denies any fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination shows mild tenderness over the wrists and knees but no swelling or deformities. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 25 mm/hr (normal < 20 mm/hr) and a C-reactive protein (CRP) of 1.2 mg/L (normal < 0.3 mg/L). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Prescribe a short course of NSAIDs

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves as the day progresses. She has difficulty in buttoning her clothes and performing household chores. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. No skin rashes are noted. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and a high erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of chronic neck pain and numbness in her right hand for the past 6 months. She reports that the numbness is mostly in her thumb and index finger and worsens after prolonged use of her computer. She has a history of osteoarthritis and hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. On physical examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch over the right thumb and index finger, and weakness of the right thumb abduction. Tinel's test at the wrist is positive. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers and no elevation in rheumatoid factor. Cervical spine X-rays show spondylotic changes. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in her right arm and hand. She reports that these symptoms have been present for over six months. The patient describes the tingling as intermittent but worsens with neck movements. She also reports occasional headaches and fatigue. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and a family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, her vital signs are stable, and there is tenderness to palpation over the cervical spine. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome and reduced grip strength on the right side. An MRI of the cervical spine indicates mild disc herniation at the C5-C6 level with minimal spinal cord compression. Which of the following conditions could also be considered in the differential diagnosis given her symptoms?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is symmetric, affecting her wrists and knees, and is particularly severe in the morning, often lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory investigations show a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 85 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. A hand X-ray reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain accompanied by stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. She has noticed swelling in her wrists and fingers, and has had difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her shirt. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as several firm, nontender subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 6,200/mm3
Platelet count 280,000/mm3
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-citrullinated peptide antibody positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports painful, swollen joints in her fingers and knees, and recurrent mouth ulcers. She denies fever, weight loss, or any recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She works as a graphic designer and spends most of her days indoors. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over the bridge of her nose and cheeks, sparing the nasolabial folds. There is mild swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocytes 5,000/mm3
Platelets 200,000/mm3
ESR 50 mm/hr
ANA Positive
Anti-dsDNA Positive
Anti-Sm Positive
Rheumatoid factor Negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in her hands, wrists, and knees for the past three months. She notes that the pain is worse in the morning and is associated with stiffness lasting over an hour before she feels functional. She works as a graphic designer and finds it difficult to type in the morning due to the joint discomfort. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in multiple proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory findings show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of painful swelling in her left great toe. These episodes have occurred three times in the past six months, each lasting about a week, and are associated with redness and warmth over the joint. She reports a history of kidney stones and hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Her current medications include lisinopril and occasional ibuprofen for pain. She drinks wine socially on weekends. On examination, her left great toe is erythematous and tender, with a palpable subcutaneous nodule. Synovial fluid analysis from a previous episode revealed needle-shaped crystals with negative birefringence. Her laboratory results show a serum uric acid level of 9.6 mg/dL (reference range: 3.5-7.2 mg/dL). What is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.C: Order a serum rheumatoid factor test
options.D: Refer to rheumatology for chronic management
options.F: Assess renal function with a comprehensive metabolic panel

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the last 6 months. She describes the stiffness as worst in the morning, lasting over an hour before she feels any relief. Her past medical history is significant for mild asthma. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with no skin changes or nail abnormalities. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which laboratory test result would most likely support the diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive deformity of her fingers over the past year. She reports pain and stiffness in her hands that is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She has a history of smoking and denies any recent trauma. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender. There is ulnar deviation of the fingers, and nodules are noted over the extensor surfaces. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with swelling and pain in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving slightly throughout the day. She reports associated fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive, and rheumatoid factor levels are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent fatigue and joint pain. She reports that, for the past 6 months, she has experienced morning stiffness lasting over an hour, along with swelling in her hands and feet. She has a past medical history of hypertension, which is managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, rashes, or significant weight changes. On examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.4 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/hr, and a rheumatoid factor of 120 IU/mL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits the clinic due to progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers and toes over the past year. She reports that her fingers turn white and then blue when exposed to cold, followed by a red flush as they rewarm, accompanied by pain and numbness. She also describes mild joint stiffness and fatigue. She has no significant medical history and takes no medications. Family history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals shiny, tight skin over the fingers with reduced ability to make a full fist. Nailfold capillary examination shows dilated capillaries. Initial laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to fatigue and diffuse muscle aches that have persisted for the past 3 weeks. She reports that the symptoms began shortly after recovering from a viral illness, which presented with fever, sore throat, and a rash. Her current symptoms include generalized body aches, mild joint stiffness in the morning, and non-restorative sleep. She denies any rashes or oral ulcers currently. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness in multiple muscle groups without significant joint swelling. Laboratory studies show a mildly elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and new onset genital ulcers, which she describes as deeply painful. She reports intermittent episodes of joint pain in her knees and ankles over the last few months. She denies any significant weight loss, fatigue, or fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination shows multiple shallow ulcers in the oral mucosa and erythematous nodules on her shins. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her knees and ankles for the past two weeks. She reports feeling fatigued and experiencing occasional low-grade fevers. She denies any recent infections, travel, or medication changes. She works as a school teacher and has been under stress recently due to an upcoming job review. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional episodes of irritable bowel syndrome managed with dietary changes. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows multiple tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior aspects of both lower legs. Both knees and ankles have mild swelling with tenderness to palpation, but full range of motion is preserved. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels, with normal complete blood count and renal function tests. A chest X-ray is ordered and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive hand deformities over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than one hour and swelling in her fingers and wrists. She has difficulty opening jars and experiences fatigue. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She does not smoke or use alcohol. Physical examination reveals swollen, tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman visits her physician with complaints of worsening deformities in her fingers and persistent joint pain. She mentions that the stiffness is especially severe in the morning and lasts for over an hour. She has had these symptoms for several years, but they have become increasingly debilitating in the past 6 months. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination shows swelling and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic for chronic pain and swelling in her hands and wrists over the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day. She has experienced some fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Physical examination reveals swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated C-reactive protein level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of swelling and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 weeks. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for several hours. She has also noticed fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. There is no skin rash or nail changes. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are present. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman visits the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her legs and intermittent blood in her urine. She describes the rash as non-itchy purplish spots on her lower extremities and has been experiencing joint pain and mild fever. She denies any recent travel, new medications, or changes in diet, and reports feeling fatigued. Her vital signs are within normal ranges. On physical examination, palpable purpura is noted on both legs, and her joints appear slightly swollen but without significant tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of recurrent fevers and painful cervical lymphadenopathy over the past 4 months. She also reports experiencing night sweats and fatigue. She describes the fevers as occurring every few weeks and often accompanied by a sore throat. She denies weight loss or any recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hyperthyroidism, for which she is taking methimazole. Physical examination reveals tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Laboratory studies show anemia, leukocytosis, and elevated C-reactive protein. An antinuclear antibody test is positive at a low titer, and rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Biopsy of an enlarged lymph node
options.E: Evaluation for sarcoidosis with a chest X-ray
options.G: Consultation with an infectious disease specialist

question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 4-month history of scaly skin lesions and joint pain. The skin changes initially started on her elbows and knees, and she describes them as itchy and occasionally bleeding when scratched. Over the past month, she has noticed swelling and pain in her fingers and toes, which is more pronounced on one side. She also experiences occasional fatigue but denies any morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales and dactylitis in a few digits. Which of the following is most likely the diagnosis for this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent lower back pain that has been occurring for the past 4 months. She describes the pain as worse in the early morning and improving with physical activity as the day progresses. She also mentions occasional pain in her buttocks and hips. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional episodes of iritis. She is a non-smoker and denies any alcohol or drug use. Family history reveals that her father has psoriasis. Physical examination shows reduced flexion of the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and negative rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent swelling in her neck and armpits for the past 3 months. She has also experienced unintentional weight loss, night sweats, and fatigue. She denies any sore throat, fever, or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for occasional migraines, for which she takes ibuprofen. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory studies reveal anemia and elevated ESR. A chest X-ray is performed and shows widened mediastinum. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash on her face. She reports that the rash is especially noticeable after spending time in the sun and covers her cheeks and the bridge of her nose. The joint pain affects her wrists and knees and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting around 30 minutes. She denies any significant weight loss or fever. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. On examination, she has a malar rash and tenderness in the small joints of her hands. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic reporting progressively worsening bilateral hip pain over the past six months. She describes the pain as dull and deep-seated, exacerbated by prolonged sitting and climbing stairs. She has also noticed a decreased range of motion in her hips. She denies any morning stiffness in other joints or systemic symptoms, such as fever or weight loss. She has a history of primary hypothyroidism, for which she is taking levothyroxine. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 126/80 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness in the groin area and limited internal rotation of the hips. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and negative rheumatoid factor. X-ray of the hips indicates joint space narrowing predominantly in the superior aspect of the acetabulum. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She has no significant past medical history and is on no medications. Her family history is significant for autoimmune disease in her mother. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and skin changes. She reports that over the past 6 months, she has experienced intermittent joint pain and swelling, primarily affecting her fingers, wrists, and knees. These symptoms are associated with morning stiffness that lasts less than 30 minutes. She denies any recent injuries or infections. Additionally, she mentions developing scaly, red patches on her elbows and knees, which have been persistent despite using over-the-counter emollients. Her family history is significant for her mother having similar skin lesions. She does not have any significant medical history and takes no medications regularly. On examination, there are well-demarcated plaques with silvery scales on her extensor surfaces and tenderness in her distal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers but are otherwise unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute knee pain and swelling that started after she participated in a local charity run. She reports that the pain began suddenly after she tripped on an uneven surface, causing her left knee to twist awkwardly. Since then, she has been unable to bear weight on the affected leg. She notes no history of recent illness, fever, or other joint involvement. Her medical history is significant for mild asthma. She takes albuterol as needed. On examination, her left knee is visibly swollen, with tenderness over the medial joint line and a decreased range of motion secondary to pain. There is no erythema or warmth. McMurray's test is positive, and Lachman's test is negative. X-rays show no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and pain in her wrists and knees for the past several months. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with activity. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and bilateral wrists, with a decreased range of motion. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 11 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr, and rheumatoid factor is positive. Her C-reactive protein is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and stiffness over the past several months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and affects her hands, wrists, and knees symmetrically. She also notes occasional low-grade fevers and unintentional weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL), erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr), and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and symmetrical swelling in her hands. She has noted small, firm nodules under the skin near her elbows. She takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief, but denies any other medications. On physical examination, there is joint tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands show marginal erosions of the joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of progressive difficulty walking and a sensation of weakness in her left foot. She describes tripping frequently and has noticed a slapping sound when she walks. She also reports occasional tingling in her left leg. Her past medical history is significant only for mild joint pain in her hands over the past year. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. She works as a graphic designer and does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, she has a high-steppage gait. Strength testing reveals 3/5 strength in dorsiflexion of the left foot. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric in the upper extremities but decreased at the left ankle. There is decreased sensation to pinprick on the dorsal aspect of the left foot. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3
Platelet count 240,000/mm3
ESR 28 mm/h
Electromyography shows reduced conduction velocity in the left peroneal nerve. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-year history of chronic joint pain in her hands and wrists. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning and gradually improve throughout the day. She also mentions noticing small, firm lumps under the skin near her elbows. She has difficulty gripping objects due to stiffness and discomfort. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is visible swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of persistent pain and stiffness in her wrists and hands for the past two years. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for several hours. Recently, she has noticed small nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She denies any significant trauma or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for smoking and hypertension. Physical examination reveals bilateral synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with palpable subcutaneous nodules on the forearms. Laboratory tests show positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Based on the presentation and findings, which of the following is most likely related to her condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for over an hour, and improves as the day progresses. She has also noticed swelling in her fingers and fatigue. On examination, her wrists, and metacarpophalangeal joints are tender and swollen bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions in the affected areas. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of a rash on her face and joint pain. She notes that the rash becomes more pronounced after spending time in the sun. She also reports experiencing joint pain in her hands and knees over the past few weeks. Additionally, she has noticed small, painful ulcers in her mouth that have been persistent. Her medical history includes a diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia. She denies any fever, significant weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, there is an erythematous rash across her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and tenderness over several small joints in the hands. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, lasting for over an hour before it subsides as the day progresses. She denies trauma or significant injury to her hands. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory testing reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive rheumatoid factor test. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain in her hands and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports that the pain improves throughout the day but worsens with extended use. She also notes occasional swelling in her wrists and fingers. She denies any recent fever, skin rashes, or weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of her hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint swelling and stiffness for the past 2 months. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning, taking over an hour to improve. She reports swelling of her wrists and the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies, and she is currently taking no medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the wrists and multiple proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to swelling and stiffness in her fingers and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning, lasting for approximately 2 hours before improving. She also notes fatigue and occasional low-grade fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr (normal <20 mm/hr), C-reactive protein of 3.5 mg/dL (normal <1.0 mg/dL), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports swelling and pain in the joints of her hands and wrists, which are symmetrical and improve somewhat with movement as the day progresses. Morning stiffness lasts for more than an hour. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her past medical history includes hypertension and seasonal allergies. She takes lisinopril and loratadine regularly. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 14-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because of a new rash on her legs and blood in her urine noticed over the past week. The rash is purpuric and located primarily on the lower extremities. She reports joint pain in her knees and ankles, and occasional abdominal pain. She denies any history of recent infections or fevers. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 112/65 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower legs and mild swelling in the knees. Laboratory results show hematuria with RBC casts on urinalysis, but normal platelet count and coagulation studies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with progressive pain and deformity in her hands over the past 5 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and hypothyroidism, both well-managed with medication. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. Hand X-rays reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressive joint pain and stiffness, predominantly affecting her hands and wrists. Her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve somewhat throughout the day. She also notes occasional swelling and warmth in the joints. Her medical history is significant for Hashimoto's thyroiditis, and she is currently taking levothyroxine. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain over the past 3 months. She describes morning stiffness in her hands, wrists, and knees lasting over an hour, improving with activity throughout the day. She has also noticed some weight loss and feels generally unwell. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of bone pain and fatigue over the past several months. She has noticed that her lower back and pelvis area are particularly sore, and she sometimes feels discomfort in her hips. She is postmenopausal and has been taking calcium and vitamin D supplements regularly. Her medical history includes hypertension, and she is on lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight changes. On examination, there is no joint swelling, but there is tenderness upon palpation of the pelvic bones. Laboratory tests reveal elevated alkaline phosphatase levels, with normal calcium and phosphorus levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past three weeks. She reports that the symptoms started shortly after recovering from a viral respiratory infection. She notes that her joints feel stiff in the morning, but this improves after she starts moving around. There is no history of fevers, skin rashes, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. On physical examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. There is mild tenderness over her proximal interphalangeal joints, but no swelling or redness is noted. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 56-year-old woman visits her primary care provider with complaints of progressive joint pain and deformity in her hands over the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour which improves throughout the day but never fully resolves. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or injuries to her hands. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally, with ulnar deviation observed. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for over an hour, gradually improving throughout the day. She has difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her shirt and typing. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-ray of the hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-month history of bilateral hand stiffness and swelling, especially in the morning. She has difficulty gripping objects and reports that the stiffness improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On examination, both her metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Further testing shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her ankles and knees. She reports feeling fatigued and having a low-grade fever. She denies recent travel but mentions that she was diagnosed with streptococcal pharyngitis a month ago and completed her antibiotic course. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals bilateral, tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior shins. Laboratory studies show a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal white blood cell count. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Perform skin biopsy of the nodules
options.E: Obtain a rapid streptococcal antigen test
options.F: Screen for sarcoidosis with chest X-ray

question: A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of recurrent, painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers that have persisted for the last few months. She denies any new sexual partners or recent travel. She also mentions experiencing fatigue and joint stiffness predominantly in the morning, which resolves as the day progresses. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any regular medications. On examination, she has multiple aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity and similar lesions in the genital area. There is no lymphadenopathy, and her joints appear normal on inspection. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with concerns of joint pain and swelling that have developed over the past two months. She reports morning stiffness lasting for over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She also mentions fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history includes hypertension and seasonal allergies. On examination, she has synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her legs and blood in her urine. She reports joint pain in her knees and ankles, as well as fatigue. She denies any recent upper respiratory infections, sore throat, or fever. Her vital signs are temperature 98.9°F (37.2°C), blood pressure 132/85 mmHg, heart rate 78/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. On examination, there are palpable purpuric lesions on the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and elevated serum creatinine. A urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with chronic neck pain and intermittent tingling in her arms and hands for the past 8 months. She reports that the symptoms worsen with prolonged sitting at her desk job and occasionally wakes her up at night. The tingling is often accompanied by a burning sensation, and she feels weakness in her grip strength. She denies any recent trauma. She has a history of osteoporosis managed with calcium and vitamin D supplements. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and tenderness over the cervical paraspinal muscles. Neurological examination reveals diminished sensation to light touch in the C6 dermatome and mild weakness in wrist extension. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of swelling and pain in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She also notes occasional stiffness in her knees. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies and hypothyroidism. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Serology is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache, which is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She reports significant stiffness in the morning lasting for over an hour. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma, fever, weight loss, or rashes. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 50-year-old woman is referred to your neurology clinic due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 6 months. She reports weakness in her legs, particularly when climbing stairs or standing from a seated position. She also notes intermittent joint pain in her hands and knees, accompanied by occasional morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies numbness or tingling in her extremities. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals proximal muscle weakness in the lower extremities and mild tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Her creatine kinase (CK) levels are elevated, and an electromyography (EMG) study shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-week history of migrating joint pains and swelling. She initially noticed pain in her knees and ankles that seemed to improve before recurring in her wrists and elbows. She also reports fatigue and a sore throat that resolved without medication approximately 2 weeks before the onset of joint symptoms. She denies any recent travel, skin rashes, or weight loss. Her family history is significant for a sister with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the wrists and elbows, with no skin rashes or nodules. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but normal anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) and rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Obtain an X-ray of the affected joints
options.D: Test for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody
options.F: Refer to a rheumatologist for further evaluation

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of progressively worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and knees, particularly in the morning. She reports that the stiffness lasts for about an hour each morning before improving slightly. She has difficulty gripping objects and occasionally drops them. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation observed at the wrists. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to swelling and pain in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and lasting for over an hour before improving during the day. She has also noticed some fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She works as a software developer and has difficulty typing due to the pain. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and finds it challenging to open jars or hold a pen. She has noticed swelling and tenderness in both wrists and several fingers. She also mentions the presence of small, firm nodules under the skin near her elbows. Her mother had a similar condition in her later years. On physical examination, there is bilateral synovitis of the wrists and fingers, with subcutaneous nodules palpable on the extensor surfaces. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the last six months. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and affects both her wrists and knees. She denies any recent infections or trauma. She has a history of hypertension and is currently taking lisinopril. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in her wrists and knees bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 86 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, particularly affecting her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She also notes occasional swelling of her fingers. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint swelling, stiffness, and pain in her hands and knees. The stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting several hours before improving throughout the day. She denies any skin rashes or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, which are well-controlled with medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild synovitis. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serologic tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. A plain radiograph of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and mild joint space narrowing. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman visits the clinic due to sudden onset of joint pain and swelling in her knees, wrists, and ankles for the past week. She mentions that she returned from a vacation in Costa Rica two weeks ago. The patient reports fever, fatigue, and a rash that developed on her trunk and extremities. She does not have a history of previous joint issues, and her medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Physical examination reveals symmetrical joint swelling and tenderness, with a maculopapular rash on her legs. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive bilateral hip pain over the past 12 months. She describes the pain as a deep ache that is worse with activity, particularly after walking or standing for long periods. She reports that the pain has gradually become more constant and is now present even at rest. She denies any history of trauma or recent illness. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well controlled with medication. She has no family history of autoimmune or connective tissue diseases. On examination, there is no joint swelling, but there is tenderness over the greater trochanter bilaterally with pain on internal rotation of the hips. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and a negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of worsening pain in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning lasting more than an hour. She also reports fatigue and has noticed several firm nodules under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of intense pain and swelling in her right big toe, which started last night. She describes similar episodes in the past two years, affecting the same joint, each resolving within a week. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. Her diet includes frequent consumption of red meat and seafood, and she drinks wine occasionally. On examination, her right first metatarsophalangeal joint is swollen, erythematous, and extremely tender to touch. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum uric acid level. A joint aspiration shows needle-shaped crystals. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman visits her primary care clinic complaining of a rash on her face and joint pain in her hands and wrists that started 3 months ago. She describes the rash as red and appearing on her cheeks and the bridge of her nose, which becomes more pronounced after exposure to the sun. She also reports fatigue and occasional fevers. Her medical history is unremarkable. On examination, she has a symmetrical, erythematous rash over her cheeks and nose sparing the nasolabial folds. Her joints are tender to palpation but show no swelling. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 5,500/mm^3, and a normal comprehensive metabolic panel. An ANA test returns positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute knee pain and swelling that began after a soccer match yesterday. She reports that she twisted her knee awkwardly during the game, followed by immediate pain and inability to continue playing. Her medical history is significant for occasional joint pain in her hands, but she denies any morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. Vital signs include temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure 122/78 mm Hg, pulse 84/min, and respirations 16/min. On examination, the right knee appears swollen with a palpable effusion and is tender to touch, with limited range of motion due to pain. An aspiration of the knee joint is performed, showing cloudy fluid with a high white cell count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 4 months. She describes the pain as symmetric, primarily affecting her wrists, knees, and elbows. She mentions that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for about an hour before she can start moving around comfortably. She denies fevers, weight loss, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She reports no alcohol use and has a 10-pack-year smoking history. On examination, there is synovial swelling and tenderness over the wrists and metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 88 fL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty holding objects due to pain. Her past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. She works as an office manager and reports no recent changes in her environment or medication use. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal:

Serum:
ESR: 45 mm/hr
CRP: 30 mg/L
RF: Positive
Anti-CCP: Positive
ANA: Negative

X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past year. She states that the pain is particularly severe in the morning and subsides somewhat as the day progresses. She also reports feeling fatigued and having difficulty performing daily tasks due to stiffness in her fingers. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, treated with lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal joints and ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and the presence of rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and fatigue. She has a family history of autoimmune disease. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Serological tests show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is a characteristic feature that supports the diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is more pronounced in the morning and lasts for about 1 hour before improving as the day progresses. The pain is mostly in her hands and wrists, and she has noticed some swelling in these areas. She denies any recent infections, fever, or weight changes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory findings reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and night sweats. She reports feeling fatigued and has noticed some weight loss, but denies any changes in appetite. She also mentions occasional aching in her knees and fingers, though she attributes this to her recent increase in physical activity. On examination, there is palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An excisional biopsy of one of the cervical lymph nodes is planned. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and knees. She reports that the joint stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the knees. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are detected. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 4 months. The pain is most pronounced in her wrists and knuckles and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports no recent infections or changes in medications. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the wrists and MCP joints bilaterally. Laboratory findings include:

Hemoglobin: 10.5 g/dL
MCV: 88 fL
WBC: 8,500/mm³
Platelets: 300,000/mm³
ESR: 75 mm/hr

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-year history of progressive joint pain and stiffness. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve as the day progresses. She has difficulty opening jars and reports swelling of her wrists and fingers. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She takes lisinopril, atorvastatin, and over-the-counter ibuprofen as needed. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to progressive deformity in her fingers and wrists over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and swelling in multiple joints, particularly in the hands. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swan-neck deformities, and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers. She reports that the ulcers appear every 3-4 weeks and take approximately 1-2 weeks to heal. She also complains of occasional joint pain in her knees and ankles, as well as generalized fatigue. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent travel. On examination, multiple aphthous ulcers are seen in the oral cavity, and there are healed scars on the labia. No synovitis or oral candidiasis is noted. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein. Based on her clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting more than an hour before improving. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are also elevated. X-rays of her hands show marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and difficulty concentrating. She also reports swelling in her legs and a 5-pound weight gain over the same period. Her blood pressure is 165/100 mm Hg, and laboratory studies show:
Blood urea nitrogen 25 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL
Urinalysis shows proteinuria and hematuria.
She has no joint pain or morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled with diet and metformin. She denies any significant family history of renal disease. Renal ultrasound shows bilateral small kidneys. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Refer for renal artery Doppler ultrasound

question: A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists, which she has noticed worsening over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent travel or infections. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, including an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on her clinical presentation?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and unintentional weight gain over the past 6 months. She also reports feeling cold more often than usual and experiencing constipation. Her family history is significant for autoimmune thyroid disease. On examination, she appears tired and has dry, coarse skin. Her thyroid is diffusely enlarged but non-tender. Laboratory tests reveal low serum T4, elevated TSH, and positive anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies. She denies joint pain, but reports occasional morning stiffness in her fingers that resolves within an hour. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty walking over the past 2 months. She reports that her legs feel weak and often give out when she tries to stand up from a seated position. She describes intermittent pain and stiffness in her knees and fingers, especially in the mornings, improving with movement throughout the day. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension, both well-controlled with medication. On examination, she has difficulty rising from a chair without using her arms and exhibits proximal muscle weakness in the lower limbs. Reflexes are normal. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, Leukocyte count 6,800/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/h, Creatine kinase 210 IU/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of worsening joint pain and swelling over the past 6 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning her shirt. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hyperlipidemia. She is currently on metformin and atorvastatin. On physical examination, her hands show bilateral swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with tenderness and reduced grip strength. There are no skin rashes. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following treatments is most appropriate for this patient's condition?

A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of fatigue, weight loss, and the recent development of firm, painless nodules over the extensor surfaces of her elbows and forearms. She also reports occasional low-grade fevers and generalized aches. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she is on lisinopril, and she notes that her mother had a 'similar condition' in her later years. On examination, she has symmetrical swelling of her wrists, but her range of motion is preserved. Laboratory results show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?

options.G: Testing for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with movement throughout the day. She has a family history of autoimmune conditions. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies and rheumatoid factor are positive. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressive joint pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. The pain initially affected her wrists and fingers but has now also involved her knees and ankles. She reports significant fatigue and occasional low-grade fever. She has a family history of autoimmune disease. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with visible ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies show:

Rheumatoid factor: Positive (high titer)
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies: Positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 50 mm/hr
C-reactive protein (CRP): Elevated

X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of unexplained weight gain and edema around her eyes. She reports feeling stiff in her joints, particularly in the morning, and notes that her clothes have been fitting more tightly around the waist. She also describes episodes of fatigue and difficulty concentrating. She denies any recent changes in diet or physical activity. On physical examination, she has periorbital edema, and pitting edema is noted in her ankles. Blood pressure is 140/88 mmHg, and her pulse is 78/min. Further laboratory workup reveals elevated serum creatinine and low serum albumin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits the clinic due to worsening joint pain and stiffness over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour and difficulty with daily activities due to swelling in her hands and feet. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, both well-controlled with medication. She denies any recent fevers, weight changes, or skin rashes. Her family history is notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, as well as the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and erosions consistent with rheumatoid arthritis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.F: Order an MRI of the hands
options.G: Monitor symptoms with follow-up in 6 months

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of generalized body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fever. She reports recovering from a viral upper respiratory infection 3 weeks ago. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medication. She denies joint swelling or redness. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals diffuse tenderness over multiple muscle groups, but no swelling or erythema of the joints. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 5,800/mm3, Platelet count 250,000/mm3, and C-reactive protein 12 mg/L (normal <5 mg/L). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
