A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in her lower extremities. She reports frequent falls and increasing fatigue. She has occasional joint pain in the mornings, which resolves by midday. She has hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and takes no other medications. On examination, there is significant weakness in her bilateral hip flexors and knee extensors. Reflexes are 1+ at the knees and absent at the ankles. Sensation is intact. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3
Creatine kinase 450 U/L
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 35 mm/h
Antinuclear antibody titer negative
Rheumatoid factor positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 5-year history of progressive stiffness and pain in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning. She describes the stiffness as lasting more than an hour after she wakes up every day. She also reports difficulty with daily activities such as buttoning her shirt and opening jars. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Deformities of her fingers, including ulnar deviation, are noted. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain, fatigue, and intermittent fevers. She reports that the pain is primarily in her wrists and knees, and it is associated with morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She also notes worsening fatigue and an unintentional weight loss of 5 kg during this period. On physical examination, she is pale and has noticeable swelling and tenderness in her wrists and knees. Complete blood count shows pancytopenia characterized by a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,500/mm3, and platelet count of 110,000/mm3. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and pain in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness improves throughout the day but recurs each morning. She also mentions fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and decreased range of motion in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 27-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of a rash that appears after sun exposure, joint pain, and mouth sores for the past two weeks. She notes that the rash worsens when she spends time outdoors. She denies any significant weight loss, night sweats, or recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over her cheeks and bridge of her nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. She also has tender swelling of her wrists and mild swelling of her proximal interphalangeal joints. Oral examination reveals shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa. Her vital signs are temperature 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 78/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of swelling, pain, and deformity in her hands and wrists over the past 5 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, and she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Bilateral ulnar deviation and subluxation are noted. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves as the day progresses. She has noticed firm nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and reports fatigue and weight loss over the past few months. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated on the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman visits her physician due to a 6-month history of unexplained weight gain and periorbital swelling. She also describes a mild morning stiffness in her wrists and knees that improves gradually throughout the day. Over the past few months, she has felt increasingly fatigued and has noticed her face seems puffier than before. She reports no changes in diet or physical activity. Her medical history is significant only for seasonal allergies, and she takes cetirizine as needed. Physical examination reveals puffy eyelids, slight pallor, and non-pitting edema of the lower extremities. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, elevated TSH levels, and low free T4 levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of increasing muscle weakness. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. The weakness is primarily in her shoulders and hips. She denies any joint pain, but mentions feeling more fatigued than usual. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. She takes loratadine as needed. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness. There is no joint swelling or tenderness. The skin examination shows a faint erythematous rash on her upper eyelids. Laboratory studies show:
Creatine kinase 1200 U/L
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/h
Antinuclear antibody positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and feels fatigued despite adequate sleep. Her mother had a similar condition. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There is ulnar deviation and subluxation at the wrists. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing, marginal erosions, and osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and episodes of fingers turning blue in the cold, which she has noticed for the past 8 months. She also reports fatigue and mild joint pain that is worse in the morning. The patient mentions that her sister has rheumatoid arthritis. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals taut, shiny skin on the fingers of both hands and decreased range of motion in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Nail fold capillary examination shows dilated capillaries with areas of dropout. Laboratory results reveal positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 3-month history of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. She also reports occasional joint pain in her hands and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension treated with amlodipine. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, she appears tired, her heart rate is 58/min, and her blood pressure is 135/86 mm Hg. Her thyroid is enlarged on palpation. Laboratory tests show elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in both her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is particularly severe in the morning, and it takes her over an hour to feel more comfortable and able to move around. She has no history of trauma to the joints. On examination, the patient's metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and stiffness, particularly affecting her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves slightly with activity. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Examination reveals bilaterally swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated at 45 mm/hr. Hand X-rays reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after tripping and falling on an outstretched hand. She reports immediate swelling and difficulty moving her wrist. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteoporosis. She takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. On examination, there is significant swelling and tenderness around the wrist, with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is performed and shows a distal radius fracture. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers and a complete blood count is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the urgent care clinic complaining of joint pain and a rash. She reports spending a weekend hiking in a wooded area of Pennsylvania about two weeks ago. Since then, she has experienced fatigue, recurrent low-grade fevers, and joint pain predominantly in the knees and wrists. She also noticed a large, circular, red rash with central clearing on her right thigh. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional tension headaches. On examination, her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), and she has multiple tender and swollen joints in her hands and knees. A skin examination reveals the described rash on her thigh. Her laboratory results show mild leukopenia and elevated ESR. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care physician because of persistent joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings, over the past 6 months. The symptoms are primarily affecting her wrists and fingers on both hands and improve as the day progresses. She reports significant fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers but denies rash or photosensitivity. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her family history is notable for an aunt with systemic lupus erythematosus. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of fatigue and joint pain affecting both her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. Her symptoms started 6 months ago and have progressively worsened. She denies any recent illness or trauma. Her medical history includes hypertension and she takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein of 2.5 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely condition causing her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of hand pain and stiffness for the past 8 months. She states that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and typically lasts for over an hour. She has difficulty performing tasks such as buttoning her shirt due to the pain. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally, along with ulnar deviation. Laboratory investigations show a positive anti-citrullinated peptide antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of purplish lesions on her lower legs and intermittent abdominal pain. She also noticed blood in her urine on two occasions. The patient denies any recent trauma or significant illness. She has a history of seasonal allergies and anxiety, for which she takes cetirizine and sertraline, respectively. On examination, there are palpable purpuric lesions on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a serum creatinine of 1.3 mg/dL, normal range erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and urinalysis reveals hematuria with red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and pain in multiple joints over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache, primarily affecting her wrists, hands, and knees, and notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has also experienced significant fatigue and has difficulty performing daily activities. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, both well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as mild synovitis in the knees. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin concentration of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), and normal thyroid function tests. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody levels are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive pain in her hands and wrists for the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing tasks such as opening jars. She has also noticed small lumps on the back of her forearms. She denies any fever or recent weight loss. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes controlled with metformin. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal joints and subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her neurologist with a 9-month history of chronic neck pain radiating to her right arm, accompanied by intermittent tingling and numbness in her thumb and index finger. She has noticed her grip strength has diminished over the last few months, and she struggles with tasks requiring fine motor skills. The patient denies any trauma but mentions frequent stiffness in her neck in the mornings that improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is decreased sensation in the right C6 dermatome and diminished biceps reflex on the same side. Cervical spine MRI shows disc herniation at C5-C6 with foraminal narrowing. Which of the following is most consistent with this patient’s presentation?

A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of joint pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past year. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, often lasting for over an hour before improving with activity. She has difficulty with tasks like opening jars and turning door knobs. Her medical history includes osteoporosis and hypothyroidism, for which she takes alendronate and levothyroxine. She denies any recent trauma or unusual physical activity. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. An x-ray of her hands demonstrates periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 3-month history of lower back pain that improves with exercise but worsens with rest. She reports occasional morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She denies any swelling in her hands or feet. Her medical history is unremarkable, but her father has a history of chronic back issues. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show an elevated C-reactive protein level and a normal rheumatoid factor. Pelvic radiographs are ordered, which show bilateral sacroiliitis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of painful oral and genital ulcers over the past year. She reports these ulcers typically last for several weeks before healing and are associated with flares. She also has experienced intermittent joint pain, particularly in her knees and elbows, which is more pronounced in the morning but usually resolves by mid-day. These episodes are accompanied by fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She denies any significant weight loss, ocular symptoms, or bowel changes. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any chronic medication. On examination, multiple shallow, round ulcers are noted on the buccal mucosa and at the labia majora. Joint examination reveals mild tenderness of the knees without swelling. Laboratory workup shows elevated ESR and CRP, negative ANA, and negative anti-dsDNA antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of bilateral hand joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports difficulty opening jars and pain in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Her medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory results reveal:

ESR: 45 mm/hr
CRP: 18 mg/L
RF: Positive
Anti-CCP antibody: Positive
ANA: Negative

An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of right shoulder pain. She notes that the pain worsens when reaching overhead and has restricted her ability to perform daily activities, such as combing her hair. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 15 minutes but denies any fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the acromioclavicular joint and limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder. There is no swelling or erythema in other joints. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.B: Corticosteroid injection into the shoulder joint

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with progressive thickening of the skin on her fingers, accompanied by episodes of severe finger discoloration upon exposure to cold. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red and painful. Additionally, she reports fatigue and occasional joint stiffness, especially in the mornings. She denies swelling, and her medical history is otherwise unremarkable except for infrequent heartburn. On examination, there is noticeable tightening of the skin over the fingers, and telangiectasias are visible on her face. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with a speckled pattern, but rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman is referred to the neurology clinic due to worsening weakness in her shoulders and hips over the past 6 months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. She denies joint pain, but notes some discomfort in her fingers. She also complains of fatigue and a 10 lb weight loss over this period. Her past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes and hypertension. On examination, she has decreased strength in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower extremities. Reflexes are 2+ throughout, and there is no sensory loss. Laboratory studies reveal a creatine kinase level of 750 U/L (normal range: 20-200 U/L). Further workup is planned. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past three months. She has also noticed occasional joint pain in her knees and wrists but attributes it to her increased workload. The patient denies any significant changes in diet or exercise. Her medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her temperature is 98.0°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, hematocrit of 28%, and a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Further serological testing is ordered to assess her condition. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of 6-month history of pain and stiffness in her fingers, wrists, and knees. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before she can function normally. She has been using over-the-counter NSAIDs with partial relief. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including her mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists are swollen and tender bilaterally. There is no skin rash or fever. Laboratory studies show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent lymphadenopathy, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds. She also reports experiencing periodic fevers and generalized fatigue. The patient mentions that she has noticed painless swelling in her neck and axillary regions. On examination, multiple firm, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory studies reveal a mild normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of unexplained weight gain and increasing periorbital edema over the past 3 months. She reports morning joint stiffness lasting about 20 minutes and notices her rings have become tighter. She denies any significant changes in her diet or lifestyle. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. She does not have a family history of thyroid or autoimmune diseases. On examination, her blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg, pulse 82/min, temperature 98.6°F (37°C), and BMI is 29 kg/m2. She has notable puffiness around the eyes and mild pallor of the conjunctiva. The thyroid is not enlarged. Laboratory results reveal:

Serum:
Na+: 136 mEq/L
K+: 4.1 mEq/L
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
TSH: 5.8 mIU/L (normal: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L)
T4: 0.7 ng/dL (normal: 0.8-1.7 ng/dL)

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 31-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation presents to the clinic with lower back and pelvic pain for the past two months. She describes the pain as a constant dull ache that is exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking. She denies fever, urinary symptoms, or vaginal discharge. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes prenatal vitamins. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and a waddling gait. Her laboratory tests, including a complete blood count and comprehensive metabolic panel, are normal. An ultrasound of the pelvis shows no abnormalities. What is the most appropriate management for this patient's condition?

options.C: Physical therapy and pelvic support belt

question: A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past 6 months. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and tends to improve by the afternoon. She has also noticed swelling and some deformity in her fingers. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism and she takes levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals swelling and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past 6 months. She also reports feeling cold more often than usual and has noticed some swelling in her hands and feet. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, she appears tired, and her skin is dry. Her vital signs include: temperature 97.8°F (36.6°C), blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, pulse 58/min, and respirations 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal: TSH 8.5 μIU/mL (normal 0.5–4.5 μIU/mL), free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal 0.8–1.8 ng/dL), and a positive antithyroglobulin antibody. Which of the following is the most likely associated finding in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of persistent lower back pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, which improves with movement throughout the day. She reports feeling fatigued, and on some days, she experiences mild fever. Her past medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, she has limited spinal mobility with tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her range of motion is significantly restricted in all planes, and she struggles with bending forward. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays show sacroiliitis but no erosive joint changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic because of difficulty walking and a noticeable foot drop on the right side for the past 3 weeks. She reports tripping over her foot frequently and has begun using a cane for support. She describes mild discomfort in her right calf and an occasional tingling sensation in her foot. She has a history of osteoarthritis and hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent trauma or changes in her medication. Neurological examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the right ankle and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot. Reflexes are intact. An MRI of the lumbar spine is unremarkable. Electromyography (EMG) indicates denervation in the muscles innervated by the right common peroneal nerve. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

options.B: Peroneal nerve compression at the fibular head

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute left knee pain and swelling that started after playing soccer three days ago. She describes the pain as sharp and rates it as 7/10. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or other joint involvement. She has a history of mild asthma and takes albuterol as needed. On examination, her left knee is swollen, warm, and tender with a limited range of motion. There is no erythema. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 12,000/mm³, with neutrophil predominance. X-ray of the knee reveals joint effusion with no fractures or visible erosions. Arthrocentesis yields cloudy synovial fluid with an elevated white cell count and negative Gram stain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the primary care clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 5 months. She reports that her joints, especially her wrists and knees, are stiff in the morning for about an hour. She has experienced swelling in her hands that makes it difficult to grasp objects. She denies any recent weight loss, fever, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 85 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. A rheumatoid factor test is positive, and an anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody is also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a three-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting her wrists and knees. She reports a persistent rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure and frequent mouth ulcers. She also complains of generalized fatigue and mild fever. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any smoking or alcohol use. Her mother has a history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. A malar rash is noted, and there is tenderness and swelling of the wrists and knees. Laboratory tests reveal anemia, positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA), and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old woman visits the clinic due to persistent fatigue and unexplained weight loss over the past 3 months. She reports intermittent fevers and night sweats. Recently, she noticed painless swelling in her neck that has gradually increased in size. She also experiences occasional joint pain, primarily in her knees and wrists, but denies morning stiffness. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her family history is unremarkable. On examination, there is a 3 cm non-tender, mobile lymph node in the left cervical region, and mild splenomegaly is noted. Laboratory studies reveal mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing fine motor tasks. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her medical history is notable for hyperlipidemia and she is currently taking atorvastatin. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 42 mm/hr. Hand radiographs demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers over the past six months. She also reports episodes of joint pain in her knees and ankles, as well as occasional fatigue. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination shows multiple shallow ulcers in the oral mucosa and two healing ulcers in the genital area. There is mild tenderness and swelling in the knees bilaterally, but the range of motion is preserved. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 54-year-old woman visits the clinic due to persistent pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for over an hour. She also reports fatigue and weight loss of 4 kg (8.8 lbs) over the past 3 months. She has a family history of autoimmune disorders. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and deformities of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Several firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory testing shows:
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3
Platelet count 320,000/mm3
C-reactive protein 15 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of painful, swollen joints in her hands and wrists, which she describes as being stiffest in the morning for about an hour before loosening up. She reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h and a C-reactive protein level of 14 mg/L. Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are elevated. Radiographs of the hands show juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of hand deformities and pain that have been worsening over the past 3 years. The pain is most severe in the morning, lasting for several hours, and gradually improves as the day progresses. She reports difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her shirt and opening jars. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swan-neck deformities of the fingers and ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and bony erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of a rash on her face and joint pain. She reports that the rash becomes more pronounced with sun exposure and that the joint pain is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She also notes occasional swelling in her knees and wrists. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for recurrent oral ulcers. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over her cheeks and nasal bridge sparing the nasolabial folds, as well as mild swelling and tenderness of the wrists and knees. Laboratory findings show a mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with persistent pain in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She describes the pain as aching and notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has noticed subcutaneous nodules near her elbows and reports fatigue and a decreased range of motion in her wrists. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints are swollen and tender bilaterally with visible deformities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels, along with positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. She reports using over-the-counter artificial tears multiple times a day and frequently sipping water to alleviate oral dryness. In addition, she has noted bilateral swelling in the jaw area, particularly after meals. She denies any significant joint pain or swelling but mentions occasional fatigue. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, currently managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, bilateral parotid gland enlargement is noted. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal a white blood cell count of 5,800/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 28 mm/h, and a negative rheumatoid factor test. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening in her fingers that she has noticed over the past 3 months. She also reports episodes of her fingers turning white and then blue when exposed to cold, followed by redness upon rewarming. These episodes are accompanied by pain and numbness in her fingers. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional heartburn. She denies any joint swelling or significant fatigue. On examination, her fingers show shiny, tightened skin with some loss of the normal skin folds. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of bilateral joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports fatigue and difficulty with gripping objects due to swelling in her wrists and fingers. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. On examination, her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender to palpation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain, primarily affecting her fingers and wrists. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, often associated with stiffness lasting over an hour. She has difficulty gripping objects and performing daily activities due to this stiffness. She denies any recent injuries or infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any regular medications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of her hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is most consistent with this patient's diagnosis?

options.D: Uric acid crystals in joint fluid

question: A 58-year-old female presents to the clinic with progressively worsening deformity of her fingers over the last few years. She reports persistent pain and stiffness in her hands, particularly in the morning, which lasts for over an hour. She has difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her clothes and opening jars. She has been taking ibuprofen with limited relief. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. On examination, there is noticeable ulnar deviation and swan-neck deformities of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain affecting both her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving as the day progresses. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurring painful ulcers in her mouth and genital area over the past year. She describes having at least 3-4 episodes in the past 12 months, with each episode lasting about 1-2 weeks. During these episodes, she also experiences joint pain and swelling in her knees and wrists. She denies any significant fatigue, eye redness, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes ibuprofen occasionally for joint discomfort. She has no significant family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, she has several small, shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and one on the vulva. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 120/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal normal complete blood count, normal liver and renal function tests, negative rheumatoid factor, and mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and a 'slapping' gait that has progressively worsened over the past few weeks. She reports that she recently suffered from a viral infection, which resolved without complications. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and she is a long-term smoker. Her current medications are metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. On neurological examination, she has weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot, decreased pinprick sensation over the lateral aspect of her right leg, and an absent right ankle reflex. Laboratory studies show a normal blood glucose level and ESR is mildly elevated. An EMG confirms peroneal nerve involvement. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour that improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is significant for eczema during her teenage years. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show symmetric joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and a dry cough. She reports experiencing fatigue and weight loss over the same period. Additionally, she has noticed blood in her urine and swelling in her ankles. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals bilateral crackles in the lung bases and mild edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show anemia, elevated creatinine, and hematuria with red blood cell casts. A chest radiograph indicates patchy pulmonary infiltrates bilaterally. Which of the following conditions is most likely the underlying cause of her symptoms?

options.F: Chronic kidney disease of unknown etiology

question: A 59-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of fatigue, weight loss, and generalized malaise. She reports a recent onset of non-painful nodules over her forearms and shins. She also describes occasional joint pain, but denies morning stiffness. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, she has firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her arms and legs. Her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 140/85 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin concentration of 11 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and mild transaminase elevation. An autoimmune panel is ordered. Which of the following is most likely related to the nodules observed in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands due to swelling and tenderness. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 130/84 mm Hg, heart rate is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her fingers and toes. She notes that her symptoms worsen at night and occasionally interrupt her sleep. She also reports fatigue and has noticed that her hands become cold and occasionally swell. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, for which she takes metformin. She denies recent infections or significant weight changes. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in the distal extremities and reduced vibratory sense. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 125/82 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin A1c of 7.8%, a normal complete blood count, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 30 mm/h. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.C: Perform electromyography and nerve conduction studies

question: A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to chronic pain in her hands and wrists for the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands for daily activities. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with several firm subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and generalized weakness for the past few months. She also notes nodules on her elbows and occasional low-grade fevers. She describes joint discomfort, particularly in her hands and wrists, which is exacerbated by activity and relieved by rest. The patient has a past medical history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both adequately controlled with medication. On examination, there are several firm, non-tender, subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral interstitial markings. Which of the following is most likely associated with this patient's presentation?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain. She reports that the pain is worse with movement and that she has difficulty reaching overhead or behind her back. She denies any recent trauma or injury. Her past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterior shoulder and restricted range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no erythema or swelling of the joint. Strength testing is limited due to pain. Laboratory studies show: ESR 25 mm/hr, CRP 1.2 mg/dL. X-rays show no acute fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 57-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist with complaints of worsening joint deformities in her hands and wrists over the past six months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and significant pain that improves slightly with movement throughout the day. Her medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals notable ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints and mild swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of her hands displays joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports joint pain in her wrists and knees, as well as having painful oral ulcers. She denies any recent weight loss or fever. Her medical history includes mild seasonal allergies, and she takes no regular medications. She has never smoked and drinks alcohol socially. On examination, she has a well-demarcated erythematous rash over her cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Her wrists and knees show mild tenderness without swelling. Laboratory results show:

ANA: Positive
Anti-dsDNA: Positive
Rheumatoid factor: Negative
ESR: 35 mm/hr
WBC: 3,800/mm³

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She describes the pain as affecting both hands and wrists symmetrically, with stiffness lasting over an hour in the morning. The patient reports fatigue and difficulty performing daily activities due to joint pain. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the past month due to weakness in her lower extremities. She describes a clumsy gait and occasional falls. She reports morning stiffness and mild pain in her knees and hips that improves with activity. She has experienced headaches and blurry vision intermittently for the past two weeks. She denies any recent infections, fevers, or weight changes. Her medical history is notable for migraine headaches and she takes sumatriptan as needed. On physical examination, she has reduced muscle strength in both lower limbs and hyperreflexia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint stiffness, and new-onset hypertension. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the mornings and lasts for more than an hour. She has no known medical conditions and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. Urinalysis reveals mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

options.B: Chronic kidney disease secondary to hypertension

question: A 49-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive deformity of her fingers and wrists over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. She has difficulty performing daily tasks like opening jars and writing. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with visible ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies, with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with pain and swelling in her right wrist following a fall on an outstretched hand while playing soccer. She reports immediate pain and inability to move her wrist without discomfort. There is no history of previous joint issues. Her medical history is unremarkable, except for seasonal allergies. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the distal radius, and she has a limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care office with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the last six months. She notes that her hands, wrists, and knees are affected, with pain and stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. She also reports feeling unusually tired even after a full night's sleep. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. She denies recent infections, weight loss, or fever. On examination, her hands and knees are swollen and tender bilaterally, with decreased range of motion. Laboratory findings include:
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 84 fL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 78 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor Positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide Positive
Antinuclear antibody Negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old Caucasian woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past several months. She reports frequent use of over-the-counter artificial tears and finds it difficult to swallow dry foods without water. She also notes occasional swelling in the region of her cheeks and mild joint pains, primarily in her hands, with the discomfort being worse in the mornings. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.G: CT scan of the parotid glands

question: A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in her fingers and wrists and is worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving. She also mentions fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. There is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with some ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is most specific for confirming the diagnosis associated with her symptoms?

options.C: High levels of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies

question: A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of a rash on her face and joint pain that have been present for several weeks. She describes the rash as worsening with sun exposure. She also notes fatigue and intermittent fever. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Family history is significant for systemic lupus erythematosus in her mother. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and tenderness in the small joints of the hands. Laboratory tests show a positive ANA titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that her symptoms are primarily in her wrists, fingers, and knees, with stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and she is a non-smoker. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 132/78 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show a high erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits the clinic due to worsening joint pain and stiffness in her hands and feet over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour, and improves throughout the day. She has noticed swelling in her fingers and wrists. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory findings reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 6 months. She reports feeling excessively tired despite adequate sleep and has lost 10 pounds without trying. Additionally, she mentions occasional joint pain, particularly in her hands and knees, but denies swelling or morning stiffness. Her menstrual cycles have been irregular for the last year. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, she appears pale and her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory investigations reveal a microcytic anemia with low serum ferritin. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive muscle weakness, particularly affecting her shoulders and hips. She also reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. There is no associated joint pain, fever, or weight loss. She has a history of hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals decreased muscle strength in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs, with no tenderness or swelling of the joints. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase and normal inflammatory markers. Electromyography reveals myopathic changes. A muscle biopsy is performed, showing perimysial inflammation and perifascicular atrophy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive stiffness and swelling in her hands. She mentions that the symptoms started about six months ago and have gradually worsened. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting several hours before easing slightly. She has difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars and buttoning shirts. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She reports these symptoms have progressively worsened and are now accompanied by a burning sensation, particularly in her feet. She also notes intermittent joint pain and swelling, particularly in her hands, which worsens in the morning. She has a history of well-controlled hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL and an MCV of 102 fL. Her serum B12 level is found to be low. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

options.E: Order an MRI of the spine
options.F: Refer to rheumatology for rheumatoid arthritis evaluation

question: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe bone pain and generalized fatigue. She reports that the pain has been worsening over the past few months, and she often feels more tired than usual. She denies any recent injuries or falls. She also mentions intermittent nausea and occasional episodes of constipation. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, she appears mildly dehydrated. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 130/84 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal a serum calcium level of 12.2 mg/dL and a parathyroid hormone level that is elevated. A bone density scan confirms osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of joint pain in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for about an hour before improving throughout the day. She also notes swelling in her fingers and wrists. She denies any recent fevers, weight loss, or rashes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (normal 0-20 mm/hr) and C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 1.2 mg/dL (normal <0.5 mg/dL). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand while hiking. She reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist. She denies any numbness or tingling in her fingers. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. On examination, the right wrist is swollen and tender, with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no obvious deformity, and neurovascular examination is intact. An X-ray of the wrist is pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting around one hour before she starts feeling better. Over the past few months, she has developed small, firm nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She also reports significant fatigue and decreased grip strength. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for about 2 hours before improving. She denies any recent trauma or fevers but reports feeling fatigued and having occasional low-grade fevers. She has no skin rashes or photosensitivity. Her medical history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is synovitis and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal the following: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, White blood cell count 8,000/mm³, Platelet count 250,000/mm³, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the office with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting her hands and wrists bilaterally. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that gradually improves through the day. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent infections. She is not on any medications and has no known allergies. On physical examination, her joints are warm and swollen with decreased range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Additionally, her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Considering these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain, primarily affecting her wrists and fingers bilaterally. She reports that the pain and stiffness are most pronounced in the morning, lasting up to 1 hour, and improve as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. There is no erythema or increased warmth over the joints. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal uric acid levels. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and progressive joint deformity over the past 2 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her hands and wrists. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of her hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the pain is symmetric and affects her wrists and fingers, particularly in the morning, lasting for several hours. She has noted increased fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, she has swollen and tender joints in her hands, with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor test is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are also elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that improves with activity but worsens with rest. The pain is most severe in the morning and gradually decreases throughout the day. She also reports occasional pain in her hips and fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes ibuprofen with minimal relief. She denies any bowel or bladder symptoms, fevers, or recent infections. Physical examination reveals a reduced range of motion in the lower back. Her vital signs are normal, and there are no peripheral joint abnormalities. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her hands and feet. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that gradually improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections, trauma, or weight loss. Her family history includes a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain, increasing fatigue, and swelling around her eyes over the past 4 months. She also describes morning stiffness in her joints, predominantly affecting her wrists and knees, lasting for about an hour each day. She denies any recent changes in diet or physical activity and has no significant past medical history. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, she appears slightly overweight with notable periorbital edema. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show elevated cholesterol and triglyceride levels. Her TSH is 8.2 mIU/L (normal: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness that has been worsening over the last 3 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour before she feels any relief. The pain is primarily in her wrists and the proximal joints of her fingers. She has no significant past medical history. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially. Her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are also elevated. Hand X-rays show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. Her symptoms are particularly severe in the morning and improve as the day progresses. She also reports occasional swelling in these joints. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is synovitis of the bilateral wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints, along with the presence of subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following findings is most likely to support the diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist with complaints of increasing joint stiffness and deformity over the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting several hours and pain in her hands and wrists, which has progressed despite NSAID use. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender, with noticeable ulnar deviation and swan-neck deformities. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, a positive rheumatoid factor, and high titers of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand radiographs reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is most consistent with this patient's current condition?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities due to joint pain. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there are swelling and tenderness noted in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists, as well as the presence of subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory studies reveal:
Rheumatoid factor (RF) positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 45 mm/hr
C-reactive protein (CRP) elevated
Hand X-rays show marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is most specifically associated with this patient's diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting more than an hour before improving with activity. She has also noticed swelling in her fingers and wrists, making it difficult to perform her job as a typist. Her medical history includes hypertension for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, rashes, or fever. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, tender, and warm bilaterally, with no nodules or deformities. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows soft tissue swelling and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with difficulty walking and a noticeable dragging of her right foot. She reports that these symptoms began gradually over the past two months. She denies any recent trauma or injury. On further questioning, she admits to some mild fatigue and morning stiffness in her hands and wrists that improves as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and diminished sensation over the dorsum of the foot and lateral aspect of the calf. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric in the upper extremities, but the right ankle jerk is diminished. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and occasional genital ulcers. She reports experiencing joint pain in her knees and ankles, along with fatigue. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers on the oral mucosa and a single ulcer on the genital area. There is no synovitis or deformity in the joints, but she exhibits mild tenderness in the knees. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping. She reports that her pain is persistent and affects her neck, shoulders, back, and hips. She experiences stiffness in the morning that lasts for over an hour. She also mentions feeling more tired than usual, even after a full night's sleep, and has difficulty concentrating at work. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, there are multiple tender points noted on the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and gluteal muscles. Laboratory tests including a complete blood count and metabolic panel are unremarkable, but the erythrocyte sedimentation rate is slightly elevated.
