Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 3-month history of persistent swelling of the lymph nodes in her neck, accompanied by fatigue and night sweats. She occasionally experiences low-grade fevers and has lost approximately 5 kg (11 lbs) unintentionally over the past few months. She denies any cough, hemoptysis, or recent infections. On examination, she has multiple non-tender cervical lymph nodes that are 2-3 cm in size. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 12.5 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 9,000/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. A chest X-ray shows mild mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain is most pronounced in her hands and wrists, and she experiences morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has noticed swelling in her fingers and decreased grip strength. She denies any fever, skin rashes, or recent infections. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated at 50 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive deformity and pain in her hands over the past 3 years. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her clothes. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and osteoarthritis. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Her medications include lisinopril and acetaminophen. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Ulnar deviation of the fingers is noted. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of persistent joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving slightly. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. She has a history of hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and reports mild fatigue over the same period. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with slight ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated at 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves with activity throughout the day. She has also noticed some deformities developing in her fingers. She denies any skin rashes, oral ulcers, or photosensitivity. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with tenderness and reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks like opening jars. On examination, both her hands show synovitis at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with notable subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and she has a high titer of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with pain and swelling in her right wrist after slipping on ice and landing on her outstretched hand earlier in the day. She reports immediate pain and inability to move the wrist. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis and fibromyalgia. She takes alendronate and ibuprofen as needed. On examination, there is swelling, bruising, and tenderness over the distal radius, with limited range of motion due to pain. Her vital signs are stable. An x-ray of the wrist shows a distal radius fracture with dorsal angulation. Her complete blood count is normal, but the erythrocyte sedimentation rate is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Immobilize the wrist with a splint
options.D: Order a bone mineral density test
options.F: Perform a closed reduction of the fracture
options.G: Start calcium and vitamin D supplementation

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers for the last 2 years. She also reports episodes of genital ulcers that heal with scarring. She denies any recent sexual activity or new partners. Over the past few months, she has noted joint pain in her knees and ankles, which is accompanied by swelling and morning stiffness. On examination, her vital signs are stable. There are multiple shallow ulcers with erythematous borders in the oral cavity and healed scars on the labia. No synovitis is noted on joint examination. Laboratory testing shows elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of progressively worsening joint pain and swelling in her hands. She notes that her fingers are becoming increasingly deformed, particularly the proximal interphalangeal joints, and she experiences significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and denies any recent infections or new medications. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender. There is a ulnar deviation of the fingers at the MCP joints. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of back pain that is worse in the mornings and improves with activity. She also notes fatigue and occasional pain in her hips. She reports no fevers or weight loss. Her family history is significant for her father having a similar back condition. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited spinal mobility. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein levels and negative rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the pelvis reveals bilateral sacroiliitis. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 53-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. The symptoms are worse in the morning and improve as the day progresses. She has noticed swelling in her fingers and tenderness to touch. She denies any recent infections or injuries. Her past medical history includes mild hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-week history of generalized fatigue and swelling in her legs. She also reports a new onset of headaches and blurred vision. Her past medical history includes obesity. She is not on any medications. Her blood pressure is 165/100 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals bilateral lower extremity edema. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. Urinalysis reveals proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her sister. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the urgent care clinic with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling following an intense running session 2 days ago. She describes the pain as sharp and localized around the knee, worsening with movement and weight-bearing. She denies any history of trauma, fever, or recent illnesses. Her medical history is significant for asthma and she is currently taking albuterol as needed. Physical examination reveals a swollen, warm right knee with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no erythema or evidence of systemic involvement. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies show a normal white blood cell count and elevated C-reactive protein. An aspiration of the knee joint reveals a cloudy fluid with increased white blood cell count predominantly neutrophils, but no crystals. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

options.A: Start empirical antibiotics for suspected septic arthritis
options.B: Order an MRI of the knee to evaluate for meniscal injury
options.D: Refer to orthopedics for potential surgical intervention
options.E: Check rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies
options.F: Initiate physical therapy for knee rehabilitation

question: A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening, particularly over her fingers, and discoloration of her fingers in response to cold temperatures. She describes that her fingers turn white and blue when she is exposed to cold, and then become red and painful. She also notes occasional joint pain, especially in the morning, along with fatigue. She denies any dry eye or dry mouth symptoms. On physical examination, her vital signs are stable, and there is noticeable thickening of the skin over the fingers. Her fingers appear swollen with mild capillary dilation visible at the fingertips. Laboratory tests reveal elevated antinuclear antibodies (ANA). Which of the following is most likely to be found in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with complaints of progressive joint deformity over the past 3 years. She describes her fingers as being misshapen and having difficulty performing daily tasks such as opening jars. She also experiences morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and worsening hand pain. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals swelling and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints, along with boutonniere and swan-neck deformities in multiple fingers. Laboratory studies show positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An x-ray of the hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint swelling and pain in her fingers and wrists. She notes that the stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for more than an hour. She also reports feeling fatigued and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the last 2 months. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with a three-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. On examination, there is synovitis with tenderness and swelling of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serology is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Considering these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressively worsening deformities of her fingers over the past 3 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and notes that her symptoms improve with activity throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She has been taking lisinopril and atorvastatin regularly. On physical examination, there is notable ulnar deviation of both hands and nodules on the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of her hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents to the clinic with worsening hand and wrist pain over the past six months. She describes morning stiffness lasting approximately two hours, which improves with activity throughout the day. She reports difficulty with tasks requiring fine motor skills, such as buttoning her clothes. Her current medication regimen includes methotrexate and low-dose prednisone. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), along with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies.

Which of the following is most consistent with this patient's condition?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending to lift a light object. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her mid-back, and it worsens with movement. She reports a history of occasional joint pain and morning stiffness in her hands, but no recent trauma or heavy lifting. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoporosis for which she takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the thoracic spine, but no neurological deficits. An X-ray of the thoracic spine shows a vertebral compression fracture. What is the most likely underlying cause of her vertebral fracture?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting her wrists and small joints of the hands bilaterally. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies and occasional migraines. She is a non-smoker and denies alcohol use. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). She tests positive for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. The symptoms are most pronounced in the mornings, lasting over an hour, and involve the wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. She has difficulty with everyday tasks such as turning doorknobs and opening jars. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in these joints, as well as ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers that last for about 2 weeks each time. She also reports occasional genital ulcers. Over the past few months, she has noticed joint pain in her knees and ankles, associated with morning stiffness lasting about an hour. Her past medical history is notable for mild acne treated with topical benzoyl peroxide. She denies any recent travel, new medications, or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. On examination, her vital signs are normal. Oral examination reveals a healing aphthous ulcer on the buccal mucosa. Genital examination shows a small ulcer on the labia majora. There is tenderness and mild swelling of the bilateral knees. An ESR is elevated at 45 mm/hr, and CRP is 3.5 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and a history of joint pain in her knees and ankles over the past three weeks. She reports a recent sore throat that resolved without treatment and denies any fever or night sweats. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. She denies recent travel or new medications. On examination, she has tender, erythematous nodules over the anterior aspects of both lower legs. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated ESR and C-reactive protein but normal rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. A chest x-ray is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 29-year-old woman visits the clinic after experiencing sudden pain and swelling in her right knee following a weekend hiking trip. She recalls twisting her knee awkwardly while descending a steep trail. The pain was immediate and she noticed swelling over the next few hours. She denies any previous history of joint problems or significant medical history. She reports no fever or systemic symptoms. On examination, her right knee is swollen and tender with a limited range of motion. There is no warmth or redness over the joint, and the contralateral knee is unremarkable. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past several months. She describes stiffness in her hands and knees that is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She also reports difficulty performing daily activities due to pain. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands for daily activities. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with several subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Radiographs of her hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of increasing fatigue and joint pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She has no significant past medical history and reports that both her mother and sister have arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 84 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr. A chest radiograph is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of unexplained weight loss, night sweats, and persistent fatigue. She reports feeling generally unwell and has noticed swelling in her neck and armpits. She has a history of hypertension controlled with a thiazide diuretic and is a non-smoker. On examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory results reveal anemia and elevated lactate dehydrogenase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent nasal congestion, post-nasal drip, and cough. She reports fatigue and mild joint pain but denies any morning stiffness. She also notes blood-stained sputum and an unintentional weight loss of 4 kg (8.8 lb) over the past 2 months. She has no significant past medical history but mentions two recent episodes of sinusitis treated with antibiotics. Her blood pressure is 145/90 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals nasal crusting and tenderness over the sinuses. Auscultation of the lungs reveals crackles at the bases. Laboratory studies show elevated creatinine levels and urinalysis reveals hematuria with red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands that started a few weeks ago. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving somewhat as the day progresses. She also reports fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past month. She has no significant past medical history and denies any recent viral illnesses. On examination, she has symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her laboratory tests reveal anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. X-rays of the hands show juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is an expected finding in this patient?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of difficulty walking and a dragging sensation in her right foot for the past month. She mentions that her right foot seems to "slap the ground" when she walks, and she has to lift her leg higher on that side to avoid tripping. Additionally, she reports some joint pain in her hands and occasional fatigue. The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. On examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of her right foot, and reduced sensation over the top of the foot and the lateral aspect of the lower leg. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of recurrent sinus infections, chronic cough, and increasing fatigue. She also reports recent onset of hematuria and mild joint pain. She has a history of allergic rhinitis and takes an over-the-counter antihistamine regularly. On examination, her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 138/90 mmHg, pulse is 84/min, and respirations are 20/min. Auscultation of the lungs reveals bilateral crackles, and urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. The physician notes mild tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine. A chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is most likely to explain this patient’s clinical presentation?

A 35-year-old woman seeks medical attention for progressive fatigue, morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and swelling in multiple joints including both wrists, knees, and several proximal interphalangeal joints of her fingers over the last three months. She reports feeling more tired than usual and struggles with daily activities due to joint pain. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals symmetric joint swelling and tenderness in the small joints of both hands, along with mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory studies indicate normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and a positive rheumatoid factor. A plain radiograph of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands, wrists, and knees that began suddenly 4 days ago. She reports that she recently returned from a trip to the Caribbean. She describes the joint pain as severe and has had difficulty performing daily activities. She also notes mild fever and a rash on her face that resolved spontaneously. She denies any previous joint problems. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the affected joints, but no synovial effusion is noted. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 39-year-old woman visits the clinic due to unexplained weight gain and persistent periorbital edema over the past 2 months. She also reports stiffness and mild aching in her fingers and knees, particularly in the morning. She mentions feeling fatigued and has noticed swelling in her ankles. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies, but she takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears overweight with noticeable periorbital puffiness and mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Joint examination reveals no synovitis, but she exhibits reduced grip strength bilaterally. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, elevated serum creatinine, and low serum albumin levels. Urinalysis reveals proteinuria of 4.2 g/24 hours. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Initiation of diuretics to manage edema
options.E: Renal biopsy to assess underlying kidney pathology

question: A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-month history of stiffness and pain in her right shoulder, which is progressively worsening. She describes difficulty lifting her arm above her head and reaching for objects. The pain is often worse at night and disrupts her sleep. She denies any trauma or overuse. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes, both well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is significant limitation in active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no visible swelling or deformity, but diffuse tenderness is noted around the joint. An X-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Physical therapy focusing on range of motion exercises
options.D: Rheumatoid arthritis workup including serum rheumatoid factor
options.F: Imaging with MRI to evaluate soft tissue structures

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive joint swelling and pain in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. She has no history of trauma, and her family history is unremarkable. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving with activity. She also notes occasional swelling and warmth in her finger joints. Her medical history is significant only for well-controlled hypertension. She is a non-smoker and takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show:

Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm^3
Platelet count: 250,000/mm^3

Serum:
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 45 mm/hr
C-reactive protein (CRP): 20 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor: positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP): positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of progressive difficulty in walking and a noticeable foot drop on the left side. She reports tingling sensations in the left foot and occasional sharp pain radiating down the leg. She denies any recent trauma or significant medical history but notes some generalized fatigue. Her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the left foot. Reflexes are 2+ bilaterally in the knees but diminished at the left ankle. MRI of the lumbar spine shows no significant abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty walking due to an inability to lift her right foot. She reports no recent trauma but mentions some intermittent low back pain and occasional tingling in the right leg. She denies fever, weight loss, or changes in bowel or bladder habits. Her past medical history is significant for irritable bowel syndrome. She takes a daily multivitamin and denies alcohol or drug use but smokes a pack of cigarettes per week. On physical examination, the patient walks with a high-stepping gait. There is weakness of dorsiflexion in the right foot, but the remainder of the neurologic exam is normal. There is no joint swelling or tenderness. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.C: Measurement of serum vitamin B12 levels

question:
A 46-year-old woman is evaluated for progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers and hands over the past 6 months, associated with episodes of fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red upon exposure to cold. She also reports joint pain and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes, fatigue, and occasional heartburn. She denies any new medications or recent infections. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals shiny, thickened skin on the fingers with loss of wrinkles, and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show:
Complete blood count: Normal
ESR 28 mm/hr
ANA Positive
Anti-Scl-70 Positive
Rheumatoid factor Negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour, and tends to improve as the day progresses. The joints affected include her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. She has also noticed some swelling and redness in these areas. She denies fever, skin rashes, or any recent infections. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the mentioned joints with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour, gradually improving as the day progresses. She reports swelling in both her hands and wrists, and occasionally in her feet. She has been taking ibuprofen with partial relief of symptoms. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis, and she is worried about developing the same condition. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with a slight ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of chronic pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour, which improves slightly throughout the day. She also reports swelling of the joints and the presence of subcutaneous nodules near her elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or travel. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with palpable nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis based on these findings?

A 32-year-old woman presents to your clinic with a history of recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy over the past three months. She reports intermittent episodes of fatigue and night sweats, but denies any significant weight loss. She also mentions experiencing joint pain in her fingers and wrists, which subsides with over-the-counter pain medication. Her medical history is significant for migraines and seasonal allergies. She takes no chronic medications and is otherwise healthy. On physical examination, she is febrile with a temperature of 100.8°F (38.2°C). There is noticeable enlargement of cervical and axillary lymph nodes, which are non-tender. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia, elevated ESR, and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic reporting chronic lower back pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past year. She describes the pain as dull and aching, predominantly in the lower back, and notes that it is particularly severe in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief, which provides some benefit. The patient denies any recent trauma, weight loss, or fever. Her family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, and her range of motion is notably limited in spinal flexion. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint swelling in both hands and wrists over the past three months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes difficulty gripping objects. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Autoantibody screening shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with unexplained weight gain over the past 4 months, along with periorbital edema and stiffness in her joints, especially in the morning. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has difficulty concentrating at work. She denies any significant changes in her diet or physical activity. Her medical history is significant for anxiety and migraines, managed with medication. Physical examination reveals puffy eyelids, dry skin, and mild swelling in her hands. Blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, pulse is 72/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman visits her physician due to a 3-month history of progressively worsening pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning but improves over the course of the day. She has no significant past medical history but reports that her mother had similar symptoms in her 30s. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There is no skin rash, nodules, or signs of vasculitis. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3, Platelet count 300,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/h, and C-reactive protein 3.0 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles bilaterally. The pain is associated with morning stiffness lasting for approximately 45 minutes. She reports that the fatigue has been progressively worsening, and she has noted some weight loss. She does not smoke, and her alcohol consumption is minimal. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the wrists and the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with wrist pain after falling on her outstretched hand. She reports landing directly on her left wrist, which is now swollen and painful to move. She has a history of mild osteopenia but otherwise has been in good health. On examination, her left wrist is tender with visible swelling and an apparent deformity. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered and shows a fracture of the distal radius. Her vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 122/78 mmHg, pulse of 76/min, and respirations of 16/min. The patient denies any other joint pain or stiffness and has no family history of autoimmune disease. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Immobilize the wrist with a splint
options.G: Obtain an MRI of the wrist

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her small joints, especially in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves throughout the day with movement. She has also noticed general fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Imaging of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic by her mother due to complaints of joint pain. She had a sore throat two weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Over the past week, she has developed pain and swelling in her knees, elbows, and wrists that seem to move from one joint to another. She also mentions feeling more tired than usual and has noticed a mild fever. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and her immunizations are up to date. On examination, there is tenderness and mild swelling in the left knee and right wrist. A soft systolic murmur is heard at the apex. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 40-year-old woman visits the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain that has persisted for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as symmetrical, particularly affecting the small joints of her hands and wrists. Morning stiffness lasts for over an hour. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, weight loss, or skin rashes. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 88 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling that has been progressively worsening over the past 2 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with movement as the day progresses. She has a history of hypertension and osteoporosis. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with some ulnar deviation of the fingers. There are also rheumatoid nodules over the elbows. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels are elevated. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She has difficulty with daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt and opening jars. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Her temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 120/75 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is positive, and rheumatoid factor (RF) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her big toe. The pain started about a year ago, and since then, she has experienced four such episodes, each resolving after a week. She notes the toe becomes red, warm, and extremely tender during these episodes. She has a history of hypertension and is on hydrochlorothiazide. She consumes alcohol occasionally. On examination, there are nodular deposits around her toe and ear helix. Her basic metabolic panel is normal. Which of the following is most likely to be elevated in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, primarily affecting her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in her daily routine. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with worsening pain and swelling in her hands and wrists over the past 8 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty gripping objects. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for her condition?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressively worsening deformity of her fingers over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists and multiple hand joints. Her medical history is notable for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers and swan neck deformities. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. She is currently taking NSAIDs with partial relief of symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of symmetric swelling and pain in her wrists and hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has also noticed fatigue and low-grade fevers. Her medical history is significant for hypertension. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the past three months. She reports persistent swelling in her neck and armpits and occasional joint pain in her hands. She denies fever, chills, or recent infections. She has a family history of autoimmune conditions and her mother had hypothyroidism. On physical examination, multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, heart rate is 82/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). An excisional biopsy of the cervical lymph node is performed. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities due to pain. She has noticed firm nodules over her elbows. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 63-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness that lasts for about an hour and difficulty using her hands due to pain in her wrists and fingers. She has a history of hypertension and hypothyroidism, managed with lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. On examination, there is visible swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, a positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-month history of joint pain and fatigue. She notes swelling in her wrists and fingers, especially upon waking, which improves as the day progresses. She also reports a rash that worsens with sun exposure and painful oral ulcers that have persisted for weeks. Her medical history is significant for Raynaud's phenomenon. She denies any recent illnesses or travel. Physical examination reveals a photosensitive malar rash, swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints, and oral ulcers. Laboratory studies show anemia and the presence of antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She reports occasional sharp, shooting pains in these areas, primarily at night. She has a history of type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and recent poor glycemic control. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Review of systems reveals occasional joint stiffness and fatigue but no significant joint swelling or redness. On physical examination, she has decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in a stocking-glove distribution. Her reflexes are 1+ in the lower extremities and 2+ in the upper extremities. Basic metabolic panel and complete blood count are within normal limits. Hemoglobin A1c is 9.5%. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of persistent joint pain and swelling. She reports that the pain is most pronounced in the wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints of her hands. The patient describes stiffness in her joints that lasts for over an hour each morning. She has no significant past medical history, and her only medication is an occasional ibuprofen for pain relief. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the wrists and multiple finger joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,000/mm3
Platelet count 340,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 50 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 9 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor Positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide Positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and lumps on her elbows and forearms that have been present for the last 3 months. She reports that the lumps are not painful but have become more noticeable. She also describes generalized aches and stiffness, particularly in the morning, which eases as the day progresses. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she is taking medication. On examination, multiple firm subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her arms. There are no signs of active joint inflammation or deformities. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and liver function tests, but elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman presents to her dermatologist with concerns about a rash and joint pain for the past month. She notes that the rash becomes more pronounced after sun exposure and describes it as red and itchy. Additionally, she reports experiencing pain and swelling in her fingers and knees. She has noticed small, painful ulcers in her mouth over the past two weeks. Her past medical history is unremarkable, except for frequent migraines. On physical examination, there is an erythematous, maculopapular rash over her cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Her fingers appear slightly swollen, with mild tenderness upon palpation. Laboratory studies reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She describes the stiffness as being most severe in the morning, often lasting for 1-2 hours before improving slightly. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are both elevated. X-rays of the hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist with complaints of persistent bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been ongoing for several years. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and notes the presence of small, firm nodules under her skin, particularly on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following laboratory findings is most likely to be present in this patient?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty in making a fist. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour, and affecting her wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands symmetrically. She has noticed some improvement with ibuprofen. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for a family history of autoimmune conditions. On examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, and mild swelling of the wrists. Laboratory findings show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing reveals positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-year history of joint pain. She reports that her pain is primarily in her wrists and fingers, and is associated with stiffness that lasts for more than an hour each morning. She has been experiencing fatigue and unintentional weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for mild hypertension. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and raised anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on these findings?

A 53-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of progressive joint pain and swelling over the past few years. She notes that her hands and wrists are particularly affected, with significant stiffness in the morning that lasts over an hour. She has a history of hypothyroidism, well-managed on levothyroxine. Her family history is significant for an autoimmune disorder in her mother. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Given these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. She reports occasional joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings, which seems to improve as the day progresses. Her past medical history includes hypertension, for which she is on lisinopril, and she has no known allergies. On physical examination, her temperature is 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. She appears tired but is in no acute distress. There is no lymphadenopathy or rash. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are unremarkable. Mild tenderness is noted in the small joints of the hands, but without notable swelling or deformity. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are pending. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient’s condition?

options.C: CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
options.D: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibody testing
options.H: Autoantibody panel including ANA and ANCA

question: A 28-year-old female presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of knee pain and swelling after participating in a local marathon. She reports that she did not notice any specific injury or trauma during the run, but experienced a sharp pain in her left knee shortly after finishing. Her medical history is significant only for seasonal allergies. She denies any fever, chills, or recent illnesses. On physical examination, her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg, heart rate is 72/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. The left knee is swollen with tenderness along the medial joint line, and there is limited range of motion due to pain. Lachman test is negative. Radiographs of the knee show no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Obtain a knee MRI to assess for meniscal tear
options.B: Prescribe non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and rest
options.D: Perform a knee joint aspiration for synovial fluid analysis
options.E: Order a CT scan to evaluate for possible osteochondritis dissecans
options.F: Refer to physical therapy for knee strengthening exercises

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to your clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain and persistent fatigue for the past three months. She notes the pain is most severe in her lower back and hips and describes it as a dull, achy sensation. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, well-controlled on medications. She also mentions occasional joint stiffness in the mornings that resolves within 30 minutes. Blood tests reveal an elevated serum calcium level of 11.2 mg/dL (reference range: 8.5-10.2 mg/dL) and normal renal function. Physical examination shows tenderness on palpation over the lumbar spine and hips. A bone density scan is ordered. What is the most likely cause of the bone pain and elevated calcium levels in this patient?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She reports increasing weakness in her lower extremities and mild lower back pain. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or changes in bowel or bladder function. Her medical history is significant for episodic migraines, and her only medication is an oral contraceptive. She works as a software engineer and has noted increased fatigue, particularly in the mornings. Family history is significant for multiple sclerosis in her mother. On physical examination, her vital signs are normal. Neurological examination reveals decreased strength (4/5) in bilateral hip flexors and knee extensors, with intact sensation and reflexes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
options.C: MRI of the brain and spine
options.G: Referral to a neurologist for further evaluation

question: A 58-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She states the stiffness is worse in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving. She has been experiencing significant fatigue but denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for a past diagnosis of hypertension, managed with amlodipine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs). An X-ray of her hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with persistent joint pain in her hands and wrists that has worsened over the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness in her joints is particularly pronounced in the morning and lasts for about 2 hours before improving somewhat through the day. She also notes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has a family history of autoimmune disease, with her mother having lupus. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg. Physical examination reveals synovitis with tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,800/mm3
Platelet count 290,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies positive
Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful red nodules on her shins and joint pain that has been worsening over the past 2 weeks. She reports the nodules appeared suddenly and are tender to touch. Her joint pain is mostly in her knees and ankles, making it difficult to walk. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. She denies any alcohol or tobacco use. On examination, she is afebrile with symmetrical swelling and tenderness in her knees and ankles. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but negative rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. A chest X-ray is ordered. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in her hands and wrists over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has noticed deformities developing in her fingers and experiences difficulty performing daily tasks. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are also elevated. Radiographs of the hands indicate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and recent onset of genital ulcers. She reports that these episodes have been occurring intermittently over the past year. Additionally, she experiences fatigue and has noticed some joint pain and swelling in her knees and wrists over the past few months. There is no history of eye redness or vision changes. Her vital signs are stable, and physical examination reveals multiple aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity and two shallow, painful ulcerations with erythematous borders on the labia. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because of a 2-week history of purplish spots on her legs and occasional blood in her urine. She also complains of intermittent abdominal pain and swelling in her ankles and knees. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 112/68 mm Hg, pulse rate is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent fatigue and bone pain over the past several months. She reports increased thirst and frequent urination. She has a history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes atorvastatin. Her family history is significant for osteoporosis and diabetes mellitus. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness to palpation over the lower ribs and spine. Laboratory studies show:
Calcium 12.1 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.2 mg/dL)
Phosphate 2.3 mg/dL (normal 2.5-4.5 mg/dL)
Alkaline phosphatase 220 U/L (normal 44-147 U/L)
Intact parathyroid hormone 85 pg/mL (normal 10-65 pg/mL)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits the clinic due to progressive stiffness and swelling in her fingers over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and has noticed that her symptoms improve somewhat with activity but worsen with rest. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (anti-CCP) are both positive. X-rays of her hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of neck pain and intermittent tingling in her right arm. She reports that the symptoms began gradually and have worsened over time. The pain is described as a dull ache, and the tingling is most pronounced when she sleeps or when she extends her neck. She has a past medical history of mild hypertension. Family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals tenderness on palpation of the cervical spine and decreased sensation to light touch in the right C6 dermatome. Upper extremity reflexes are 2+ bilaterally. An X-ray of the cervical spine shows mild degenerative changes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Surgical consultation for possible cervical decompression
options.C: Starting gabapentin for neuropathic pain relief
options.E: Physical therapy focusing on neck strengthening exercises
options.G: Cervical MRI to assess for disc herniation

question: A 28-year-old woman visits her primary care clinic due to a 3-week history of red spots on her legs and blood in her urine. She reports having a sore throat about one month ago but has otherwise been healthy. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Upon examination, there are palpable purpuric lesions on her lower extremities, and her joints appear normal. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Her vital signs are stable. A skin biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis, and a urinalysis reveals red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and a red rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports mouth sores and generalized fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable except for intermittent episodes of joint swelling. Physical examination reveals a maculopapular rash over the bridge of the nose and cheeks, sparing the nasolabial folds, and tender, mildly swollen joints in her hands. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with swelling and pain in her wrists and fingers that began 3 months ago. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities due to joint pain. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressively worsening joint pain and swelling. She reports that the swelling is most prominent in her hands and wrists, and the stiffness is worse in the morning, lasting about an hour. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteopenia, for which she takes hydrochlorothiazide and calcium supplements. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein level of 20 mg/L. She tests positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue and multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her arms. She also describes diffuse joint pain and stiffness in the morning lasting less than 30 minutes. She denies any fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with medication. On examination, there are several firm, non-tender nodules over the elbows and forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive joint pain and stiffness in her hands, wrists, and knees for the last 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour, and improves gradually as the day progresses. She denies any significant past medical history but mentions that her mother had 'arthritis' issues in her late 40s. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serology is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist complaining of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness over the last six months. She describes the stiffness as more pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before easing. She notes swelling in her wrists and fingers, and on examination, the joints exhibit tenderness and swelling with reduced range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), along with positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and intermittent low-grade fever. She reports aching pain and stiffness in her fingers, wrists, and knees, particularly in the morning, which eases with activity. She also notes the recent appearance of painless subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and elbows. She denies any significant weight loss, gastrointestinal symptoms, or skin rashes. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, there are several firm, non-tender nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms and elbows. Her joints are tender with reduced range of motion, but there is no significant swelling. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3, Platelet count 230,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 54 mm/hour. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-CCP antibody is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old female presents to the clinic with a three-month history of joint pain and swelling. She has noticed morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks. Her mother has a history of arthritis, but the patient has no other significant past medical history. On examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with tenderness bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detected. X-rays of the hands show marginal erosions and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing tasks that require dexterity. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with the presence of subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, currently well-managed with levothyroxine. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling affecting her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning, lasting for over an hour. She also notes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show:
Rheumatoid factor (RF): Positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP): Positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 55 mm/h
C-reactive protein (CRP): Elevated
A radiograph of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions.
