Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening fatigue and joint pain. She describes the joint pain as symmetric, involving her wrists, knees, and ankles, and reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has noticed swelling in the affected joints. She denies fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness in the wrists and knees with limited range of motion. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin (Hb) 11.5 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 90 fL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 50 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past 4 months. She reports that the sensation is worse at night and has been gradually worsening. In addition, she has experienced some fatigue and occasional joint discomfort, but she is mostly concerned about her inability to feel her fingers properly. Her medical history is notable for obesity and hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a glove and stocking distribution. Reflexes are reduced in the ankles. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,000/mm3
Platelet count 210,000/mm3
Blood glucose 89 mg/dL
ESR 22 mm/hr
A1c 5.7%
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a complaint of joint pain that has migrated from her knees to her ankles and wrists over the past two weeks. She reports having a sore throat a few weeks ago but did not seek medical attention at that time. She has been experiencing fatigue and low-grade fevers. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness of the affected joints. Her throat appears clear at this time, and her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory investigations show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. An anti-streptolysin O titer is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to your clinic with a five-year history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness that lasts over an hour and reports difficulty with daily activities such as buttoning her shirt. She mentions that her mother had similar joint problems in her later years. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, primarily affecting her wrists and the small joints of her hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies (anti-CCP) are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past year. She reports difficulty swallowing dry foods and frequent use of artificial tears. Recently, she has noticed swelling around her jaw, which has been persistent and mildly tender. She denies joint pain or morning stiffness. Medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, she has bilateral parotid gland enlargement and dry mucous membranes. Laboratory tests reveal positive ANA and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is positive, but anti-SSA and anti-SSB antibodies are notably positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint swelling and morning stiffness that lasts for over an hour. She reports symmetrical swelling of her wrists and small joints of her hands. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A radiograph of her hands shows periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving slightly as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma or injury. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with mild ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purplish spots on her lower legs and blood in her urine. She reports mild joint pain in her knees and ankles but denies any significant health issues in the past. She has no history of recent infections, travel, or new medications. Her family history is unremarkable. On examination, there are palpable purpura on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 7,800/mm3, hemoglobin level of 12.4 g/dL, and platelet count of 220,000/mm3. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive joint deformity over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her fingers and wrists. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism and she is on levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is ulnar deviation of the fingers, swelling of the wrists, and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is also elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 3 weeks of joint pain involving her wrists, knees, and ankles. She recently returned from a trip to Southeast Asia, where she spent time in rural areas. She notes associated fever, fatigue, and a rash that started on her trunk and has spread to her limbs. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals multiple joints with mild swelling and tenderness. A maculopapular rash is noted on her arms and legs. Laboratory studies are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of diffuse bone pain and easy fatigue. She reports muscle weakness and frequent headaches. She also mentions that her appetite has decreased and she has lost 4.5 kg (10 lbs) in the past two months without trying. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, for which she takes amlodipine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her family history is significant for her mother having had osteoporosis. Physical examination shows tenderness over the ribs and sternum. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL, phosphorus level of 2.2 mg/dL, and PTH level is elevated at 130 pg/mL. There is no evidence of osteolytic lesions on a plain radiograph of the long bones. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 4 months. She notes that her hands, wrists, and knees are affected and the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for about an hour before gradually improving throughout the day. She denies any rashes, recent infections, or trauma. Her family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits the clinic due to tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past 6 months. She reports a burning sensation that worsens at night and occasional muscle cramps. She has been feeling unusually tired and has had difficulty concentrating. She has a history of hypothyroidism, which is managed with levothyroxine. She denies any recent weight changes, fever, or joint pain. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, she is found to have decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in her lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin concentration of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 102 fL, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain and fatigue over the past two months. She reports difficulty walking due to pain in her hips and lower back. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She has a 20-pack-year smoking history but quit 10 years ago. She drinks alcohol occasionally. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 12.5 mg/dL (normal: 8.6-10.2 mg/dL), serum phosphate level of 2.2 mg/dL (normal: 2.5-4.5 mg/dL), and parathyroid hormone level of 89 pg/mL (normal: 10-65 pg/mL). A bone densitometry scan is pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Start oral calcium and vitamin D supplements

question: A 42-year-old woman arrives at the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for about an hour before gradually improving. She describes the pain as affecting both her hands and wrists symmetrically. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her family history is notable for her mother having 'some kind of arthritis' in her later years. On examination, she has tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated at 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with complaints of acute pain and swelling in her wrists and ankles. She states that the symptoms began abruptly 3 days ago while she was visiting her sister in Puerto Rico. She denies any significant past medical history but reports feeling feverish and experiencing fatigue. Her travel history includes recent visits to several tropical countries. On examination, her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 92/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. There is notable bilateral swelling and tenderness over the wrists and ankles, with limited range of motion due to pain. Laboratory results show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of joint pain. She describes stiffness lasting over an hour each morning and swelling in her hands and wrists. She has difficulty opening jars and performing other daily activities. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, she has synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show:

Serum:
RF: 94 IU/mL (Normal <14 IU/mL)
Anti-CCP: Positive
ESR: 48 mm/hr
CRP: 32 mg/L
ANA: Negative

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of burning sensations and numbness in her feet, which have gradually worsened. She also reports occasional tingling in her hands. The symptoms are exacerbated by walking and relieved somewhat by rest. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin. She denies alcohol use and has no history of trauma. She reports occasional joint pain in her wrists and fingers, which she attributes to aging. On physical examination, there is reduced pinprick sensation in a stocking-glove distribution, but no findings of active synovitis or joint deformities. Her laboratory results reveal a normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel, with a hemoglobin A1c level of 7.4%. Anti-nuclear antibody and rheumatoid factor tests are negative. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers over the past year. She has also experienced intermittent episodes of joint pain in her knees and ankles, which are more pronounced in the morning. She reports fatigue and malaise. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow, round oral ulcers and a few healed lesions on her genital area. There is mild tenderness over her knees, but no swelling or visible deformity. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Antinuclear antibody test is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of neck pain radiating to her right arm, accompanied by tingling and numbness in her thumb and index finger. The symptoms worsen with head movements and improve with rest. She has a history of osteoarthritis and hypertension. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and diminished biceps reflex on the right side. Sensory examination shows reduced sensation over the lateral aspect of the right forearm and thumb. MRI of the cervical spine reveals mild degenerative changes and a right-sided C6-C7 disc herniation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a complaint of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past six months. She describes the stiffness as particularly severe in the morning, lasting for several hours before improving as the day progresses. She also notes fatigue and a weight loss of 5 pounds over the last two months. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no medications except for occasional ibuprofen for pain relief. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 12,000/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 70 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 45 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody are both positive. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in her wrists and hands for the past 5 weeks. She reports that the joints are stiffest in the morning, making it difficult to grasp objects, but the stiffness tends to improve as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. She has no significant family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is erythema and swelling over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that have been progressively worsening over the last 6 months. She reports that the pain is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before it begins to improve as she moves around. She works as a librarian and finds it increasingly difficult to sit for prolonged periods. She denies any recent injury, fever, or weight loss. She occasionally experiences pain in her hips and shoulders. She has a family history of inflammatory arthritis. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals limited mobility of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most appropriate first step in management to address her spine symptoms?

options.A: Physical therapy focusing on back exercises

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening fatigue and joint pain over the past 3 months. She reports feeling extremely tired throughout the day and has noticed bruising more easily than before. Additionally, she experiences pain and swelling in both her wrists and fingers. She denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals pale mucous membranes, petechiae on her extremities, and tender joints in the hands and wrists. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,800/mm³, and platelets of 70,000/mm³. Additional tests indicate elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal liver function tests. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

options.G: Transfusion of packed red blood cells

question: A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent swelling in her neck and underarms for the past 3 months. She reports feeling fatigued and has experienced unintentional weight loss of 7 kg (15 lb) over this period. She also mentions night sweats and occasional low-grade fevers. She denies any recent travel, exposure to sick contacts, or significant joint pain. She has a history of hypertension and is on lisinopril. Physical examination reveals multiple enlarged, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin concentration of 11.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 8,500/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 78 mm/h. Chest X-ray shows no acute abnormalities. An excisional lymph node biopsy is performed. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and a productive cough. She reports blood-streaked sputum occasionally and has noticed increased shortness of breath over the past few weeks. She also mentions fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lbs) over the last 3 months. Her medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, she appears pale and has tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory studies show an elevated creatinine level of 2.3 mg/dL, a decreased hemoglobin level of 9 g/dL, and elevated inflammatory markers. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. A chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of worsening lower back pain over the past 9 months. She describes the pain as dull and persistent, exacerbated by rest and improving with physical activity. She denies any history of trauma or injury. The patient also reports occasional morning stiffness lasting about an hour and difficulty bending forward. She has noticed some fatigue but denies weight loss, fever, or recent infections. Her family history is significant for her brother, who has rheumatoid arthritis. Vital signs are normal, and examination shows limited lumbar spine flexion. Laboratory tests reveal: 

- Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL
- Leukocytes: 8,500/mm3
- ESR: 45 mm/hr
- CRP: 1.5 mg/dL
- HLA-B27: Positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and symmetrical swelling of her hands and feet. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. On examination, she has tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 87 fL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h, and normal thyroid function tests. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain that has moved from her knees to her wrists over the past week. She notes that the joint pain began shortly after recovering from a sore throat about two weeks ago. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. Her temperature is 99.5°F, heart rate is 90/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals tender and swollen joints in her wrists and knees. A throat culture taken today is negative for streptococcal infection. An echocardiogram is ordered, which shows no valvular abnormalities. Laboratory tests show a leukocyte count of 11,500/mm³ with a neutrophil predominance and elevated C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past 3 months. She describes stiffness in the morning lasting more than an hour, which improves throughout the day. She reports that her symptoms initially started in the small joints of her hands and have now progressed to involve her wrists and knees symmetrically. She has no history of trauma, fever, or recent infections. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as mild effusion in both knees. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Leukocytes 7,500/mm3
Platelets 280,000/mm3
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 45 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor Positive
Anti-CCP Positive
ANA Negative
Anti-dsDNA Negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism and migraines. She takes levothyroxine and sumatriptan as needed. She denies any recent trauma or changes in her health status. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints as well as the wrists. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated on the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain and stiffness, primarily affecting her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. The patient has difficulty opening jars and buttoning shirts. She has noted swelling around her knuckles and occasional low-grade fevers. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal:
Rheumatoid factor: positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies: positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 55 mm/h
C-reactive protein (CRP): 12 mg/L
Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and erosions at the proximal interphalangeal joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of worsening pain and swelling in her hands over the past year. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before she can carry out her daily activities. Her medical history is significant for several episodes of fatigue and mild fever. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated inflammatory markers, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She notes that her symptoms are most pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving slightly with activity. She describes swelling in her hands, particularly affecting her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness over the aforementioned joints with decreased range of motion. Laboratory studies reveal the following:

Complete blood count:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocytes 8,900/mm3
Platelets 320,000/mm3

Serum:
Rheumatoid factor Positive
Anti-CCP antibody Positive
ESR 55 mm/hr
CRP 3.5 mg/dL

What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of progressive swelling and deformity of her fingers. She reports stiffness in her hands lasting more than 1 hour each morning, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is notable for treated hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. Physical examination shows ulnar deviation of the fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints and prominent nodules on the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal positive rheumatoid factor and high titers of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with concerns of fatigue and joint pain that have been ongoing for the past 6 months. She reports feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and notes that her symptoms are progressively worsening. She also states that her joints, particularly her knees and wrists, have been painful and sometimes swollen. Upon further questioning, she mentions occasional episodes of easy bruising and a noticeable increase in the frequency of minor infections, such as colds. Her past medical history is significant for iron deficiency anemia, which was treated successfully 2 years ago. She is not on any regular medications other than a daily multivitamin. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies show pancytopenia with low hemoglobin, low white blood cells, and low platelets. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and states that her fingers feel swollen and difficult to move, particularly in the morning. Her medical history includes hypertension, well-controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to pain and swelling in her joints for the past 3 weeks. The discomfort started in her knees and has since spread to her wrists and ankles. Her mother mentions that her daughter had a sore throat about a month ago, which resolved without treatment. The patient denies recent fever, fatigue, or skin rashes. She has no significant past medical history and is up to date on her vaccinations. Her family history is unremarkable for autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the knees, wrists, and ankles, without erythema or warmth. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to persistent dry eyes and dry mouth that have worsened over the past six months. She also reports mild fatigue and occasional joint discomfort, particularly in her knees and elbows, but denies any significant morning stiffness. On examination, there is noticeable bilateral parotid gland enlargement, and her oral mucosa appears dry. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, well-managed with levothyroxine. The physician suspects an autoimmune condition and orders laboratory tests. Which of the following would be the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the suspected condition?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with persistent cough and dyspnea over the past 3 months. She also reports frequent episodes of nasal congestion and sinus pressure, along with occasional hemoptysis. Her past medical history is significant for recurrent sinusitis and one episode of renal colic last year. Current medications include an over-the-counter decongestant and an albuterol inhaler prescribed for presumed asthma. Physical examination reveals bilateral inspiratory crackles on lung auscultation and mild tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Laboratory studies are notable for elevated serum creatinine at 2.1 mg/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Upper respiratory tract biopsy reveals necrotizing granulomatous inflammation. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with increasing joint pain and deformity over the past 3 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her hands and feet. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swan-neck deformities, and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis given these findings?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists that have persisted for over a year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes swelling around her knuckles. Additionally, she mentions the presence of small, firm nodules beneath the skin on her forearms. She has tried over-the-counter pain relief with minimal effect. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Visible subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of her hands reveals joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of persistent fatigue and joint pain. She reports pain and swelling in her wrists, fingers, and knees. The symptoms are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for the pain, which provides minimal relief. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), and a positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show soft tissue swelling but no erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of joint pain and stiffness that have been ongoing for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is most severe in the morning, lasting for about 1-2 hours before she feels more functional. The symptoms have progressively worsened, particularly affecting her fingers and wrists bilaterally. She has noticed some swelling but denies any recent trauma. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with limited range of motion due to pain. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most important initial treatment for managing her condition?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past 6 months. She also reports feeling unusually fatigued and has noticed some swelling around her neck, particularly near the jawline. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any significant joint swelling or morning stiffness but mentions occasional joint pain in her hands. On examination, notable findings include bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory studies reveal a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal thyroid function tests. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that began two months ago. She describes the pain as significant, particularly in the morning, affecting both her hands and wrists symmetrically. She also reports fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her medical history is notable for a healed wrist fracture, and she denies any family history of autoimmune disorders. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive, and rheumatoid factor (RF) is also elevated. Which of the following autoimmune disorders is most consistent with this patient's clinical presentation?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain and fatigue for the past 3 months. She also mentions increased thirst and frequent urination. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent falls or trauma. Physical examination reveals diffuse bony tenderness, but no significant joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests show a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL (reference range 8.5-10.2 mg/dL), serum phosphorus of 2.4 mg/dL (reference range 2.5-4.5 mg/dL), and an elevated serum parathyroid hormone level. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Repeat serum calcium and phosphorus tests

question: A 38-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a deep ache, particularly severe in the mornings, improving slightly by midday. She also experiences fatigue and has lost interest in jogging, an activity she previously enjoyed. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She has no family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, her hips exhibit decreased range of motion and tenderness on palpation, but there is no visible swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies reveal normal CBC and ESR, with negative rheumatoid factor. Hip X-rays show patchy sclerosis and subchondral cysts. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning her clothes. She has noticed firm, painless nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She denies any skin rashes or recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Firm subcutaneous nodules are noted on the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal anemia of chronic disease, a positive rheumatoid factor, and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein are also elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the last 6 months. She reports that the pain is present in both wrists and hands, and is worse in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving slightly. She has difficulty opening jars and typing on her computer due to the pain. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty making a fist. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old female presents to her neurologist with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in her hands and arms for the past few months. She reports that the symptoms worsen with certain neck movements and are sometimes accompanied by numbness. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, both of which are well-controlled on medication. She denies any history of recent trauma or injury. On physical examination, she has reduced range of motion in her cervical spine and decreased sensation to light touch in her upper extremities, particularly in a glove-like distribution. Reflexes are brisk and there is some muscle weakness noted in her hands. An MRI of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes with disc protrusion at multiple levels. Which of the following would be most associated with her current presentation?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists that began 3 months ago. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. She denies any recent trauma or fever but mentions that her mother had similar joint issues. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of progressive pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour and difficulty with buttoning her clothes. Over the past few months, she has also noticed firm lumps under her skin near her elbows. She has hypertension, which is controlled with lisinopril. Her mother had osteoarthritis, and her sister has psoriatic arthritis. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 126/82 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. There is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with visible subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty lifting her right foot and tripping over her own toes while walking for the past two weeks. She also reports numbness and a tingling sensation along the lateral aspect of her leg and dorsum of her foot. She denies any recent trauma or known history of neurological disorders. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism and a positive ANA test performed two years ago during evaluation for unexplained joint pain and fatigue. Her current medications include levothyroxine. On physical examination, she is unable to dorsiflex her right foot and exhibits steppage gait. The right leg is tender to palpation over the fibular head. Deep tendon reflexes are normal, and there is no muscle wasting. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, normal metabolic panel, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive muscle weakness, particularly affecting her shoulders and hips. She finds it increasingly difficult to climb stairs and lift objects above her head. She also reports generalized fatigue and has experienced a 5 kg weight loss over the past 3 months. She denies any joint pain, fever, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies managed with antihistamines. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. Neurological examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness in the upper and lower limbs with preserved reflexes. A comprehensive metabolic panel is unremarkable, but her creatine kinase is markedly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman reports a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as particularly severe in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour before she is able to move comfortably. She denies any recent injuries or fevers. She works as an office manager and finds it increasingly difficult to type and perform her duties. Her mother had similar symptoms in her hands at a comparable age. On physical examination, there is visible swelling in the metacarpophalangeal joints and tenderness in the wrists bilaterally with mild ulnar deviation. Laboratory results reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. A hand X-ray shows erosions at the joint margins. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning. She notes that the stiffness lasts for over an hour each day. She also reports the presence of firm, subcutaneous nodules over her elbows. She denies any trauma or significant weight loss, but mentions occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There are also subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3, Platelet count 350,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with this patient's findings?

A 35-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance for the past 6 months. She also reports occasional joint pain, but denies any morning stiffness or swelling. Her medical history is significant for postpartum depression following the birth of her second child three years ago, but she is currently not on any medication. She has no significant family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals a slightly enlarged thyroid gland and dry skin on her arms and legs. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 37-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of widespread musculoskeletal pain for the last 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is more intense in the morning and seems to affect her entire body. She reports feeling fatigued and having difficulty sleeping, often waking up feeling unrefreshed. She denies any significant morning stiffness or swelling in the joints. The patient has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. On physical examination, there are tender points in the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and gluteal regions. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, normal ESR, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of bilateral hand and wrist pain that has persisted for the past 8 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which gradually improves throughout the day. She also notes swelling in her finger joints and occasional fatigue. She denies any recent injuries or infections. Her family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following conditions is most likely in this patient?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with concerns about persistent skin lesions and joint pain that have been troubling her for the past year. She describes red, scaly patches on her elbows and knees, which occasionally itch. Over the last month, she has developed pain and swelling in her right knee and left wrist, as well as stiffness in these joints that improves with activity. She denies any morning stiffness that lasts longer than 30 minutes. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, well-managed with lisinopril. Her vital signs are stable. On examination, the skin lesions are erythematous with silvery scales, and both the right knee and left wrist are swollen and tender with no nodules. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past few months. She notes feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and experiences frequent episodes of shortness of breath. Physical examination reveals pallor, mild hepatosplenomegaly, and tenderness in her wrists and knees. Laboratory studies indicate severe pancytopenia, with hemoglobin at 7.5 g/dL, platelet count at 90,000/mm3, and a white blood cell count of 2,500/mm3. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. A peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia and no evidence of hemolysis. The patient denies any recent infections, weight loss, or night sweats. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of joint pain and swelling affecting her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is particularly severe in the morning and subsides somewhat as the day progresses. On examination, there is visible swelling and tenderness over the bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Additionally, she has two small subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surface of her forearms. She denies any recent trauma or history of similar symptoms in the past. Her family medical history is notable for her mother having a 'joint condition.' Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old woman presents to the office with a 3-month history of persistent nasal congestion and postnasal drip. She reports frequent sinus infections requiring antibiotics over the past year and has been experiencing a persistent cough with occasional blood-tinged sputum. Additionally, she has noticed increasing fatigue, unintentional weight loss, and occasional joint pain in her hands. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, and she takes lisinopril. She denies smoking or alcohol use. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and mild edema of the nasal mucosa. Lung examination reveals diffuse crackles bilaterally. Her blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria and red blood cell casts. Serum testing shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive c-ANCA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care office with joint pain and swelling involving both hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports stiffness lasting more than an hour each morning. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, which is well-managed with levothyroxine. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination shows synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with joint pain and a facial rash that she noticed worsening with sun exposure. She reports that the joint stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and takes about an hour to improve. She also mentions episodes of fatigue and mild hair loss over the past few months. Her medical history is unremarkable. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over her cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and tenderness in her wrists bilaterally. Laboratory workup reveals normal blood counts and renal function. Which of the following laboratory tests would be most helpful in confirming the diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, often lasting for several hours before easing as the day progresses. She has a history of hypothyroidism and takes levothyroxine daily. She denies any recent trauma or changes in her activity level. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 6 weeks. She describes the pain as bilateral and more intense in the morning, taking about an hour to improve. She denies any recent infections, trauma, or fever. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of diffuse musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping. She reports that the pain is constant and feels like it is affecting her whole body, including her shoulders, back, and hips. The fatigue is debilitating, making it difficult for her to get through the day. She denies joint swelling or redness and reports that the pain does not worsen in the morning. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness at multiple muscle sites, but no joint deformities are observed. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers, but her antinuclear antibody is positive at a low titer. Rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old woman comes to the clinic with recurrent pain and swelling in her left big toe. She reports several episodes over the past year, each lasting about a week, with the pain being severe enough to wake her at night. She describes the toe as red, hot, and extremely tender to touch during these episodes. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. Physical examination reveals multiple subcutaneous nodules over the elbows and Achilles tendons. Laboratory results show:

Serum:
Uric Acid: 10.5 mg/dL
Na+: 140 mEq/L
Cl-: 102 mEq/L
K+: 4.2 mEq/L
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in her hands and wrists that has been persistent for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which gradually improves as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is tenderness and slight swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm^3
Platelet count: 240,000/mm^3

ESR: 35 mm/h
CRP: 4.5 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-citrullinated protein antibody: Positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of progressive skin tightening of her fingers and hands, accompanied by episodes of color changes in her fingers upon exposure to cold, indicative of Raynaud's phenomenon. She reports her fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red, which is often painful. She also complains of joint pain, particularly in her hands and wrists, and fatigue. She denies any significant weight loss, fevers, or recent infections. On physical examination, there is skin thickening over the fingers, and telangiectasias are noted on the face. Her blood pressure is 138/84 mmHg, and there are no signs of synovitis. Laboratory results show positive antinuclear antibodies with a speckled pattern and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.F: Evaluate for systemic sclerosis through capillaroscopy
options.G: Order a hand X-ray to assess joint damage

question: A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 4-month history of dry mouth and dry eyes. She reports difficulty swallowing dry foods and frequently needs to sip water throughout the day. Her eyes often feel gritty, and she uses artificial tears multiple times daily for relief. Over the past few weeks, she has noticed swelling and discomfort in the area around her jaw, particularly after meals. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Her medical history is notable for treated hypothyroidism and mild osteoarthritis. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely associated condition in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and pain in her hands and feet. She reports significant difficulty performing daily activities due to morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She also notes that her joints feel swollen and warm. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands, as well as similar findings in the metatarsophalangeal joints of both feet. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL (normal 12-16 g/dL) and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (normal <20 mm/hr). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to new-onset hypertension. She reports gradual weight gain, fatigue, and a decreased urine output over the past few months. She also mentions occasional joint stiffness in the morning that resolves within an hour. Her past medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism. Her medications include levothyroxine and she occasionally takes ibuprofen for joint pain. Her family history is significant for diabetes and hypertension. On examination, her blood pressure is 158/92 mm Hg, heart rate is 78/min, and she has mild bilateral lower extremity edema. Laboratory tests reveal a serum creatinine of 1.9 mg/dL (increased from 1.0 mg/dL six months ago), a blood urea nitrogen of 28 mg/dL, and mild proteinuria on urinalysis. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows normal kidney size with no evidence of hydronephrosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of worsening bone pain, primarily affecting her pelvic region and spine. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, currently managed with lisinopril. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, she appears slightly uncomfortable, but her vital signs are stable. There is tenderness on palpation over the lumbar spine and pelvic bones, but no significant joint swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level with normal calcium and phosphate levels. Rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies are negative. A bone scan demonstrates increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Start calcium and vitamin D supplementation
options.B: Order a dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint deformity in her hands. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and worsening pain in her wrists and fingers. Her medical history includes type 1 diabetes mellitus, and her medications include insulin and metformin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with notable subluxation and ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as affecting both her hands and knees symmetrically, particularly in the morning, and lasting for about an hour before she can start her day. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, currently managed with lisinopril. On examination, her hands show swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following conditions is most consistent with this patient's presentation?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of gradually worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past two years. She notes that her fingers have become increasingly deformed and that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, improving as the day progresses. She has a history of hypothyroidism and is on levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals subluxation and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints, as well as swan-neck deformities in several digits. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive joint deformity over the past 5 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and worsening pain in her hands and wrists. Physical examination reveals bilateral ulnar deviation at the MCP joints, swan-neck deformity of several fingers, and tenderness of the wrists. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Laboratory tests are positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past 6 months. She also notes occasional joint pain and swelling in her hands. She reports difficulty swallowing dry foods and a need to drink water frequently throughout the day. On examination, she has bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Her labs show a positive ANA and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports stiffness in her fingers and wrists, particularly in the morning, lasting over an hour. The pain has progressively worsened, and she has difficulty performing daily tasks. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis, and she recalls having occasional low-grade fevers and fatigue. She does not smoke or drink. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory results show high levels of rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain for the past year. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour, and it improves somewhat with activity. She has noticed some small, firm lumps under the skin over her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of both arms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-week history of difficulty walking. She describes a 'slapping' sound with each step and has been tripping over her toes. Additionally, she reports intermittent lower back pain and occasional fatigue. She has no history of trauma. On examination, there is weakness in ankle dorsiflexion and reduced sensation over the dorsum of the right foot. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies, including inflammatory markers, are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Order MRI of the lumbar spine
options.F: Prescribe orthotic support for the foot

question:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and swelling in her fingers. On examination, there is symmetrical synovitis and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over her elbows. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Her rheumatoid factor is positive. X-rays of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. The physician suspects rheumatoid arthritis and considers initiating treatment with methotrexate.

Which of the following findings is most specific for rheumatoid arthritis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past 3 months. She describes a 'pins and needles' sensation that disrupts her sleep. She also reports occasional burning pain in these areas, but denies any weakness or loss of function. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. She admits to fatigue over the past few months but denies fever, weight loss, or significant joint pain. Her diet is primarily vegetarian, and she occasionally consumes alcohol. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in her fingers and toes, but normal strength and reflexes. Laboratory tests show mild normocytic anemia and elevated fasting blood glucose levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as aching and primarily affecting her wrists, hands, and knees bilaterally. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well controlled with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, weight loss, or night sweats. Laboratory findings show a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 85 fL (reference range: 80-100 fL), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), and a positive rheumatoid factor test. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender bilateral wrist and metacarpophalangeal joints. X-rays of her hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressively worsening deformities in her fingers over the past year. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing tasks like opening jars and typing. She denies any history of trauma. Her medical history includes hypertension and seasonal allergies. She is currently taking lisinopril and cetirizine. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, subluxation of the metacarpophalangeal joints, and swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show elevated rheumatoid factor and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain for the past two weeks. She notes that the joint pain coincided with the onset of the nodular rash. She denies any recent trauma or injury. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 18/min. On examination, there are tender, erythematous nodules over her anterior shins. Her knees and ankles are slightly swollen but without significant warmth or erythema. Laboratory workup shows a mild leukocytosis and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 weeks. She describes the joint pain as symmetric, affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles, and notes that the pain is worse in the morning, improving somewhat as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of significant fatigue and joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. She experiences morning stiffness lasting over an hour and reports difficulty in performing daily activities due to pain in her wrists, knees, and small joints of both hands. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent illness or weight loss. Family history is positive for autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. A rheumatoid factor test is also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past 3 weeks. She has a history of a recent viral upper respiratory tract infection, which resolved 2 weeks ago. Since then, she has been experiencing generalized muscle pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, that improves as the day progresses. She denies any joint swelling. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. On examination, her vital signs are normal, and there are no signs of joint inflammation or swelling. Laboratory investigations reveal a normal complete blood count, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.B: Order a rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.C: Reassurance and symptomatic treatment with acetaminophen
options.F: Refer to a rheumatologist for potential rheumatoid arthritis
options.G: Order a full autoimmune panel including ANA and anti-dsDNA

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of persistent pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She notes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning her shirt. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism. She denies any recent trauma or overuse. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with subcutaneous nodules noted over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in her wrists and knees for the past three months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, and she has been taking levothyroxine for the past five years. The patient has no history of trauma or recent infections. On physical examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her serum rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-citrullinated peptide antibody (ACPA) is detected at high levels. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a complaint of left shoulder pain that has progressively worsened over the past 3 months. She describes the pain as a constant ache and notes that it severely limits her ability to raise her arm above her head or reach behind her back. She denies any trauma or injury to the shoulder. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies fever, chills, or weight loss. She reports occasional joint pain in her knees but assumes it's age-related. Physical examination reveals limited active and passive range of motion in the left shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is tenderness over the deltoid region, but no swelling or erythema. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that her symptoms are most severe in the morning, lasting for about an hour before she feels more comfortable. The joints involved are primarily her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints. She also notes that she has become more fatigued over the past few months. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, with her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the aforementioned joints. Laboratory findings reveal elevated inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A lab test comes back positive for rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports that the pain is associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour and improves as the day progresses. She has noticed swelling in her wrists and fingers, and there are small, painless nodules on her forearms. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, well-managed with levothyroxine. On examination, there is tenderness and synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?
