A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty walking and an inability to lift her right foot, resulting in frequent tripping. She reports a gradual onset of weakness in the right leg over the past month. Additionally, she mentions experiencing fatigue and mild joint discomfort in her wrists and knees over the last few weeks. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. On physical examination, there is weakness of right ankle dorsiflexion, decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot, and normal deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Nerve conduction studies are ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, weight gain, and feeling cold for the past 6 months. She also mentions occasional joint pain and morning stiffness in her fingers. Her medical history includes osteoarthritis and seasonal allergies. She notes that her fatigue has progressively worsened, impacting her daily activities. On physical examination, her vital signs are: temperature 36.5°C (97.7°F), blood pressure 135/80 mmHg, pulse 60/min, and respirations 16/min. Her skin appears dry, and there is a slight swelling in her hands. Laboratory tests reveal a TSH level of 8.5 mU/L (normal range 0.5-4.5 mU/L) and free T4 level of 0.7 ng/dL (normal range 0.8-1.8 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been progressively worsening over the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and reports difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her shirt and holding a cup. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, her bilateral wrist and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen, warm, and tender. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. The pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She also reports the presence of firm nodules under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of 3 months of fatigue, morning stiffness, and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness improves after about an hour of activity. She denies any recent infections, fevers, or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Family history is notable for her mother having an autoimmune thyroid disease. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Lab tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/hr and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-year history of worsening bilateral hand and wrist pain. The pain is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has significantly affected her ability to perform daily tasks. On examination, there is synovitis of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She has a history of hypertension controlled with lisinopril. Her laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. A hand X-ray reveals joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 3-month history of progressive shoulder pain and stiffness. She notes difficulty in performing overhead activities and reaching behind her back. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, controlled with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies recent trauma or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. On examination, there is marked limitation in both active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is mild tenderness over the deltoid region, but no swelling or erythema. Initial laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP. An X-ray of the shoulder demonstrates no acute bony abnormalities.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a six-month history of painful swelling in her fingers and wrists. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting approximately two hours. She has been feeling fatigued and has noticed difficulty opening jars or holding a pen. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. The wrist joints are also tender and swollen on palpation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detectable. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions in the carpal bones. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that her symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past year. She experiences morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and difficulty in using her hands in the morning. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not smoke or consume alcohol. Examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory results show a normocytic anemia with hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 56 mm/hr, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Given these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to increasing stiffness and deformity in her hands over the past 8 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning her shirt. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before it improves with activity. She has no known allergies and her medical history is significant only for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal:

Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (CCP) antibodies: Elevated
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 55 mm/h
C-reactive protein (CRP): 22 mg/L

X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital with a 3-week history of fever, night sweats, and fatigue. She also reports experiencing generalized muscle aches and a loss of appetite. On examination, her temperature is 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 94/min, and respirations are 16/min. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), and mild anemia. Blood cultures are negative, and a chest X-ray is unremarkable. Autoimmune serology reveals a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Perform a positron emission tomography (PET) scan
options.F: Consider rheumatoid arthritis as a diagnosis
options.G: Prescribe a trial of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 1-year history of worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that her symptoms are particularly severe in the morning, lasting over an hour before subsiding as the day progresses. She has difficulty performing daily tasks such as opening jars and typing. Her family history is notable for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is most consistent with this patient's diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening pain in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as dull and reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has noticed swelling in her finger joints and small, firm nodules under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history includes a family history of autoimmune diseases, and she reports being a smoker for 20 years. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with worsening muscle weakness over the past 6 months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She has also noticed a rash on her eyelids and some joint pain but denies any significant morning stiffness. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism controlled with levothyroxine and hypertension managed with lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, her proximal muscle strength is reduced, and there is a violaceous rash over the eyelids known as heliotrope rash. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels and positive anti-Jo-1 antibodies. Which of the following is most likely associated with her condition?

A 50-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of progressively worsening pain in both hips. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is more pronounced in the mornings and after prolonged periods of inactivity. She denies any trauma, but reports occasional stiffness in her hands and knees. She also notes feeling fatigued more often than usual. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any significant family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is tenderness on palpation of the bilateral hip joints, and limited range of motion is noted. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). X-rays of the hips show joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 3 months of progressively worsening joint swelling and stiffness, particularly in the morning. She notes that the stiffness lasts for more than an hour each morning and involves the wrists, MCP, and PIP joints bilaterally. She denies recent infections or new medications. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Family history reveals her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination shows bilateral swelling and tenderness of the wrists and small joints of the hands. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling primarily affecting her wrists, hands, and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 1 hour, which improves throughout the day. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h and C-reactive protein of 15 mg/L. Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are elevated. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling without erosions.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of painful nodules on her elbows and knees. She reports associated fatigue, occasional low-grade fevers, and weight loss of about 5 pounds over the past two months. She denies any joint swelling or significant morning stiffness but notes some discomfort in her wrists and fingers that improves throughout the day. Her medical history is notable only for a recent upper respiratory infection which resolved without antibiotics. She works as an administrative assistant and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, firm, non-tender nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knees. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal kidney and liver function tests. A skin biopsy of one of the nodules is performed. Which of the following is most likely associated with this patient's current presentation?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that radiates to her buttocks. She notes that the pain is worse in the morning, improves with activity, and does not resolve with rest. She also reports stiffness lasting for over an hour each morning. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and reduced anterior flexion of the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Radiographs of the pelvis reveal sclerosis of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Refer for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with diffuse body aches and fatigue lasting for the past three weeks. She reports that the symptoms began shortly after recovering from a viral illness characterized by fever, headache, and sore throat. She denies any significant joint swelling or morning stiffness but mentions feeling generally unwell and having difficulty concentrating. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On physical examination, she appears tired but in no acute distress, with diffuse tenderness to palpation of her muscles but no synovitis noted. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain and difficulty with mobility over the last few months. She is postmenopausal and denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. She has a history of hypertension controlled with medication. On examination, her vital signs are stable, and notable findings include tenderness over the pelvic girdle and proximal femur. Laboratory studies show: Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL, Phosphorus: 3.5 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase: 180 U/L (normal 44-147 U/L). Serum protein electrophoresis is normal. A bone scan reveals increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old pregnant woman, currently at 28 weeks of gestation, comes to the physician because of lower back and pelvic pain that has progressively worsened over the past two months. She describes the pain as a deep aching sensation that radiates to the buttocks and thighs, making it difficult to stand or walk for prolonged periods. The pain is most severe after physical activity and at the end of the day. She denies any fever, weight loss, morning stiffness, or joint swelling. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the hips due to pain. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of persistent fever, night sweats, and an unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lbs). She reports generalized fatigue and occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees. She denies any recent travel or exposure to sick contacts. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F), blood pressure of 120/80 mm Hg, pulse of 90/min, and respirations of 16/min. There is no lymphadenopathy or skin rashes. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 9,000/mm3
Platelets: 230,000/mm3
ESR: 80 mm/hr
CRP: 15 mg/L

Blood cultures are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness that is particularly severe in the mornings and lasts for about an hour. She describes swelling and tenderness in her wrists and the small joints of her hands. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and she is currently taking lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, rashes, or systemic symptoms such as fevers. On examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory studies show mild anemia, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain affecting both hands and knees for the past 4 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She has no history of recent infections or travel. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL, a normocytic anemia, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female presents to her primary care clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour that gradually improves as the day progresses. She reports fatigue and has noted some weight loss despite no changes in diet or exercise. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands are swollen and tender. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serum analysis is positive for anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs) and rheumatoid factor. Which of the following indicates the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. She also reports occasional swelling of her fingers and the appearance of small, firm nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She denies any recent infections, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. She notes that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before gradually improving throughout the day. The pain is primarily in her wrists, hands, and knees, and she reports difficulty in performing daily activities such as opening jars and buttoning her clothes. She denies any recent infections, fever, or significant weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is a non-smoker. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old female presents to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling after a dance class. She reports that the pain started suddenly during a jump, and she heard a 'pop' sound. She denies any previous trauma to the knee or history of joint issues. Her medical history is significant for asthma, which is well-controlled with inhaled corticosteroids. On examination, the right knee is swollen and tender, with a limited range of motion. The skin over the knee is intact with no erythema or warmth, but a moderate joint effusion is present. Anterior drawer test is positive. The rest of her joints are unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a two-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and mentions difficulty performing daily tasks due to hand weakness. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable over her elbows. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). A hand X-ray reveals joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening pain in both hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain and swelling. She notes the presence of small nodules under the skin of her elbows. She denies fever, weight loss, or rashes. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Family history reveals that her mother had 'arthritis.' On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with warm, erythematous skin overlying the joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show a rheumatoid factor of 120 IU/mL (normal <15 IU/mL) and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of weakness in her right foot and difficulty walking for the past two weeks. She reports that she has been tripping over her toes and finds it challenging to lift the front part of her foot off the ground. The weakness started gradually and has been worsening. Additionally, she mentions occasional numbness and tingling in her right leg. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism controlled on levothyroxine. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her family history is unremarkable. On examination, her right foot demonstrates a foot drop with decreased dorsiflexion strength, and she has a steppage gait. The right ankle reflex is diminished. There are no signs of joint swelling or deformities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral hand swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour for the past three months. She mentions that the stiffness improves as the day progresses but is accompanied by occasional low-grade fevers and fatigue. She denies any recent trauma or overuse of her hands. On physical examination, there is swelling, tenderness, and warmth over the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Hand X-rays show marginal erosions and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to chronic pain in her hands and wrists that has persisted for the past 8 months. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour, which gradually improves as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with the presence of subcutaneous nodules over the elbows. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is most likely to support the diagnosis of her condition?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that worsens with prolonged sitting and improves with physical activity. She also reports morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss but mentions occasional fatigue. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications. On examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally, and there is limited range of motion in her lumbar spine. The Schober test is positive. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 6-month history of lower back pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also reports occasional stiffness in her hips and difficulty standing for prolonged periods. The patient denies any significant trauma and has no history of recent infections. She has noticed increased fatigue but denies other joint pain or swelling. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A pelvic radiograph shows changes consistent with sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue, morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and joint pain involving her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and knees. She reports difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars and buttoning shirts. She has a family history of autoimmune disease, specifically her mother with lupus. Her vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in her wrists and MCP joints bilaterally, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 88 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 42 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with generalized bone pain and fatigue for the past 2 months. She has no significant past medical history, and her family history is unremarkable. She reports a recent decrease in appetite and has lost about 10 pounds over the same period. She denies fever, night sweats, or recent travel. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal: serum calcium 11.8 mg/dL (reference range: 8.5-10.2 mg/dL), phosphorus 2.5 mg/dL (reference range: 2.5-4.5 mg/dL), and alkaline phosphatase 120 U/L (reference range: 30-120 U/L). A bone scan demonstrates diffuse uptake. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and painful swelling in her wrists, knees, and fingers. She notes significant fatigue and difficulty performing daily tasks. She has no significant past medical history. Her family history is notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. She denies any recent infections or changes in her medications. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as both wrists and knees, with warmth and erythema. Laboratory results show: ESR 48 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), CRP 12 mg/L (reference range: <10 mg/L), and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Rheumatoid factor is also elevated. X-rays of the hands and wrists show early periarticular osteopenia but no erosions.

Given the clinical findings and laboratory results, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 6-month history of persistent fatigue and intermittent joint pain. She reports a purplish, net-like rash on her legs that worsens with cold exposure. She has had two miscarriages in the past year and recently experienced swelling and pain in her left calf. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on both lower extremities and mild tenderness in the left calf. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 124/78 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 7,200/mm^3, Platelet count 240,000/mm^3. Serum electrolytes are within normal limits. A Doppler ultrasound of the left leg confirms a deep vein thrombosis. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of nasal congestion, postnasal drip, and a persistent cough. She also reports fatigue and shortness of breath on exertion. She has experienced episodes of hemoptysis and notes intermittent discomfort in her joints. Her medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections over the past year. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, she appears fatigued. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 94/min, and blood pressure is 132/78 mm Hg. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 9,200/mm3
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL
Urinalysis: Red blood cell casts, 2+ proteinuria
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of bilateral hand and wrist pain for the past 18 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour before improving throughout the day. She has no history of trauma. She also reports the presence of firm nodules under her skin near her elbows. Her medical history is significant for Hashimoto's thyroiditis. She is currently taking levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over both forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that her hands and feet are swollen, and the pain is worse in the morning, lasting for over an hour. She has no significant past medical history, but her mother had a similar condition. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), blood pressure is 122/80 mm Hg, heart rate is 76/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to fatigue and joint pain. She reports that for the last six months, she has had pain and stiffness in both her wrists and hands, which is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. She takes ibuprofen occasionally, which provides partial relief. She has no significant past medical history and does not take any regular medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. No skin rashes or lymphadenopathy are noted. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 35%
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm^3
Platelet count: 250,000/mm^3

Serum:
Na+: 139 mEq/L
Cl-: 103 mEq/L
K+: 4.4 mEq/L
HCO3-: 22 mEq/L
ESR: 50 mm/hr
CRP: Elevated

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent fatigue and joint pain. She reports stiffness in her hands and knees that is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She denies fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is non-contributory. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. She has a mild ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 72 mm/h. A test for rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with worsening bone pain over the past several months, particularly in her hips and lower back. She is postmenopausal and has a history of osteoporosis for which she takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. She denies any recent trauma, but notes increased fatigue and some difficulty with walking long distances due to the pain. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine and hips. Her lab results show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, but normal serum calcium and phosphate levels. A bone scan shows increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that has progressively worsened. She describes the pain as dull and aching, localized to the lower lumbar region, and reports morning stiffness lasting for about an hour. The stiffness improves with exercise but worsens with rest. She denies any trauma or injury. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, both well-controlled. She does not smoke and occasionally drinks alcohol. On examination, she has limited spinal mobility with tenderness over the lower lumbar vertebrae. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg, pulse is 78/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 40 mm/hr. A pelvic x-ray shows sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old pregnant woman in her third trimester presents to the obstetrics clinic with lower back and pelvic pain that has been gradually worsening over the past two months. She reports that the pain is particularly noticeable when standing for long periods or when getting up from a seated position. She denies any trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her medical history is notable for gestational diabetes, which is well-controlled with diet. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Her vital signs are within normal limits, and there is no evidence of edema or erythema in the joints. Laboratory investigations show a normal complete blood count and elevated alkaline phosphatase, which is expected in pregnancy. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She notes that the stiffness is worst in the morning and lasts for about 1 to 2 hours before improving somewhat. She denies any recent trauma or overuse injuries. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is also elevated. Hand X-rays show early erosions and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of worsening fatigue and joint pain over the past six months. She describes the pain as affecting both hands and wrists, and it tends to improve as the day progresses. She also notes stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, normocytic red blood cells, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 3-month history of scaly skin lesions on her elbows and knees. She also reports joint pain in her fingers and knees, which is asymmetric and fluctuates in intensity. She has noticed morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes but denies any fever or significant weight loss. Her past medical history is notable for mild depression, for which she takes sertraline. She denies smoking or alcohol use. Family history is significant for psoriasis in her father. On examination, there are well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on extensor surfaces. Swelling is noted in several distal interphalangeal joints of the hands. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient's condition?

A 30-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers for the past 6 months. She also reports episodes of joint pain and swelling in her knees and ankles, as well as occasional fatigue. She denies any fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and erythematous lesions on the genital area. There is mild synovitis in her knees. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers but are otherwise unremarkable. The patient has no significant past medical history, and she takes no medications regularly. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain for the last 6 months. She describes the joint pain as involving both hands and wrists, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She denies recent weight loss or night sweats. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 6,200/mm3, platelet count of 340,000/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/h, and a positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 weeks. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. She also notes difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent travel. Her medical history is significant for asthma, managed with inhalers. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the office with a 3-month history of fatigue, low-grade fever, and weight loss. She has no significant past medical history. She reports persistent joint pain, particularly in her wrists and knees, but denies any morning stiffness. On examination, she has multiple small, firm subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and forearms. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and normocytic anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 6-week history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and fatigue. She also reports experiencing generalized lymphadenopathy and occasional joint pain. She denies any recent travel, insect bites, or sick contacts. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes, and mild swelling of her right wrist. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, including C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A complete blood count shows mild leukocytosis. A chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

options.D: Conduct a CT scan of the chest and abdomen

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of neck pain and tingling sensations radiating down her right arm. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens with prolonged sitting and when turning her head to the right. She also reports occasional numbness and weakness in her right hand. She denies any recent trauma or prior neck surgeries. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and diminished sensation in the C6 dermatome. Reflexes are intact. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a three-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has noticed generalized aches, particularly in her wrists and knees, without significant swelling. She also mentions occasional nosebleeds and frequent gum bleeding when brushing her teeth. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications besides a daily multivitamin. Vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, she appears pale with petechiae on the lower extremities and mild tenderness in the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, leukocytes 2.5 x 10^3/μL, and platelets 95,000/mm3. A peripheral blood smear shows normocytic, normochromic cells. What is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands for the past 6 weeks. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and also mentions fatigue and low-grade fevers. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, which is well-controlled on levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody tests return positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, mild ankle swelling, and new-onset hypertension. She reports a dull ache in her hands and knees, which she attributes to gardening. Her family history is significant for lupus in her mother and hypertension in her father. Her current medications include lisinopril and a multivitamin. On examination, her blood pressure is 155/95 mm Hg, and she has mild edema in her lower extremities. Laboratory results reveal a serum creatinine level of 2.0 mg/dL, which was previously 1.0 mg/dL six months ago. Urinalysis shows 1+ proteinuria and occasional red blood cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to increasing pain and deformity in her hands over the last year. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, her fingers show ulnar deviation and swan-neck deformities. There is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve with activity as the day progresses. She also notes some fatigue and low-grade fevers. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is unremarkable. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,200/mm3
Platelet count 310,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/h
C-reactive protein 3.2 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports the pain is particularly prominent in her wrists and the small joints of her hands, and she experiences stiffness lasting more than an hour each morning. She denies any fevers, weight loss, or recent illness. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies and rheumatoid factor are positive. A radiograph of the hands shows marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of progressive fatigue and joint pain over the last 6 months. She describes the pain as primarily in her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands, noting that it is worst in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She also reports generalized stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On physical exam, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.8 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h, and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. She has difficulty gripping objects and opening jars. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, the patient has tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a high rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of increasing fatigue, weight loss of 15 pounds, and generalized weakness. She also reports occasional mild joint pain without significant morning stiffness. Her medical history is notable for long-term hypertension managed with lisinopril. Vital signs show temperature 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure 138/84 mmHg, pulse 80/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, hematocrit 29%, mean corpuscular volume 75 fL, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Further tests indicate a low serum ferritin level. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, improving as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with visible ulnar deviation of the fingers. Her laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of her hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of 6 months of fatigue and joint pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands. She has a history of hypertension for which she takes lisinopril. She does not use tobacco or alcohol. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 11 g/dL, leukocyte count 5,500/mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 78 mm/h. Rheumatoid factor is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty in performing daily tasks such as buttoning her clothes. She occasionally takes over-the-counter ibuprofen, which provides partial relief. She has also noticed small lumps under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes the sensation as a 'pins and needles' feeling, which is particularly bothersome at night. She also reports occasional burning pain in the affected areas. Her past medical history is significant for a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is managed with metformin. She denies any recent changes in vision or weakness. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a glove and stocking distribution. Deep tendon reflexes are reduced in the ankles. Laboratory findings show:
Hemoglobin A1c 8.2%
Fasting glucose 160 mg/dL
The patient wants to know what might be causing her symptoms. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's presentation?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive joint swelling and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving throughout the day. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-month history of fatigue and aching in multiple joints, particularly in her hands and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with metformin. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, consistent with normocytic anemia, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, morning stiffness, and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities due to joint pain. She denies recent infections or new medications. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL (reference: 12-15 g/dL), mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL (reference: 80-100 fL), and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (reference: 0-20 mm/hr). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. She reports experiencing morning stiffness lasting over an hour and pain in her wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, vital signs are temperature 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure 130/85 mm Hg, heart rate 78/min, and respirations 18/min. She has symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 88 fL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that started 3 months ago. She notes that the pain is worse in the mornings and improves as the day progresses. She reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, she has symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of increasing fatigue and joint pain over the past six months. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes and swelling of her fingers. Her past medical history includes hyperlipidemia and hypothyroidism, for which she takes atorvastatin and levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or changes in bowel habits. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/95 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. There is tenderness and mild swelling at the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory testing shows serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (reference range: 0.6-1.2 mg/dL), blood urea nitrogen of 28 mg/dL (reference range: 7-20 mg/dL), and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 75 mm/hr. Urinalysis shows 2+ proteinuria and microscopic hematuria without casts. Further serologic testing reveals a positive antinuclear antibody titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports pain in her wrists and knuckles that improves with activity as the day progresses. She denies fevers or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with no deformities noted. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) tests are positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purpura on her lower extremities and hematuria. She also reports experiencing abdominal pain and arthralgias in her knees and ankles. The patient denies any fevers, recent infections, or changes in bowel habits. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the legs and mild swelling of the ankles. Laboratory results show elevated serum creatinine and hematuria with mild proteinuria on urinalysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain involving both wrists and fingers. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she has no known allergies. The patient is not on any medications. On physical examination, her hands show swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7,200/mm³, platelet count of 300,000/mm³, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody is elevated. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and symmetric joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressively worsening deformity of her fingers over the past five years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and describes pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles. Her medical history includes long-standing hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any recent infections, weight loss, or fever. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal joints, and nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Hand X-rays reveal joint space narrowing and erosions in the carpal bones. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent swelling in her neck and under her arms for the past 3 months. She also reports experiencing night sweats, unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds, and intermittent fevers. She has a history of episodic joint pain and morning stiffness, which she attributes to stress. Her family history is notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. There is no organomegaly. Her vital signs are: temperature 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 16/min. Laboratory results reveal leukocytosis and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits her physician due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 6 months. She describes weakness and stiffness in her lower extremities, which are worse in the morning and improve slightly with movement. She also reports occasional tingling in her feet. She has experienced intermittent joint pain in her hands and wrists over the past year, as well as fatigue. She denies any recent illness, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Neurological examination reveals decreased strength in the proximal muscles of the lower limbs and brisk deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and slightly elevated inflammatory markers. Further evaluation is likely to show which of the following?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of bilateral hand swelling and stiffness. The stiffness is pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour before improving. She also reports intermittent low-grade fever and fatigue. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory results reveal: 

Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL 
Leukocytes 9,400/mm3 
Platelets 320,000/mm3 
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/h 
C-reactive protein 18 mg/L 
Rheumatoid factor positive 
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody positive 

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain and stiffness, most notably in her wrists and fingers. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has difficulty performing tasks requiring fine motor skills, such as buttoning her shirt. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She denies alcohol or tobacco use. Family history reveals an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past two years. The pain is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving slightly throughout the day. She reports fatigue and occasional fever. Her medical history includes hypertension, treated with lisinopril, and she has had no surgeries. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, with noticeable subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of progressive swelling and pain in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. She has difficulty grasping objects and notes her fingers are becoming more deviated. Family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of fingers, swelling over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and warmth in the wrists. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic pain in her hands and wrists for the past 18 months. She describes the pain as dull and constant, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. She also has firm, non-tender nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril, and no known allergies. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a two-week history of difficulty walking and a sensation of weakness in her right foot. She reports that she often trips when walking, particularly when climbing stairs. Additionally, she notes intermittent numbness and tingling in her right leg. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies recent trauma, fevers, or weight loss. On physical examination, the patient demonstrates decreased dorsiflexion strength in the right foot, leading to a steppage gait. There is also decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick along the lateral aspect of the right lower leg. Reflexes are 2+ bilaterally and symmetric. Laboratory studies reveal normal blood glucose levels and thyroid function tests. Electromyography shows dysfunction of the peroneal nerve. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with concerns of worsening joint pain and stiffness over the past 8 months. She notes that her hands and wrists are particularly affected, with stiffness most pronounced in the morning lasting over an hour. Recently, she has noticed her fingers becoming more deviated to the side and has difficulty gripping objects. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and osteoarthritis. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Ulnar deviation of the fingers is noted. Laboratory findings reveal elevated ESR and CRP, positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-CCP antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following diagnoses is most consistent with this patient's presentation?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the physician because of fatigue and pain in her joints, particularly affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and fatigue. She denies recent infections or trauma. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there are symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/h (reference range: 0-20 mm/h) and a C-reactive protein level of 3.2 mg/dL (reference range: <0.5 mg/dL). Additionally, her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 8 months. She describes the pain as bilateral and affecting her wrists, elbows, and knees, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She also reports a feeling of exhaustion that has not improved with rest. Her medical history is significant for hypertension. On examination, her joints are swollen, tender, and warm to touch. There are no skin rashes, and her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr, and a normal white blood cell count. Which of the following findings would most likely confirm the diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis in this patient?

A 30-year-old woman visits the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and pain in her fingers and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness that lasts for over an hour, which improves throughout the day. She denies any fever, fatigue, or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her wrists and small joints of her hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has no significant past medical history but mentions her mother having arthritis. Her vital signs are stable. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody and rheumatoid factor are both positive. Imaging shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions in the hands. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to increasing fatigue and joint pain over the past 3 months. She reports frequent headaches and a feeling of heaviness in her legs. She experiences morning stiffness lasting about an hour and describes her joint pain as intermittent, particularly affecting the wrists and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On examination, she appears pale and has mild hepatosplenomegaly. Her blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, respiration is 16/min, and her temperature is 36.5°C (97.7°F). Laboratory findings reveal hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, leukocytes of 3,200/mm3, and platelets of 120,000/mm3. ESR is 55 mm/hr and CRP is elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation or management of this patient?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe back pain that started while lifting a heavy box. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back. She has a past medical history of rheumatoid arthritis, for which she takes methotrexate, and osteoporosis, for which she takes alendronate. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 132/84 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the thoracic spine and a slight kyphotic deformity. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory tests show mild normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A spinal X-ray reveals a compression fracture of the T8 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old woman visits the clinic due to a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking and weakness in her legs. She initially noticed fatigue and occasional numbness in her feet, which has since progressed to weakness in climbing stairs and getting up from a seated position. She has occasional hand stiffness in the mornings that resolves within an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, muscle strength is 3/5 in the lower extremities with no apparent muscle atrophy. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the lower limbs. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A nerve conduction study shows slowed conduction velocities in the lower extremities. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to persistent fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as symmetric, primarily affecting her hands and knees, and worse in the mornings. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She has no history of recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and knees. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 65 mm/h. A complete metabolic panel is unremarkable. Which of the following findings would most likely support the suspected diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness that has been progressively worsening over the past 4 months. She notes that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for several hours. The pain primarily affects her wrists, as well as the joints of her fingers and toes. She reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history is unremarkable except for a family history of autoimmune diseases. She is currently taking no medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 40 mm/hour (reference range: 0-20 mm/hour) and elevated C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-year history of gradually worsening pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve with movement throughout the day. She also reports the presence of small lumps under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-year history of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with movement as the day progresses. She also notes fatigue and weight loss over the past several months. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms.
