question: A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that have been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands due to pain and deformity. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Deformities are noted in her fingers, and there is reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 4-month history of nasal congestion, fatigue, and a persistent cough. She reports episodes of sinus pain and nasal discharge that have not improved with over-the-counter decongestants. Additionally, she notes increasing shortness of breath and occasional blood-streaked sputum. She denies fever or recent travel. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and osteoarthritis. Medications include lisinopril and ibuprofen. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, heart rate is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Mild tenderness is noted over the frontal sinuses, and there is a faint wheeze upon lung auscultation. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatinine levels and a normocytic anemia. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the last two months. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has lost 15 pounds despite no change in her diet. She also notes occasional dizziness and shortness of breath on exertion. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any history of gastrointestinal bleeding, but she does have a family history of colon cancer in her father. On examination, she appears pale. Her blood pressure is 130/78 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and her BMI is 22 kg/m². Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 75 fL
Serum iron 30 µg/dL
Total iron-binding capacity 410 µg/dL
Ferritin 8 ng/mL
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.F: Check vitamin B12 and folate levels

question: A 22-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of recurrent fevers and generalized fatigue. She reports intermittent sweating and tender lymph nodes in her neck and under her arms. She denies any recent travel or known infections. On further questioning, she mentions occasional joint pain and stiffness in her hands, particularly in the mornings, but no visible swelling. Her temperature is 100.8°F (38.2°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 18/min. On physical examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. There is no rash, hepatosplenomegaly, or significant joint deformities noted. Laboratory studies reveal leukocytosis with predominant lymphocytes, elevated ESR, and negative antinuclear antibody. What is the most appropriate next step in the diagnostic evaluation of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past year. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for more than an hour, and improves throughout the day with activity. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Several firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. She has a history of smoking 10 cigarettes per day for the past 20 years. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, which is worse in the mornings and improves as the day progresses. She reports stiffness lasting more than an hour each morning. On examination, there is visible swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with multiple subcutaneous nodules palpable over the extensor surfaces of her arms. There is no erythema or warmth in the joints. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions in the carpal joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and swelling. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. A hand X-ray shows juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the mornings and takes over an hour to improve. She has also noticed firm, painless nodules developing on her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, and she is currently taking lisinopril. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, and subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of both elbows. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive fatigue and joint pain. She describes symmetric pain and stiffness in her hands, wrists, and knees, which is worst in the morning and lasts for several hours. She has no significant past medical history. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, synovitis is noted in her metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with sudden onset of severe back pain that she has been experiencing for the last week. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back, worsening with movement and improving slightly with rest. She denies any history of trauma or lifting heavy objects. She has a history of osteoporosis and has been on calcium and vitamin D supplements. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She lives alone and admits to having a diet low in dairy products. On examination, she is in acute distress due to pain. Vital signs are stable. There is tenderness upon palpation of the thoracic spine, but no neurological deficits are noted. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to joint pain that has been migrating between her knees, elbows, and wrists over the past two weeks. She reports a sore throat approximately three weeks ago, but no recent trauma or fever. She denies any family history of joint disorders. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling noted in her right knee and left wrist. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. An antistreptolysin O titer is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain for the past 2 weeks. She describes the nodules as tender and that they appeared abruptly over the last few days. She reports no recent fever, sore throat, or respiratory symptoms. Her medical history is significant for asthma, for which she uses an inhaled corticosteroid. She took a course of oral antibiotics for a suspected urinary tract infection about a month ago. She reports feeling generally tired but is otherwise well. Physical examination reveals erythematous, tender nodules on the anterior aspects of both lower legs. Her knees and ankles are swollen and tender on palpation, with a limited range of motion due to pain. Laboratory findings show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, particularly affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles. She works as a librarian and finds it increasingly difficult to use her hands for prolonged periods. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or weight loss. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 75 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports stiffness and swelling in her hands and wrists, especially in the morning, lasting over an hour. Her medical history is notable for a family history of autoimmune disease. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A hand radiograph shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is most consistent with this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, fever, and night sweats over the past several weeks. She also reports an unintentional weight loss of 7 pounds during this period. She has noted swollen lymph nodes in her neck and under her arms. She denies any recent infections, new medications, or travel. Her medical history is significant for occasional joint pain but no formal diagnosis of arthritis. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, she appears tired and thin. Her vital signs show a temperature of 38.0°C (100.4°F), blood pressure of 120/80 mm Hg, and heart rate of 88/min. There are non-tender, rubbery lymph nodes palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A complete blood count shows mild anemia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.G: CT scan of the chest and abdomen

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of diffuse joint pain, a low-grade fever, and a red, circular rash on her right thigh that has been gradually expanding. She reports that she had been hiking in a wooded area approximately 3 weeks ago and recalls removing a tick from her skin. There is no history of recent travel outside the country. Physical examination reveals mild effusion in the right knee and an erythematous rash with central clearing, measuring approximately 10 cm in diameter on her thigh. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her knees, wrists, and fingers for the past week. She returned from Brazil two weeks ago, where she visited family. She has been experiencing low-grade fevers and fatigue. She denies any rashes, photosensitivity, or oral ulcers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints with mild effusions in both knees. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and normal complete blood count. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 25-year-old woman visits the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain that moves from one joint to another. She describes the pain as mild to moderate and initially started in her knees, then moved to her wrists and elbows, and now affects her ankles. She reports having a sore throat about a month ago but did not seek medical attention at the time. She denies any swelling or morning stiffness in her joints. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness over the affected joints, but no swelling or erythema. Throat examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), and a negative rheumatoid factor test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue, joint stiffness, and pain affecting her hands and wrists symmetrically. She describes morning stiffness lasting for about an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history includes hypertension, and she is currently taking lisinopril. On physical examination, her wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints are tender and swollen. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to joint pain and swelling. She reports a sore throat about two weeks ago that resolved without treatment. Since then, she has been experiencing pain and swelling in her knees, wrists, and ankles, which seem to move from one joint to another. She feels fatigued and has had a low-grade fever. She denies any skin rashes, abdominal pain, or chest pain. On physical examination, her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), and there is swelling and tenderness in the right knee and left wrist. A soft systolic murmur is heard at the apex. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of worsening hand pain and stiffness over the past three years. She mentions that the pain is symmetrical and primarily affects her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has been taking over-the-counter ibuprofen with partial relief. On examination, there are visible deformities in her hands, characterized by ulnar deviation and swan-neck deformities. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given her presentation?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of chronic neck pain radiating to her right arm and intermittent tingling sensations in her fingers for the past 3 months. She denies any recent trauma or injury. The pain is described as a dull ache that worsens with neck movement. She reports occasional headaches and generalized fatigue but no fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for tension headaches and mild osteoarthritis. She works as a secretary and spends long hours at the computer. Physical examination reveals restricted range of motion in the cervical spine and decreased sensation in the right C6 dermatome. Reflexes are symmetrical, and there is no muscle atrophy. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 60-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-year history of progressively worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning. She also reports noticing some firm lumps under her skin at the elbows. She denies fever or weight loss but mentions her mother was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis in her 50s. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in both wrist joints and several proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable at the olecranon processes bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent mottled discolored pattern on her legs resembling a web-like rash, which worsens with cold exposure. She also reports two episodes of deep vein thrombosis over the past year. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. She denies any joint pain, morning stiffness, or Raynaud's phenomenon. Family history is significant for systemic lupus erythematosus in her mother. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities, without any synovitis or joint deformities. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, negative antinuclear antibodies, and a positive lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin changes and discoloration of her fingers for the past 6 months. She describes the skin on her fingers as becoming progressively tighter, and mentions episodes where her fingers turn white and become painful, especially in cold weather. She also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. She denies any recent illness or travel. On examination, the skin over her fingers appears shiny and tight, and there are areas of digital pitting. Laboratory tests reveal a positive ANA. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to fatigue and joint pain that has persisted for 4 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and symmetric swelling in her wrists and fingers. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or travel. On examination, she has tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Laboratory results reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 89 fL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 43-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic for a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, night sweats, and fatigue. She reports generalized malaise and weight loss of 3 kg (6.6 lb) over this period. She has a history of mild osteoarthritis of the knees but denies other joint symptoms. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 38.4°C (101.1°F), pulse 96/min, and blood pressure 120/78 mm Hg. There is mild tenderness over the right costovertebral angle but no significant lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 78 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein level of 30 mg/L. Blood cultures are negative. A chest X-ray shows normal findings. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 58-year-old woman comes to the physician's office complaining of joint pain in both hands and wrists for the past year. She describes the pain as more severe in the morning, lasting for over an hour, and improving slightly as the day progresses. She also reports feeling unusually tired and has noticed small lumps under her skin over the elbows. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show the following:
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
C-reactive protein: Elevated
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide: Positive
ESR: 55 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain primarily affecting her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in these joints. She has difficulty performing tasks such as buttoning her shirt or opening jars. She denies any trauma, fever, or skin rash. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals tenderness, swelling, and limited range of motion in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 63-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent fatigue and diffuse bone pain, particularly in her lower back and legs, for the past two months. She reports occasional joint stiffness, but no significant swelling or redness. Her medical history is remarkable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or weight loss. On examination, her blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and a parathyroid hormone level that is elevated. Which of the following represents the most likely underlying condition causing her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of left shoulder pain that restricts her arm movement. She describes the pain as a deep ache, worsening with overhead activities and at night, disrupting her sleep. She denies any recent trauma or injury. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with metformin. She reports generalized fatigue and occasional morning stiffness lasting around 30 minutes. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the deltoid muscle and a limited range of motion in all directions of the left shoulder. There is no joint swelling observed. Laboratory tests show:

Hemoglobin A1c: 7.1%
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 28 mm/hr
C-reactive protein: 12 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor: negative

X-ray of the shoulder shows no fractures or dislocations, but mild subacromial space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's shoulder pain?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and fatigue. She has a history of mild hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr (normal: 0-20 mm/hr), and a positive anti-citrullinated protein antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of progressive joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports that her symptoms initially started in her hands and have since spread to her wrists and knees. She denies any recent infections, trauma, or changes in medication. Her medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and she is a non-smoker. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as mild swelling in both knees. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr) and a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 7 mg/L (reference range: 0-8 mg/L). She tests positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with increasing difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects over her head, which has been worsening over the past 2 months. She reports experiencing generalized fatigue and occasional joint pain, particularly in her hands and wrists. She denies any history of trauma or significant pain in the affected muscles. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, there is noticeable weakness in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs. There is mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints, but no swelling or deformity is observed. Laboratory tests show an elevated creatine kinase level. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein are mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness and swelling in her fingers and wrists, which improves throughout the day. She reports fatigue and mild fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are strongly positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, weight loss, and painful nodules on her forearms. She reports that the nodules are firm, non-tender, and located on the extensor surfaces. Additionally, she has been experiencing occasional low-grade fevers and joint pain in her wrists and knees. She denies any skin rashes, oral ulcers, or photosensitivity. Family history is significant for autoimmune thyroid disease in her mother. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals multiple subcutaneous nodules over the forearms and mild swelling of the wrists. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. A skin biopsy of the nodules is performed. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old female presents to her primary care clinic with a 3-month history of left shoulder pain. She describes the pain as a constant dull ache that worsens with overhead activities and is accompanied by a noticeable restriction in her shoulder's range of motion, especially when reaching for objects on high shelves or fastening her bra. She denies any recent trauma or injury. The pain sometimes radiates to her upper arm, but she has no numbness or tingling in her hand. Past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, well-controlled with levothyroxine. She reports occasional fatigue but no fever or weight loss. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterior and lateral aspects of the shoulder, and limited abduction and external rotation. Laboratory tests show normal ESR and CRP levels. X-ray of the shoulder shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-day history of acute pain and swelling in her right knee following a vigorous spinning class. She reports that she felt a sharp pain during the workout but continued to exercise. Her medical history is significant for mild asthma, for which she uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. She denies any history of joint disorders or recent travel. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. On examination, the right knee is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation with restricted range of motion. There are no rashes or other joint abnormalities. Laboratory studies show a normal white blood cell count and an elevated C-reactive protein level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour and difficulty in using her hands due to pain in her wrists and fingers. She has no history of trauma. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 85 fL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain for the past four months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which gradually improves throughout the day. She has noted swelling and pain in her wrists, hands, and knees bilaterally. She denies fever, skin rashes, or changes in bowel habits. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and a negative anti-nuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of joint pain, fatigue, and a rash. She notes that the rash appears on her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and it becomes more pronounced after sun exposure. She also reports experiencing painful oral ulcers. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she has mild swelling and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal joints, and a faint, erythematous rash is visible on her face. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past six months. She mentions that she frequently carries eye drops and water to alleviate her symptoms. She also notes occasional swelling and discomfort in her parotid glands. She has experienced fatigue and mild joint pain in her hands, which she attributes to her busy work schedule. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies, and she takes antihistamines as needed. She denies any recent infections or significant weight changes. Her vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Laboratory studies demonstrate elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal complete blood count (CBC). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with skin lesions and joint pain. She reports having reddish, scaly patches on her elbows and knees for the past year and increasing pain in her left wrist and right knee over the last 6 months. The joint pain is asymmetric and does not improve with over-the-counter pain medications. She denies any morning stiffness. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, there are well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales. The affected joints show mild swelling and tenderness without significant deformity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain that began 3 days ago after returning from a hiking trip in the Appalachian Mountains. She describes swelling and stiffness in her knees, wrists, and ankles. She denies any recent trauma or prior history of joint disease. Her family history is non-contributory. She reports feeling fatigued and having low-grade fevers. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), heart rate 90/min, and blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. Musculoskeletal examination reveals synovitis in her knees and wrists with limited range of motion due to pain. Laboratory findings show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.A: Start empiric antibiotics for suspected septic arthritis
options.C: Test for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies
options.E: Perform arthrocentesis to evaluate synovial fluid
options.F: Prescribe a short course of corticosteroids

question: A 65-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 10-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and the presence of firm nodules under the skin of her elbows. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for at least one hour before improving. She has difficulty using her hands in the morning and has been experiencing fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any regular medications. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening neck pain and tingling in her arms. The pain is described as a dull ache, with occasional sharp episodes radiating to her fingers. The tingling worsens when she extends her neck or when she performs computer work for long periods. She notes some difficulty with grip strength but denies any fever, weight loss, or recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension, well-controlled on lisinopril. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to pinprick in the C6-C7 dermatome, with mild weakness in wrist extension. Spurling's maneuver is positive, replicating her symptoms. MRI of the cervical spine shows disc degeneration at C5-C6 with foraminal narrowing. Which of the following conditions is most closely related to her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with concerns of progressive skin tightening over her fingers and hands, as well as intermittent episodes of fingers turning white and blue when exposed to cold. She reports associated joint pain and stiffness, which she initially attributed to aging. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any regular medications. On examination, she has thickened, tight skin over her fingers and hands, and there is evidence of digital pitting scars. Her capillary refill time at the fingers is prolonged. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to a 6-month history of persistent lower back pain. The pain is more pronounced in the morning and tends to improve as the day progresses. She also reports stiffness in her back and occasional pain in her buttocks. She denies any trauma, weight loss, or changes in bowel habits. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is currently not on any medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, and her range of motion in the lumbar spine is reduced. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An X-ray of the pelvis reveals sclerosis of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning, lasting more than an hour, and improve with activity. She also mentions having difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning her shirt. Her family history is significant for a mother with osteoarthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 7,200/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of joint pain in her hands. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting both hands, and it is worse in the morning when she wakes up. She reports stiffness lasting for about an hour each morning. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or rashes. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are also elevated. Radiographs of her hands show marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness that have been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning and improving somewhat as the day goes on. On examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her vitals are stable. She reports no recent trauma or illness and her family history is notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-week history of a rash on her face and joint pain. She mentions that the rash has worsened with sun exposure. Additionally, she has been experiencing fatigue and occasional mouth sores. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals erythematous patches over the cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and mild swelling of the wrists and knees. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody titer and mild anemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to numbness and tingling in her hands and feet for the past 3 months. She reports that the symptoms have gradually worsened and are accompanied by intermittent muscle weakness and fatigue. She denies any significant joint pain, but mentions occasional stiffness in her fingers in the morning that resolves within an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She is not on any medications. On physical examination, there is decreased sensation to pinprick in a glove-and-stocking distribution and mild muscle weakness in the distal extremities. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 13.2 g/dL and a leukocyte count of 7,800/mm3. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 38 mm/h. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to evaluate this patient's condition?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive bone pain, primarily affecting her hips and lower back. She is postmenopausal and reports no history of trauma. Her past medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies alcohol use and smokes occasionally. She notes increased fatigue but attributes it to her busy schedule. On examination, she appears well-nourished and vital signs are within normal limits. Lab tests reveal:

Calcium: 9.6 mg/dL
Phosphate: 3.2 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase: 220 U/L (elevated)
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL

A DEXA scan was performed and shows decreased bone density consistent with osteoporosis. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of joint pain and stiffness in both her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies any recent injuries or changes in activity level. There is no significant family history of arthritis. On examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening joint pain and swelling in her hands and knees over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism. She denies smoking or alcohol use. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most specific marker for her condition?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with pain and swelling in her right wrist after tripping on a sidewalk and falling onto her outstretched hand. She reports immediate pain and difficulty moving her wrist. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteopenia. She denies any numbness or tingling in her fingers. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius and limited range of motion due to pain. Her vital signs are stable. An X-ray of the wrist is pending. Which of the following is the most likely finding on the X-ray?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to widespread muscle pain and fatigue over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a constant aching, affecting her neck, shoulders, and hips. She also reports difficulty sleeping, feeling unrefreshed upon waking, and experiencing headaches. Her past medical history includes migraines and irritable bowel syndrome. She denies recent trauma or changes in bowel or bladder habits. Family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 122/74 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals diffuse tenderness to palpation over multiple soft tissue areas, but no synovitis or joint deformities. Neurologic examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and unintentional weight loss. She notes a 10-pound weight loss despite maintaining her usual diet. She reports occasional joint pain, especially in her hands, and has noticed increased bruising and pallor. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or travel history. On physical examination, she appears pale and has mild swelling in her hands. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, MCV of 80 fL, and a low reticulocyte count. ESR is elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. The pain is primarily in her wrists, hands, and knees. She also notes occasional fatigue and difficulty performing daily activities due to joint swelling. Her medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism, and she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. X-rays of the hands reveal juxta-articular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute swelling and pain in her right knee, which developed after she went hiking last weekend. She denies any history of trauma to the knee but mentions that she felt a sudden, sharp pain while descending a steep hill. She reports mild morning stiffness lasting about 5 minutes and occasional fatigue. The patient has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On physical examination, the right knee is swollen, warm, and tender, with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 8,500/mm³ and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 20 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressively worsening pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has difficulty performing tasks such as opening jars and buttoning her clothes. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. X-rays of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain and stiffness have been present for about 8 months and are worse in the morning, lasting for more than an hour. The pain is primarily located in her wrists, knuckles, and small joints of her hands, and it is symmetrical. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Past medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 36-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain in her hands, wrists, and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 2 hours, which improves with activity throughout the day. She denies any recent trauma or significant past medical history. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, with her mother having systemic lupus erythematosus. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and fatigue for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves throughout the day. The joint pain is symmetric and affects her wrists, knees, and ankles. She denies any recent trauma or infection. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (154 lb); BMI is 27.3 kg/m2. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 86 fL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of rash on her lower legs and blood in her urine. She also reports joint pain primarily in her knees and ankles, as well as a low-grade fever. She denies any recent upper respiratory infections or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her medical history is notable for seasonal allergies, and she takes antihistamines as needed. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and her family history is unremarkable. On examination, she has palpable purpura over the lower extremities and mild tenderness of the knees without swelling. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria with red blood cell casts on urinalysis, elevated ESR, and normal white blood cell count. Her renal function is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of bilateral hand and wrist pain for the past 10 months. She notices that her fingers are stiff in the mornings, which gradually improves over a few hours. She reports difficulty in doing daily activities like opening jars and typing. She also notes the presence of small, firm nodules under the skin on her elbows. Her medical history is unremarkable except for an episode of gestational diabetes 15 years ago. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 18/min. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal joints and tenderness over the wrists bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 58 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to a rash and blood in her urine. The mother reports that the rash appeared three days ago on her daughter's lower extremities, initially as small red spots, which have now coalesced into purplish patches. The patient has no fever, but she has complained of mild joint pain and swelling, particularly in her knees and ankles, over the past two weeks. She has no history of trauma and denies any recent infections or sore throat. On examination, there are palpable purpura on the lower legs, and mild swelling is noted in the knees. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Complete blood count and renal function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 4 months. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting her wrists, elbows, and knees. The discomfort is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before she feels any relief. She denies any recent travel or infections. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Her family history is significant for autoimmune conditions. Her vital signs show a blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, heart rate of 72/min, and temperature of 98.6°F (37°C). Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr), and normal renal function. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain, particularly in her hands and wrists, which she notes are stiff in the mornings. She has had these symptoms for several months and reports that ibuprofen provides only minimal relief. She denies any recent travel or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) at 45 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. The physician suspects an autoimmune condition. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given this presentation?

A 50-year-old woman comes to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe pain and swelling in her right knee after a tennis match earlier today. She describes the pain as sharp and notes that the knee is significantly swollen compared to her left knee. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and obesity. She takes lisinopril and an occasional NSAID for mild joint pains. Physical examination reveals a warm, erythematous right knee with a large effusion and limited range of motion due to pain. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 138/82 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. An x-ray of the knee is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician complaining of bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. She notes that the pain is deep and aching, and is aggravated by weight-bearing activities, especially when climbing stairs or standing for prolonged periods. She denies any recent trauma or overuse. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine respectively. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals a limited range of motion in both hips, particularly internal rotation, without any warmth or visible swelling. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein. X-rays of the pelvis demonstrate joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis in both hips. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that developed after a hiking trip in the Midwest. She reports that two weeks ago, she noticed a circular, expanding rash on her left thigh and has since experienced fatigue, muscle aches, and pain in her knees and wrists. She recalls removing a tick from her leg post-hike. Her vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 120/75 mmHg, heart rate of 76/min, and respiratory rate of 16/min. On examination, she has a faint erythematous rash on her thigh and tenderness in her knees and wrists without significant swelling. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of symmetric joint pain in her hands and feet. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is unremarkable. She does not smoke or consume alcohol regularly. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Further testing shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the rheumatology clinic due to joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past few months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving with activity. She also notes occasional swelling in the joints and feels generally fatigued. She denies any recent infections or injuries. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 weeks. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in both wrists and several fingers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes ibuprofen for pain relief. On examination, she has tender and swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
Platelet count 330,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 65 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-week history of persistent cough, difficulty breathing, and facial swelling. She reports episodes of sinus congestion and nasal discharge that have not improved with over-the-counter decongestants. Additionally, she has noticed blood in her urine and significant fatigue. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, she appears pale with a blood pressure of 150/95 mm Hg, pulse of 92/min, and temperature of 37.8°C (100°F). Lung auscultation reveals crackles in both lower lobes. Laboratory studies show a serum creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL and hematuria. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 50-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of fatigue and joint pain for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as being worse in the mornings, affecting both wrists, and lasting for several hours. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 8,000/mm³, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her legs and blood in her urine. She reports a recent upper respiratory infection. She has had joint pain in her knees and ankles, which she attributes to her daily jogging routine. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she has no known drug allergies. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on both lower extremities and mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory studies show hematuria, but no casts, and a normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely associated condition given her presentation?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to a 2-week history of a rash on her legs and blood in her urine. The rash started as small red spots on her lower extremities and has become more purpuric. She also reports mild joint pain in her knees and ankles. There is no history of recent infections, medication changes, or travel. She denies any abdominal pain, weight loss, or fevers. Her family history is unremarkable for kidney disease or autoimmune disorders. On physical examination, there are palpable purpura on the bilateral lower extremities. The rest of her examination is normal. Laboratory results show hematuria with mild proteinuria, normal platelet count, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 27-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of joint pain and a rash on her face. She describes the rash as a red, butterfly-shaped rash across her cheeks and bridge of the nose, which worsens with sun exposure. She also reports fatigue and morning stiffness in her hands that lasts about 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for one episode of pericarditis last year. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Physical examination reveals erythematous rash on the face and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show anemia and mildly elevated liver enzymes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent, dull bone pain that has been worsening over the past two months. She also reports feeling fatigued and has experienced several episodes of nausea recently. She denies any recent weight loss or fever. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, she appears fatigued but otherwise her vital signs are stable. Lab results reveal a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.2 mg/dL) and an elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) level. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks, such as buttoning her shirt. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, tender, and warm. There are subcutaneous nodules on her elbows. She denies fever or weight loss. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, and she is taking levothyroxine. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as worse in the mornings, often lasting for more than an hour before improving. She has noticed stiffness and difficulty with gripping objects. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. No skin rashes are noted, but there are small, firm nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of shoulder pain and difficulty moving her right arm. She describes the pain as constant and worse with certain movements, such as reaching overhead or behind her back. She denies any acute injury and has no prior history of similar symptoms. Her medical history is significant for mild asthma controlled with inhalers. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder and a significant decrease in active and passive range of motion, particularly with abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or redness observed. Laboratory tests show mild elevations in C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She also reports the presence of small, firm nodules on her elbows. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands are swollen and tender. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Examination reveals swelling and tenderness in multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are present over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not smoke or consume alcohol. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibody. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of worsening joint pain in her hands and knees over the past 5 years. She notes morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour and difficulty in performing tasks like opening jars. She has also observed nodules over her elbows. Her medical history is notable for anemia of chronic disease. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals swan-neck deformities and ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fever, night sweats, and weight loss of approximately 5 kg. She also complains of generalized fatigue and myalgia. There is no history of cough, shortness of breath, or recent travel. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies, and she takes loratadine as needed. She denies any family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. There is mild tenderness in her proximal muscle groups without swelling or deformity. No lymphadenopathy is noted. Lab results show hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 8,000/mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 90 mm/hr. Liver function tests are within normal limits. A chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 3 years. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning, lasting at least an hour before improving, and is accompanied by stiffness. She also mentions feeling unusually fatigued. Her medical history includes osteopenia, and she is on calcium supplements. She denies smoking or alcohol use. Physical examination reveals synovitis in both wrists and several proximal interphalangeal joints. She has subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,800/mm3
Platelet count 350,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 45 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor is positive at 120 IU/mL. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent fatigue and joint pain in her wrists and fingers over the past year. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and improves somewhat throughout the day. She denies any fevers or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a normocytic mean corpuscular volume, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of pain, swelling, and progressive deformity in her hands, particularly affecting her fingers. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. Her family history is significant for autoimmune disorders. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show juxta-articular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that her symptoms began gradually and have worsened over time. The joint pain is symmetrical, affecting her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands. She experiences stiffness in the morning lasting more than an hour, which improves with activity. She denies any weight loss, fever, or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, her wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands are swollen, tender, and warm. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 5-month history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing tasks such as buttoning her shirt. On examination, her wrists and several metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of her hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with numbness and tingling in her hands and feet for the past 3 months. She describes the sensation as a 'pins and needles' feeling that is occasionally accompanied by sharp, shooting pains. She denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Her strength is normal, and no joint deformities are noted. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, normal thyroid function tests, and a mild elevation in erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with increasing joint pain and stiffness over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour and difficulty with daily activities due to decreased range of motion in her fingers. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, which are well-controlled with medication. She takes no medications for her joint symptoms. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with noticeable ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Imaging of her hands reveals joint space narrowing and erosions. Her laboratory results show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain that have worsened over the past six months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and symmetric swelling of her wrists and fingers. She denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for stable hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, she has tender and swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 10.9 g/dL (normal 12.0-15.5 g/dL), ESR of 42 mm/hr (normal 0-20 mm/hr), and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and skin changes. She notes that her joint pain began in the distal interphalangeal joints of her right hand and has recently affected her left knee and right ankle. She has morning stiffness lasting about an hour, which improves with activity. She also reports scaly, erythematous plaques on her elbows and knees that have been present for the past few years, which she initially attributed to dry skin. Her past medical history is significant for mild hypertension. On examination, there is swelling of the right distal interphalangeal joints and tenderness in the left knee. Skin examination reveals well-demarcated, silvery plaques on the elbows and knees. Laboratory tests show normal blood counts and mildly elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists, which are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. She also reports fatigue and has noticed subcutaneous nodules on her elbows. Her grandmother had 'arthritis' in her hands. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash. She describes the joint pain as migratory, affecting her knees and wrists, and notes that it started shortly after she returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States. She also reports a circular rash on her thigh that appeared after a tick bite during the trip. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, she has an erythematous, annular rash with central clearing on her left thigh, measuring approximately 8 cm in diameter. Her vital signs are unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and pain in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is worst in the morning and lasts for about 2 hours before improving slightly. She also notes swelling of her fingers and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt. She has no significant past medical history but mentions that her mother had similar joint issues. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are high. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to persistent dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. She also reports feeling extremely fatigued and has noticed swelling and mild tenderness in her cheek. She has a history of hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. Her family history includes a sister with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. There is bilateral parotid gland enlargement, diminished salivary flow, and conjunctival injection. Her joint examination reveals mild tenderness without swelling in the small joints of her hands. Which of the following is the most specific test for confirming the suspected diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with pain and swelling in her right big toe. She reports that the pain started suddenly last night and is severe enough to limit her ability to walk. She has experienced similar episodes in the past involving the same joint, and each time, the pain resolved after a few days. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, there is erythema, warmth, and tenderness of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint. There are firm, nodular deposits on the helix of her right ear. Laboratory results show elevated serum uric acid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 63-year-old woman presents to the clinic with diffuse bone pain and fatigue. She reports this has been worsening over the past few months.
