She describes the pain as dull and persistent, with morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and improvement with physical activity. She reports occasional fatigue and stiffness in her hips and shoulders. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain relief. She works as a graphic designer and has no history of trauma. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic for evaluation of skin lesions and joint pain. She reports having red, scaly patches on her elbows and knees for several years, which have recently begun to itch and occasionally bleed. In the past few months, she has developed pain in her right knee and left wrist, which is worse in the mornings and gradually improves throughout the day. She denies any fevers or significant unintentional weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of her arms and legs. There is swelling and tenderness in the right knee and left wrist, with reduced range of motion. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate but are otherwise unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of persistent bone pain in her hips and lower back for the past several months. She mentions that she is postmenopausal and has not experienced any fractures, but her symptoms have gradually worsened, especially when she walks for extended periods. She denies any significant joint swelling or stiffness in the morning. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, she appears in mild discomfort but has no visible joint deformities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, normal calcium, and normal vitamin D levels. The patient inquires about her condition and possible next steps in management. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step for addressing her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 9-month history of skin tightening on her fingers and periodic episodes of finger discoloration triggered by cold. She describes the fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red, accompanied by a tingling sensation. She also notes occasional joint stiffness in the mornings, but it resolves after an hour of activity. She denies any difficulty swallowing or new respiratory symptoms. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, her blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F). There is reduced skin elasticity over the fingers and shiny skin on her hands. Capillary nailfold microscopy reveals dilated loops. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of symmetrical joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint deformities in her fingers. She denies any recent infections, and there is no family history of similar symptoms. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past year. She reports that her eyes feel gritty and she frequently needs to sip water to help swallow food. Over the last few months, she has also noticed a swelling near her jawline that becomes painful at times. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional joint pain which she attributes to aging. She denies any recent fever or significant weight loss. On examination, she has notable bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Her laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with her symptoms?

A 50-year-old woman visits the clinic due to persistent fatigue and joint pain over the last 6 months. She describes morning stiffness that lasts over an hour and symmetric pain in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history includes hypertension and she takes lisinopril. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and both knees. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 128/78 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3, Platelet count 280,000/mm3, ESR 75 mm/h, Rheumatoid factor positive, Anti-CCP antibody negative. X-rays of the hands reveal marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of acute knee pain and swelling that began after a weekend hiking trip. She describes the pain as sharp and worsening with any weight-bearing activities. She denies any history of trauma to the knee but mentions feeling fatigued more than usual over the past few weeks. Her vital signs are stable. On physical examination, her right knee is visibly swollen, warm to the touch, and tender on palpation, with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no erythema or rash. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A knee x-ray shows a moderate joint effusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.F: Joint aspiration for synovial fluid analysis

question: A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving as the day progresses. The pain is symmetric and involves the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. Her family history is notable for her mother having "some kind of arthritis." Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of multiple joints in both hands. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, facial pain, and occasional nosebleeds. She also reports a dry cough and progressive shortness of breath over the past few months. Recently, she has noticed swelling in her ankles and a decrease in urine output. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 138/82 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 20/min. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral rales at the bases. Laboratory studies demonstrate elevated creatinine and hematuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to 4 months of fatigue and joint pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands at work as a typist. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She has no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin concentration of 11.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 5,800/mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 78 mm/h. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of bilateral joint pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and fingers. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and fatigue. Her past medical history is unremarkable, but her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is synovial hypertrophy and warmth in the affected joints. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP levels. Further testing reveals positive anti-citrullinated protein antibodies and rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic pain and swelling in her hands and wrists that has persisted for the past 8 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, making it difficult for her to perform daily tasks. On examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 33-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a six-month history of scaling, erythematous plaques on her elbows and knees. She reports accompanying joint pain that primarily affects her fingers and toes, often leading to morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She denies fever, weight loss, or fatigue. Her medical history is significant only for mild seasonal allergies. On examination, she has plaques with silvery scales on her extensor surfaces and swelling in several distal interphalangeal joints, with tenderness of the Achilles tendon. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor is negative, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are absent. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the last 2 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. On examination, her vital signs are stable. Swelling and tenderness are noted in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Lab results reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing is positive for rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of progressive hand deformities over the past 4 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in multiple joints, including her wrists and the proximal interphalangeal joints. She also notes intermittent fatigue and difficulty performing tasks requiring fine motor skills. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints and swan neck deformities of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman visits the clinic due to joint swelling and pain in both her hands for the past 2 months. The pain is most pronounced in the mornings and improves throughout the day. She denies any significant past medical history or recent illness. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory investigations show:

Hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 6,500/mm3
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 48 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 2.1 mg/dL (normal < 1.0 mg/dL)

Serum autoantibody panel:
Rheumatoid factor Positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide Positive
Antinuclear antibody Negative
Anti-double-stranded DNA Negative

Based on these findings, which of the following conditions is most consistent with this patient's clinical presentation?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that has progressively worsened over the past year. She describes stiffness in her fingers and wrists, particularly in the morning, lasting for more than an hour. She has difficulty performing daily tasks due to the pain. Her past medical history includes hypertension controlled with lisinopril. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals juxta-articular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands, wrists, and knees for the past 3 months. She notes that the stiffness is particularly bad in the morning, lasting about 2 hours, and improves as the day goes on. She has difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars and buttoning shirts. She denies recent infections, fever, or weight loss. Her family history is notable for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as the wrists. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of chronic pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past year. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, improving slightly with activity. She denies any recent trauma or overuse. She reports feeling fatigued and having difficulty performing daily tasks. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with several firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman visits the clinic complaining of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past two months. She notes the stiffness is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She reports difficulty holding objects due to swelling of her fingers. She has not experienced these symptoms before and denies any recent trauma or infection. She has a family history of autoimmune disease; her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. No skin rashes or oral ulcers are present. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of swollen lymph nodes, persistent fatigue, and unintentional weight loss. She reports occasional fevers and night sweats but no cough or recent infections. On examination, she has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Her joints are not swollen or tender, and she has no skin rashes. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Order a complete blood count with differential
options.C: Perform an excisional lymph node biopsy

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the symptoms are most severe in the morning, lasting about 2 hours before improving. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There is also limited range of motion in her wrists. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She describes the joint pain as symmetric, primarily affecting her wrists, knees, and hands, and it is worse in the mornings, lasting for about 2 hours before improving. She reports significant fatigue throughout the day but denies any weight loss or fever. Medical history is notable for hypertension managed with amlodipine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol.

Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the bilateral wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The remainder of the exam, including cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations, is unremarkable. Laboratory results show a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL (normal: 12.0-15.5 g/dL), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (normal: <20 mm/hr), and a positive rheumatoid factor. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling, primarily affecting her wrists and fingers symmetrically. She reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her antinuclear antibody (ANA) is positive, and rheumatoid factor is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful oral ulcers that have recurred over the last six months. She also reports experiencing painful genital ulcers but did not seek medical attention, attributing them to shaving. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections but notes occasional joint pain in her knees. The patient denies any new sexual partners and uses oral contraceptives. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals multiple, shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and one healing ulcer on the labia majora. There is no lymphadenopathy. Laboratory findings show elevated C-reactive protein and normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with generalized fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, particularly affecting her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands. Her past medical history is significant for obesity and hyperlipidemia, controlled with atorvastatin. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On examination, the patient has swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein of 12 mg/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a 3-month history of worsening difficulty walking. She describes weakness in both legs, which has caused her to use a cane for stability. She denies any trauma, falls, or recent infections. She mentions that she has been experiencing vague joint pain and stiffness occasionally, which she attributes to aging. Her past medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, she has reduced strength in the lower extremities, particularly in the proximal muscles, and diminished deep tendon reflexes. There is no sensory loss. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels and normal inflammatory markers. An electromyography study indicates myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old Caucasian woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She describes swelling and pain in her fingers, wrists, and knees, which improves as the day progresses. Her mother had similar joint issues and was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis in her 50s. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands reveal soft tissue swelling and narrowing of joint spaces. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, facial pain, and postnasal drip. She reports episodes of coughing, fatigue, and shortness of breath. She denies any joint pain, but mentions occasional blood in her urine. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and crackles in the lung bases bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:

White blood cell count: 13,000/mm3
Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL
C-reactive protein: 25 mg/L
Urinalysis: Red blood cell casts, proteinuria

A chest X-ray reveals bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening pain, swelling, and stiffness in her hands and wrists. The symptoms are most pronounced in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before improving. She reports difficulty opening jars and holding a cup, which she attributes to joint deformities developing in her fingers. Her medical history is unremarkable except for episodic migraines. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Vital signs are normal. On examination, there is ulnar deviation of the fingers, and the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive stiffness and pain in her hands and knees over the past six months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities due to joint discomfort. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled with medication. She denies any recent infections or fever. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, which improves with activity throughout the day. She reports occasional pain in her hips and has noticed difficulty bending and twisting her torso. She denies any previous back injuries or trauma and has no significant past medical history. Her family history is notable for an uncle with a type of arthritis. She is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. On examination, there is limited range of motion in the lumbar spine, and her Schober test is positive. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. HLA-B27 testing is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic for evaluation of progressive joint deformity in her hands over the past 5 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and symmetrical swelling of her finger joints. She has difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning her clothes. She denies any fever or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, there is ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints and swan neck deformities of several fingers. Laboratory studies show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain, stiffness, and swelling involving her hands and wrists. The stiffness is particularly noticeable in the morning and lasts for over an hour before improving. She has difficulty opening jars and grasping objects. Her past medical history is significant for mild hypertension. She is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. Her family history is notable for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation present. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and elevated ESR. Which of the following laboratory findings would be most indicative of her condition?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of bone pain and fatigue for the last several months. She reports the pain is most pronounced in her hips and lower back. The patient is postmenopausal and has not had a menstrual period for 15 years. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril and a history of smoking, although she quit 10 years ago. Physical examination shows tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine and pelvis. Laboratory studies reveal elevated alkaline phosphatase levels with normal calcium and phosphorus. A plain radiograph of the pelvis shows cortical thickening. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 49-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain and deformity affecting both hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. She denies any recent trauma or skin rashes. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There are also noticeable ulnar deviations and swan-neck deformities of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with concerns about progressive hand deformities and joint pain over the last 6 months. She reports swelling and stiffness in her finger joints, especially in the mornings, lasting about 1 hour. She has difficulty performing daily tasks like opening jars and writing. She has a history of hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation and swan-neck deformities of several fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past two months. She also reports occasional burning sensations in her toes. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, and her blood glucose levels have been poorly controlled. She takes metformin, but admits to irregular medication adherence. She denies any recent trauma or changes in activity level. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the distal extremities in a stocking-glove distribution. Achilles tendon reflexes are diminished bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman visits your clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been ongoing for 8 weeks. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning and affecting her wrists, elbows, knees, and ankles symmetrically. She has noticed swelling and stiffness in these joints that improve as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she has not experienced any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals synovitis and tenderness in multiple joints, including the wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 8200/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/h. A hand X-ray reveals periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain accompanied by significant fatigue and difficulty sleeping. She reports that the pain is diffuse, affecting various parts of her body, and does not seem to improve with rest. She also notes feeling tired despite sleeping 8–9 hours each night. Her medical history is significant for anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. She denies any swelling or joint redness but mentions that the pain seems to be worse in the morning. Physical examination reveals tender points over the neck, shoulders, and back, with no synovitis or joint deformities. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and metabolic panel, with negative rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Initiate a trial of low-dose amitriptyline
options.B: Start a course of oral corticosteroids

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a two-week history of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fever. She reports having recovered from a viral upper respiratory infection three weeks ago. She denies any rash or photosensitivity. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals mild joint tenderness without significant swelling, and there is no synovitis. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour, and improving throughout the day. She has felt unusually fatigued but denies any recent weight loss or fever. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-year history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She notes that the stiffness is most severe in the mornings, often lasting over an hour before improving. She reports difficulty completing household tasks due to hand pain. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, her hands show swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with visible subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal anemia of chronic disease and elevated C-reactive protein. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of bluish discoloration and a lacy pattern on her skin, which becomes more pronounced in cold weather. She also reports recurrent episodes of leg pain and swelling, which she initially attributed to prolonged standing at her job as a retail manager. Her medical history includes migraine headaches, and she takes sumatriptan as needed. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination shows livedo reticularis on her lower extremities. Homan's sign is negative. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,500/mm3
Platelet count 240,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 28 mm/h
Serum
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Anticardiolipin antibodies: High
Lupus anticoagulant: Positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of itchy, scaly skin lesions and painful joints. She reports that the skin lesions began approximately six months ago and have been gradually worsening. The lesions are located predominantly on her elbows, knees, and scalp, appearing as red plaques with silvery scales. She also mentions joint pain that is mostly in her fingers, but sometimes affects her knees and ankles. The pain is asymmetric and often worsens after periods of inactivity. Her medical history is unremarkable, and there is no family history of skin or joint disease. Physical examination reveals well-demarcated erythematous plaques covered by silvery scales and swelling of the distal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP, normal rheumatoid factor, and negative anti-CCP antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of neck pain and tingling sensations in both arms. She describes the pain as aching and persistent, often radiating to her shoulders. The paresthesias have become more frequent, especially when she moves her neck. She has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications regularly. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in her cervical spine, and Spurling's test is positive. Neurological examination reveals decreased strength in the grip of her left hand. Cervical spine X-rays show some degeneration at the C5-C6 and C6-C7 levels. Laboratory studies reveal: 
C-reactive protein: 1.2 mg/dL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 25 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a six-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning and improve as the day progresses. The patient denies any recent infections, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and erosions at the affected joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening pain and deformity in her hands over the past 2 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in the joints of her hands, which improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with amlodipine. On examination, there is ulnar deviation of the fingers and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman visits the clinic with a complaint of widespread musculoskeletal pain that has been ongoing for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as persistent and affecting her shoulders, neck, lower back, and hips. Additionally, she experiences significant fatigue and reports non-restorative sleep, despite sleeping for 8 hours each night. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional tension headaches. She denies any weight changes, fever, or skin rashes. On examination, there are tender points in multiple muscle groups, especially along the trapezius and lateral epicondyle areas. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count, normal thyroid function, and slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the outpatient clinic with complaints of joint pain and a skin rash over the past week. She mentions that she returned from a hiking trip in the northeast United States two weeks ago, where she recalls removing a tick from her leg. Her joint pain involves her knees and elbows and is associated with stiffness in the morning. She also reports fatigue and a low-grade fever. On examination, there is an erythematous rash on her back with central clearing. Her temperature is 99.0°F (37.2°C), blood pressure is 115/75 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal mild leukopenia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, weight loss, and skin nodules on her elbows and knees for the past 2 months. She describes the nodules as firm and non-painful. She also reports intermittent fevers and night sweats but denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for asthma managed with an inhaler. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her vital signs are: temperature 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure 118/76 mm Hg, pulse 88/min, and respiratory rate 18/min. Physical examination reveals subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees. There are no joint deformities or active synovitis noted. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.4 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7,200/mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/h. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent joint pain and a rash on her face that worsens in the sun. She reports that the joint pain started in her hands and knees about three months ago and is accompanied by morning stiffness that lasts for about an hour. Over the past few weeks, she has noticed fatigue and mild hair loss. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. On examination, there is erythema across her cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. There is mild swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer of 1:1280 and anti-Smith antibodies. Rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of worsening bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and reports swelling and tenderness in these areas. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is symmetrical synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints, with multiple subcutaneous nodules palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of her hands shows erosions at the joint margins. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as affecting both of her wrists, hands, and knees symmetrically, and it is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for about 1 hour before improving. She has noticed increasing difficulty with her daily activities due to stiffness. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. She works as an accountant and denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, there is palpable synovitis in her wrist and finger joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 90 fL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressively worsening muscle weakness over the past six months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. There is no significant joint pain, but she does mention mild morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes. She denies any fever, rash, or recent infections. Her medical history is notable for well-controlled hypertension. On examination, she has 4/5 strength in her proximal muscles of both upper and lower limbs, while distal muscle strength is preserved. Reflexes are normal, and there is no muscle tenderness. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase levels and mild elevation of liver enzymes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the dermatology clinic due to scaly, red patches on her elbows and knees that have been present for several months. She also complains of joint pain predominantly affecting her fingers on the left hand and her right knee. The patient mentions morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes and occasional fatigue. She has no known allergies and is currently on no medications. Physical examination reveals nail pitting and dactylitis of the left index finger. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-month history of fatigue, fever, and multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her arms. She describes occasional joint pain, particularly in her wrists and knees, but denies significant morning stiffness. She also reports a 5-pound unintentional weight loss over the past month. Her medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She smokes a half pack of cigarettes daily and consumes alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals several firm, non-tender nodules on the elbows and forearms. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7,800/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/h, and C-reactive protein of 3.2 mg/dL. Which of the following tests would be most useful in clarifying the underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands over the past three months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She has also noticed fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She denies any recent infections or skin rashes. Family history is notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. Her vital signs are stable. On physical examination, there are swollen and tender joints at both wrists and the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), elevated C-reactive protein (CRP), positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with increasing stiffness and swelling in her hands and wrists over the past year. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about two hours before improving slightly. Over-the-counter NSAIDs provide minimal relief. She also notes difficulty grasping objects and frequent fatigue. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is pronounced swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Bilateral ulnar deviation and subluxation of the wrist joints are noted. Laboratory studies show:
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-CCP antibody: Positive
C-reactive protein: Elevated
ESR: 55 mm/hr
X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions at the wrist and fingers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with worsening low back pain over the past 3 months. She describes the pain as sharp and localized in the lower thoracic region. She denies any recent injury or trauma. She has a history of early-stage osteoporosis, diagnosed 5 years ago, and is on calcium and vitamin D supplements. She also takes hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension. She reports occasional morning stiffness in her hands for the past year, but denies any joint swelling. She has had significant fatigue and trouble sleeping due to the back pain. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T11 and T12 vertebrae, and a reduction in height of 2 cm since her last visit a year ago. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show normal calcium and alkaline phosphatase levels. X-rays reveal a vertebral compression fracture at T12 with mild anterior wedging. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Perform a dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan
options.F: Order an MRI of the spine

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with complaints of joint pain in her hands and feet for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour, and improves as the day progresses. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, as well as the metatarsophalangeal joints of the feet. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent lower back pain. She describes the pain as a dull ache that improves with physical activity and worsens with rest, particularly first thing in the morning. She also notes occasional stiffness in her lower back and hips. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for eczema, and she does not take any regular medications. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory results show normal ESR and CRP levels. A pelvic X-ray is ordered. What is the most likely finding on the X-ray?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past two weeks. She mentions that the pain started in her knees and later affected her wrists and ankles. The joint discomfort is accompanied by mild fever and fatigue. She recalls having a sore throat about three weeks ago but did not seek medical attention at that time. She denies any skin rash, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On physical examination, her knees and wrists are tender and slightly swollen. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). The rapid streptococcal antigen test is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness that have been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting for over an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning her clothes. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she has no known family history of autoimmune diseases. She is a non-smoker and works as a graphic designer. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive joint deformity over the past 3 years. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour and swelling in both hands and wrists. She has difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning clothes due to decreased hand dexterity. Her medications include ibuprofen and a low-dose oral corticosteroid. On physical examination, there is ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints and subluxation of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain, photosensitive rash, and oral ulcers. She reports feeling unusually tired over the past few weeks. The joint pain is primarily in her hands and knees, and she describes morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. On examination, she has a butterfly-shaped rash over her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and multiple small ulcers on the oral mucosa. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive weight gain over the past six months. She reports notable swelling around her eyes, especially in the morning, and stiffness in her hands and knees that lasts for over an hour each morning. She denies changes in her diet or physical activity levels. She has also noticed her hair becoming brittle and thinning. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, her blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and BMI is 30 kg/m². Physical exam reveals periorbital edema, non-pitting edema of the lower extremities, and mild tenderness over the small joints of the hands. Laboratory studies show a TSH of 5.8 mIU/L (reference range: 0.4–4.0 mIU/L), free T4 of 0.9 ng/dL (reference range: 0.8–1.8 ng/dL), and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 10-day history of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash. She describes the joint pain as migrating and affecting her knees, elbows, and wrists. She also reports experiencing a headache and a low-grade fever. Upon further questioning, she recalls a recent hiking trip in a wooded area where she removed a tick from her left thigh. She denies any recent travel or sick contacts. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash with a central clearing on her left thigh, consistent with erythema migrans. Laboratory tests are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.G: Refer to rheumatology for joint aspiration

question: A 50-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the symptoms are most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before easing with movement. The pain primarily affects her wrists, metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints, and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally. She denies any significant past medical history but recalls her mother had similar symptoms. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the MCP and PIP joints, with a slight ulnar deviation noted at the wrists. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and stiffness. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving throughout the day. She also notes swelling in her fingers and wrists bilaterally. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (reference range: <20 mm/hr). Radiographs of the hands show mild joint space narrowing and osteopenia around the joints.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits the clinic due to persistent pain and swelling in her hands and wrists over the past year. She reports that the pain is most severe in the morning, lasting more than an hour before improving slightly. She has also noticed the development of firm lumps beneath the skin near her elbows. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Her laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue, morning stiffness in her hands, and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She reports that the stiffness lasts for over an hour each morning and is associated with significant pain. She has been taking ibuprofen with minimal relief. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a six-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that her symptoms began with stiffness in her hands in the morning that improves over the course of the day. She also notes swelling and pain in her wrists and knees bilaterally, which has progressively worsened. She has difficulty performing daily tasks and feels more tired than usual. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies recent infections, fever, or weight changes. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, as well as bilateral knee effusions. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 60 mm/h, Rheumatoid factor positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. She reports waking up frequently at night and feeling unrefreshed in the morning. Her pain is described as a constant, dull ache affecting her shoulders, back, and hips. She denies any fever, weight loss, or joint swelling. Her medical history includes anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. Physical examination reveals multiple tender points over the neck, shoulders, and lower back. There is no joint swelling or deformity. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 44-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain accompanied by swelling and stiffness, particularly in the morning lasting more than an hour. She has noticed firm, painless nodules on her elbows. Her medical history is unremarkable except for hypothyroidism, which is well-managed with levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Vital signs show: temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, pulse 76/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with decreased grip strength. Laboratory workup shows elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies and rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swollen lymph nodes in her neck and groin, accompanied by night sweats, low-grade fevers, and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds. She also reports mild joint pain and fatigue but denies any morning stiffness or notable joint swelling. On examination, she has palpable cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy, but no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal mild normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and normal renal function. An excisional biopsy of a cervical lymph node is performed, and histopathology shows non-caseating granulomas. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes the pain as symmetric, primarily involving her wrists, knees, and ankles, and it is most severe in the morning, lasting several hours. She denies any recent travel, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in her wrists and knees with restricted range of motion. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 35%
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm^3
Platelet count: 250,000/mm^3
ESR: 55 mm/hr
C-reactive protein: Elevated
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-nuclear antibody: Negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over 60 minutes. She has noted swelling and tenderness in the joints and the development of small, firm nodules under the skin at her elbows. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor test is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detected. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of worsening joint pain and deformities over the past three years. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and swelling in her wrists and fingers. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraines. She takes ibuprofen as needed for pain relief. On physical examination, there is noticeable ulnar deviation of the fingers with swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands and wrists show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her left big toe for the past six months. Each episode lasts for a few days and then resolves spontaneously. She reports that the pain is excruciating and often wakes her up at night. She denies any fever or chills. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. She has been taking over-the-counter NSAIDs with partial relief. Physical exam reveals a swollen, erythematous first metatarsophalangeal joint with a chalky, palpable nodule. Laboratory studies show elevated serum uric acid levels. She drinks alcohol socially on weekends. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 4-month history of pain, swelling, and stiffness in her fingers and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about 1 hour before improving throughout the day. She has difficulty opening jars and holding her coffee mug in the morning. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she is taking levothyroxine. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, tender, and warm bilaterally. Her laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to pain and swelling. She has noticed several firm nodules under the skin on her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or infections. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, with multiple subcutaneous nodules palpated on the extensor surfaces of both elbows. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of painful red nodules on her shins and a low-grade fever. She reports joint pain in her knees and ankles, which has progressively worsened, making it difficult for her to walk. Her medical history is notable for mild seasonal allergies, and she denies any recent travel or new medications. She works as a nurse and has no significant family history of autoimmune diseases. Her vital signs show: temperature 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure 115/75 mmHg, pulse 80/min, and respirations 16/min. On examination, there are multiple tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior surfaces of both lower legs. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and skin lesions. She reports that for the past 8 months she has experienced pain in her joints, notably in her fingers and knees. The pain is described as worse in the morning but improves with movement throughout the day. On physical examination, there are erythematous, scaly plaques on her elbows and scalp. Some joints in her fingers are swollen, with a notable asymmetry in involvement. She denies any significant morning stiffness and has no history of any other systemic illness. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 16/min. Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour, which improves as the day goes on. The patient notes difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars and turning door knobs. She has a history of type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. Her mother had similar joint issues. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with this patient's presentation?

A 52-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her fingers and toes. She describes the sensations as pins and needles, sometimes accompanied by a burning feeling. She has also noticed occasional weakness in her grip and difficulty with buttoning her clothes. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections, fever, or unusual activities. Her family history is notable for a mother with diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, she has decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the distal extremities. Muscle strength is 4/5 in the small muscles of the hands. Laboratory studies reveal normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Her serum glucose, vitamin B12, and folate levels are within normal limits. Nerve conduction studies show decreased sensory nerve action potentials in the affected areas.

What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of joint pain in her hands and wrists, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports the pain improves with movement throughout the day. She denies recent illness, skin changes, or other systemic symptoms. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals synovial thickening and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show: 

ESR: 40 mm/hr (normal < 20 mm/hr), 
CRP: 15 mg/L (normal < 10 mg/L), 
Rheumatoid factor: Positive, 
Anti-CCP antibody: Positive. 

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 39-year-old woman visits the clinic with complaints of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past three months. She reports that the symptoms began gradually and are worsening, making it difficult for her to type at her job. She also notes occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, but denies any morning stiffness. She has a history of hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril, and is otherwise healthy. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 135/80 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in her hands and feet. A complete blood count is normal, but laboratory tests show elevated fasting blood glucose levels. An EMG confirms a peripheral neuropathy. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her peripheral neuropathy?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressive joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. Over the past few months, she has noticed deformities developing in her fingers. She denies any fevers or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. On physical examination, there are visible ulnar deviations of the fingers, and several joints are swollen and tender to touch. Laboratory testing reveals elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Considering these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful red nodules on her shins and joint pain primarily in her knees and ankles. She reports feeling fatigued and has noticed occasional low-grade fevers. She denies any recent travel, weight loss, or drug use. She also mentions a prior episode of mild diarrhea that resolved without treatment. On examination, tender erythematous subcutaneous nodules are noted on both shins, and there is tenderness and swelling of the knees and ankles bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers, and a chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent fevers and fatigue over the past three months. She also reports significant weight loss and night sweats. The patient notes that she has experienced generalized lymphadenopathy, which has not resolved despite initial antibiotic treatment prescribed by her primary care physician. Her past medical history is significant for migraines and seasonal allergies. She takes ibuprofen as needed for headaches. On examination, her temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, heart rate is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Physical exam reveals multiple enlarged, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Initial laboratory tests show leukocytosis, elevated ESR, and mild anemia. A chest X-ray is performed and shows no mediastinal masses. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain and skin changes that have been present for the past 6 months. She reports intermittent swelling and pain in her knees and fingers, which seem to affect one side more than the other. She also mentions silvery scales on her elbows and knees that have been worsening over the past year. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her knees and distal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender, and there are well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on extensor surfaces. Laboratory studies show a negative rheumatoid factor and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects. She notes fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands but denies morning stiffness. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, she has proximal muscle weakness, particularly in the shoulder and hip girdles. A skin rash is noted on her eyelids and knuckles. Laboratory results show elevated creatine kinase and aldolase levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling. She reports the pain started yesterday after she completed a 10-mile hike. She has no significant past medical history but mentions occasional joint stiffness lasting less than 15 minutes in the morning. She takes ibuprofen as needed for headaches. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. The right knee is swollen with warmth and tenderness over the joint line. There is a mild effusion, and the range of motion is limited by pain. Laboratory studies show:
Leukocyte count 9,800/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/h
C-reactive protein 2.8 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Order an MRI of the knee

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of shoulder pain and stiffness. She reports difficulty in reaching overhead and fastening her bra due to restricted range of motion. The pain is worse at night and disrupts her sleep. She denies any recent trauma or heavy lifting. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she is on levothyroxine. On examination, there is decreased active and passive range of motion in the left shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is tenderness over the deltoid region but no redness or swelling. Vital signs are normal. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with complaints of fatigue, weight gain, and feeling cold all the time for the past 6 months. She also reports occasional joint stiffness and pain, primarily in the mornings, but denies any swelling. Her menstrual cycles have become irregular over the last few months, and she notes thinning of her hair. She has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications regularly. She denies alcohol or drug use and has no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland, dry skin, and brittle nails. Vital signs are within normal limits.

Laboratory tests show elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free T4 levels. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling that began yesterday after a long hike. She reports the pain worsens with movement and is accompanied by noticeable swelling and warmth around the joint. She denies any fever, chills, or recent trauma. Her past medical history is significant for asthma, for which she uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. She takes no other medications. On physical examination, her right knee is swollen with an effusion, and there is tenderness on palpation. Range of motion is limited due to pain. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 7,800/mm³ with a normal differential. A knee aspiration is performed, and analysis of the synovial fluid is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP levels. Serological tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Hand X-rays demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following findings is most consistent with this patient's diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to 3 months of progressive joint pain. She reports swelling and stiffness in her fingers and wrists, which is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. She also notes feeling fatigued and experiencing low-grade fevers intermittently. On examination, the joints of her hands and wrists are swollen, tender to palpation, and warm. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving slightly. She also notes swelling and tenderness in her fingers and wrists bilaterally. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. On physical examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal the following: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hr, C-reactive protein 8 mg/L, positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that she has been experiencing for the past 3 months. The joint pain is symmetric and involves her wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. Her past medical history includes hyperlipidemia, for which she takes atorvastatin.
