On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old female presents to the clinic with a three-year history of chronic lower back pain that is progressively worsening. She describes the pain as dull and persistent, with stiffness that improves slightly with activity but worsens after rest. Over the last six months, she has also noticed occasional swelling in her knees and ankles. She denies any morning fatigue or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis, and she takes lisinopril and ibuprofen as needed. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her ESR is mildly elevated, and HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past 4 months. She also reports new periorbital edema, morning stiffness in her wrists and fingers lasting about 30 minutes, and generalized fatigue. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies and mild hypertension, which is controlled with lisinopril. She denies any recent changes in her diet or exercise regimen. Her vital signs are stable, with a blood pressure of 126/78 mmHg, heart rate of 72/min, and temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C). Physical examination reveals puffy eyelids and mild tenderness of the wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show normal thyroid function, normal renal function, and a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful red nodules on her shins and diffuse joint pain for the past two weeks. She describes the nodules as warm, tender, and raised. She also mentions stiffness in her knees and ankles that is worse in the morning and improves slightly as the day progresses. Her past medical history includes asthma, for which she uses inhaled corticosteroids. She denies any recent travel or respiratory symptoms but does mention feeling more fatigued than usual. On examination, she has erythematous nodules on both shins, and her knees and ankles are tender to palpation without swelling. Laboratory findings show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). What is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's presentation?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, low-grade fever, and tender lumps on her forearms. She reports that these nodules have appeared on the extensor surfaces, particularly the elbows and forearms, and have become progressively more painful. She also notes generalized joint pain, particularly in her knees and wrists, that is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies any recent infections or significant weight loss. Her personal medical history includes hypothyroidism, well-managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals multiple firm, non-fluctuant nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms and elbows, mild synovitis of the wrists, and a warm, tender right knee with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. What is the most likely cause of the nodules in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. She describes the pain as widespread, affecting her shoulders, back, and hips, and notes that it is particularly severe in the morning. She reports feeling exhausted despite sleeping 8-9 hours each night. The patient has tried over-the-counter pain medication with minimal relief. She has a history of mild depression and has been taking sertraline for the past year. Her vital signs are stable: temperature 98.5°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, pulse 76/min, and respirations 14/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness at multiple soft tissue sites without significant joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies show: ESR 10 mm/hr, CRP 3 mg/L, ANA negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been progressively worsening over the last two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily activities such as opening jars. She also mentions the presence of firm lumps under the skin near her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and a rash. She reports being bitten by a tick a month ago while hiking in a forested area. She initially developed a circular rash around the bite, which has since resolved, but now she experiences migratory pain in her knees and wrists. She also complains of fatigue and occasional headaches but denies any history of similar joint issues or family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is tenderness in her knees and wrists but no visible swelling. A maculopapular rash is noted on her trunk. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild leukocytosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Conduct an MRI of the affected joints

question: A 46-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with worsening joint pain and stiffness over the past 4 months. She reports that the stiffness lasts for several hours in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent fevers or weight loss. On examination, her hands show signs of deformities with swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions at the proximal interphalangeal joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting her wrists, knees, and hands, and reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She denies any recent infections or injuries. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and bilateral wrists. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic complaining of worsening joint pain and deformity over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities like opening jars and buttoning clothes. Her medical history is unremarkable except for a diagnosis of hypertension treated with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of both hands, swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and rheumatoid nodules on the elbows. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand x-rays demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman visits the clinic due to progressive deformities in her hands over the past 5 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and persistent joint pain. On examination, there are ulnar deviations and swan-neck deformities at the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following best describes this patient's condition?

A 42-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to burning and tingling sensations in her feet for the past 3 months. She notes the symptoms worsen at night and occasionally interfere with her sleep. She also occasionally experiences numbness in her fingertips. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in her daily activities. Her medical history is notable for obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, currently managed with metformin. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, she has decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the distal aspects of both feet. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Recent laboratory studies show normal hemoglobin A1c and renal function. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the underlying cause of her symptoms?

options.D: Test serum glucose levels over a 24-hour period
options.F: Conduct a skin biopsy to evaluate small nerve fibers

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint swelling for the past 6 months. She reports experiencing morning stiffness lasting over an hour which improves with activity. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serologies show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of a mottled, purplish discoloration on her legs that becomes more pronounced in cold weather. She also reports a history of recurrent miscarriages and occasional pain in her joints, particularly in her hands and knees. She denies any recent infections or fevers. Her past medical records reveal two previous deep vein thromboses, and she has been on anticoagulation therapy intermittently. Family history is significant for a mother with lupus. On physical examination, livedo reticularis is noted on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel. Antinuclear antibody test is positive, but low titer. Anticardiolipin antibodies are positive. The patient inquires about the possible diagnosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Perform skin biopsy to confirm diagnosis
options.F: Refer to a hematologist for further evaluation

question: A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of joint pain and swelling in both hands that started two months ago. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She denies any recent injuries or changes in medication. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Serological tests show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with joint pain and stiffness that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as primarily affecting her fingers and wrists, and notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for about 1 hour before improving with movement. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of dry eyes and a dry mouth that have been persistent for the past 6 months. She also reports occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, but denies any morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well-nourished with bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Her vital signs show a temperature of 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure of 120/75 mmHg, pulse of 78/min, and respiratory rate of 16/min. Schirmer's test is positive, indicating reduced tear production. Laboratory tests are pending, including the antinuclear antibody panel and rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of body aches and fatigue. She reports that she had a sore throat and mild fever two weeks ago which resolved after a few days, but since then she has experienced persistent muscle and joint pain that is worse in the morning. She denies any joint swelling, fever, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the large muscle groups, particularly the thighs and upper arms, but no joint swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests show a mildly elevated ESR and normal CRP levels. A rapid strep test performed during her initial illness was positive, and she completed a course of antibiotics. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and skin changes that have developed over the past two months. She describes asymmetric pain and swelling involving the fingers of her right hand and left knee, which worsens with activity. Additionally, she reports the development of red, scaly patches on her elbows and scalp. She has been feeling more fatigued than usual but has not noticed any fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension, well-controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals tender, swollen joints in the right hand and left knee, along with well-demarcated erythematous plaques covered with silvery scales on the elbows. Her vital signs are stable, and she is in no acute distress. Laboratory investigations show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to bilateral hand and wrist pain that has persisted for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. She has noticed swelling and stiffness in both hands and difficulty gripping objects. She has developed subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her elbows. She denies any recent trauma. Her past medical history is notable for one episode of uveitis two years ago. Her mother has a history of autoimmune thyroiditis. On examination, there is ulnar deviation of the fingers and synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of hand swelling and joint pain that has developed over the past two months. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, often lasting for more than an hour before it improves. Her mother had a history of autoimmune disease. On examination, her vital signs are stable, and there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serological tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of her hands show marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. She describes stiffness in her fingers and wrists that is worse in the morning and lasts for about 2 hours. She has difficulty gripping objects and says her wedding ring no longer fits due to swelling in her hands. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints, metacarpophalangeal joints, and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 10.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 80 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour and reports recent onset of small, firm nodules over her elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and limited range of motion in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain that has progressively worsened. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities. She denies any recent trauma. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Firm, flesh-colored subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She reports that she has been experiencing increasing weakness in her legs, which has made it difficult for her to climb stairs or stand for long periods. She also mentions having generalized fatigue and occasional joint discomfort in her knees and wrists. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medications. The patient does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, she has reduced muscle strength in both lower extremities, particularly in the proximal muscles. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetrical throughout, and sensation is intact. Laboratory tests show a mild anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and normal thyroid function tests. Electromyography reveals evidence of myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain lasting over a year, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports difficulty in performing daily activities, such as dressing and typing. She has noticed small, firm nodules under the skin at her elbows. Her medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease, managed with lisinopril and omeprazole, respectively. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, along with subcutaneous nodules at the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory workup shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of joint pain affecting her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as being worse in the mornings, lasting about an hour, and improving as the day progresses. She has also noticed some swelling in her fingers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She works as a graphic designer and reports no recent changes in her work routine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. A hand X-ray shows marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is most likely related to this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to joint pain in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves as the day progresses. She has difficulty buttoning her clothes in the morning due to stiffness and pain. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Rheumatoid factor is also positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and the joint pain is in her hands, wrists, and knees, symmetrically. She has noted difficulty with household tasks due to pain. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and there is no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, her hands and wrists are warm and tender with swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 11.8 g/dL, a normocytic mean corpuscular volume, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is most likely to be associated with this patient's condition?

options.D: Low C3 and C4 complement levels

question:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her lower legs and blood in her urine. She notes that the rash appeared shortly after she recovered from an upper respiratory infection. She describes the rash as purple spots that do not blanch with pressure. Additionally, she reports joint pain in her ankles and knees, but no significant swelling. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent travel. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on her shins and mild swelling of the ankles. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin: 13.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 7,500/mm3
Platelet count: 250,000/mm3
Blood urea nitrogen: 18 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL
Urinalysis shows red blood cells and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of diffuse bone pain, particularly in her hips and lower back, for the past three months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, often worse at night and not relieved by over-the-counter analgesics. She has also noticed her back seems more curved. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. Her family history includes osteoporosis in her mother. She denies any recent trauma, weight loss, or fever. Her last menstrual period was at age 52. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the lumbar spine and hips but no swelling or erythema of the joints. Laboratory studies reveal:

Alkaline phosphatase: 320 U/L (elevated)
Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL
Phosphate: 3.5 mg/dL
25-hydroxyvitamin D: 26 ng/mL

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings. She notes that her hands and wrists are most affected, with difficulty in gripping objects. She denies any recent trauma or significant medical history. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with subluxation and ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with several months of joint pain in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity as the day progresses. She denies any skin rashes, photosensitivity, or recent infections. Her medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Her medications include lisinopril and omeprazole. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old female presents to her primary care clinic with 4 months of worsening fatigue and joint pain affecting her wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in the affected joints. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and she is a non-smoker. Physical examination reveals warmth, swelling, and tenderness over the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show normocytic anemia, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 75 mm/hr, and a negative rheumatoid factor test. A radiograph of her hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman, who is 24 weeks pregnant, presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back and pelvic pain for the past 3 weeks. She describes the pain as dull and throbbing, worse when standing for prolonged periods or after physical activity. She denies any trauma. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications except prenatal vitamins. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated so far. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion of the lumbar spine. The fetal heart rate is 140 beats per minute. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and electrolytes. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is mildly elevated at 35 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for her condition?

options.B: Initiate physical therapy focused on pelvic stabilization exercises
options.C: Recommend bed rest and activity restriction
options.D: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.F: Advise use of a maternity support belt

question: A 55-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness and swelling in her hands and wrists, lasting over an hour each morning. She also notes fatigue and intermittent fevers. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She denies recent infections or travel. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, with sparing of the DIP joints. Vital signs include temperature 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure 132/85 mmHg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 8.2 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman is evaluated for several months of persistent joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has noticed small, firm lumps under her skin near her elbow. Her mother had a history of similar joint issues. Vital signs are stable. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the arms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman comes to the emergency department due to sudden onset severe back pain after bending down to pick up a light object. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the lower thoracic region. She has a history of osteoporosis and takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. She also mentions occasional morning stiffness in her hands and knees, but no significant swelling or redness. Her medications include alendronate and an over-the-counter multivitamin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T11 vertebra and limited range of motion in the spine due to pain. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory studies show a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). X-rays of the thoracic spine reveal a compression fracture at T11. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. The stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and lasts for about an hour before improving slightly with activity. She reports fatigue and occasional fevers but denies any recent infections or trauma. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. There is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Blood tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An autoantibody panel is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the rheumatology clinic with pain in her hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past year. She describes stiffness in the morning that lasts for about an hour and improves with movement throughout the day. She reports that over-the-counter pain medications provide minimal relief. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine daily. On examination, her vital signs are stable. She has symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints with decreased range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling following a weekend hiking trip. She describes the pain as sharp and rated 7 out of 10 in severity. She denies any trauma or falls. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medications. On examination, her temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 124/76 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. The right knee is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation with limited range of motion due to pain. The left knee and other joints are unremarkable. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers. Synovial fluid analysis reveals increased white blood cells, but no crystals or organisms are seen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notices swelling and tenderness in her joints. She also mentions feeling fatigued more than usual. She has no significant past medical history and is a non-smoker. On examination, there are subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. The metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints are swollen and tender to palpation. Laboratory investigations reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A hand X-ray demonstrates joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent nasal congestion, chronic headaches, and a worsening cough over the past 6 months. She also notes increased fatigue and joint stiffness in the mornings. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and she reports no allergies. Recently, she experienced hematuria and has had a decrease in urine volume. On physical examination, there is bilateral tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and scattered crackles heard on lung auscultation. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 65 mm/hr. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosing this patient's underlying condition?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She describes stiffness in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning, lasting about 2 hours before improving. She also notes fatigue and occasionally low-grade fevers. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies weight loss or gastrointestinal symptoms. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with no involvement of the distal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain and stiffness for the past 4 months. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for about 2 hours before improving. The pain primarily affects her wrists, hands, and knees symmetrically. She has a family history of rheumatoid arthritis. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, warm, and tender bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past six months. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and reports difficulty in performing tasks that require fine motor skills. On examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the presence of subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the pain is primarily in her hands and wrists and is worse in the mornings, often lasting over an hour before she feels relief. Her past medical history includes hypertension and seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show positive anti-citrullinated protein antibodies and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of low back pain and stiffness that is worse in the mornings and improves with exercise. She reports that the pain sometimes wakes her up during the second half of the night. She denies any trauma, but notes that her father has 'some kind of arthritis.' On examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old female presents to the dermatologist with complaints of skin changes in her hands over the past year. She describes a tightening sensation in the skin of her fingers and has noticed that they become white and painful in response to cold weather. Additionally, she reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. She denies any skin rash elsewhere on her body. Physical examination reveals thickened skin over her fingers and a distinct loss of skin folds. Nailfold capillary examination shows dilated capillary loops. Laboratory tests show a positive ANA and negative anti-CCP. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of numbness and tingling in her hands and feet for the past 3 months. She describes the sensation as pins and needles, which sometimes wakes her up at night. She also notes occasional muscle cramping and fatigue. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus for 10 years, which is managed with metformin. On examination, her blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in her distal extremities. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin A1c level of 8.5%. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and finds it difficult to perform daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with subcutaneous nodules palpable at the elbows. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Refer to a rheumatologist for further management
options.E: Order an MRI of the hands
options.G: Prescribe physical therapy for hand exercises

question: A 52-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has persisted over the past two years. The pain is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves throughout the day. She also reports noticing small, firm, subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism. Medications include lisinopril and levothyroxine. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with limited range of motion. Laboratory findings show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old girl is brought to the pediatrician by her mother because of a purplish rash on her legs and blood in her urine over the past week. The mother reports that the child had a sore throat and mild fever previously, which resolved without treatment. On examination, the patient has several palpable purpuric lesions scattered over both lower extremities. She reports mild abdominal pain but denies any recent vomiting or diarrhea. There is no history of trauma. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria, with normal renal function tests. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with recurrent episodes of fever and generalized lymphadenopathy over the past three months. She reports intermittent joint pain in her fingers and knees, accompanied by morning stiffness that resolves within an hour. Her medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections. She denies any weight loss, night sweats, or recent travel. On examination, she appears mildly ill with palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Her temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count 15,200/mm3
Neutrophils 70%
Lymphocytes 20%
Monocytes 8%
Eosinophils 2%
C-reactive protein 15 mg/L
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening over her fingers and hands that has been progressively worsening over the past 8 months. She also reports experiencing episodes of color changes in her fingers when exposed to cold, turning white, then blue, and finally red upon rewarming. She occasionally experiences joint pain, but there is no significant morning stiffness. She has been feeling more fatigued recently. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Family history is notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is noticeable sclerodactyly, and her fingers appear shiny and taut. Nailfold capillary examination shows dropouts. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer of 1:160 with a speckled pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of rash and blood in her urine. She describes the rash as small, red to purple spots primarily on her lower legs. She also reports mild joint pain and fatigue but denies fever or recent infections. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, there is palpable purpura on her lower extremities, but no synovial swelling in her joints. Vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests show hematuria and mild proteinuria, but no casts. Blood tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated ESR, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, stiffness, and swelling. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting over an hour before she can move her fingers freely. She also reports the presence of small nodules under the skin on her elbows. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable on both elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions at several joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports pain and swelling in her hands and wrists that is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She has also noticed some generalized tiredness and occasional low-grade fever. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia with a hematocrit of 32% and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. A rheumatoid factor test returns positive. Which of the following conditions best explains this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with increasing pain and swelling in her hands and wrists over the past 8 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with visible ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with increasing pain and stiffness in her fingers and wrists over the past 2 years. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and lasts at least an hour. The patient says that she often feels fatigued and has noticed swelling in her hands. She denies any recent illness or trauma. Medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of swelling and pain in her wrists, knees, and ankles for the past week. She returned from a vacation in the Caribbean 10 days ago. She also reports a low-grade fever and fatigue. She has no history of similar symptoms in the past. On examination, there is synovitis of the wrists and symmetric swelling of the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests reveal a mild leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. Antinuclear antibody test is negative. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of symmetric swelling and pain in her hands and wrists, worse in the morning. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history is unremarkable except for mild asthma managed with inhaled corticosteroids. She denies any recent trauma or infection. On examination, she has tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic due to right shoulder pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She reports difficulty reaching overhead and combing her hair. The pain is often worse at night, and she feels some relief when her arm is supported. She denies any significant trauma to the shoulder. Past medical history is notable for hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is a limited range of motion in all directions at the right shoulder, with marked pain at the extremes. No swelling or erythema is noted. Laboratory tests reveal normal ESR and CRP levels, and rheumatoid factor is negative. X-rays of the shoulder show no acute bony abnormalities. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has been gradually worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a deep ache that is exacerbated by prolonged sitting and standing, and it occasionally radiates to her groin and thighs. She also reports morning stiffness lasting for about 15 minutes. Her medical history is notable for obesity and hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any history of trauma or recent infections. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is tenderness upon palpation over the hip joints, and the range of motion is slightly restricted. Laboratory tests reveal normal ESR and CRP levels. X-rays of the hips show loss of joint space and osteophyte formation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral heads

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a worsening rash on her legs and episodes of chest pain for the past two weeks. She describes the rash as a purplish, net-like pattern on her thighs and lower legs. She also reports two episodes of chest pain that resolved spontaneously after 15 minutes. Her past medical history is significant for recurrent miscarriages and she takes oral contraceptives for polycystic ovarian syndrome. On physical examination, she has a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 130/85 mmHg, pulse of 88/min, and respirations of 16/min. The rash is consistent with livedo reticularis. Laboratory tests reveal a platelet count of 110,000/mm³ and a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT). What is the most likely underlying diagnosis causing this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist with concerns about her worsening joint symptoms. She reports that her fingers have become increasingly deformed over the last year, with persistent pain and morning stiffness lasting more than an hour each day. She has difficulty with daily activities such as buttoning her shirt and holding a pen. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism. She is currently on lisinopril and levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints with swan-neck deformities. Laboratory tests show positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. She reports stiffness in her hands and knees, especially in the morning, which lasts for a few hours. She denies any recent travel, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She works as a software engineer and is a non-smoker. Vital signs are: temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, heart rate 82/min, and respirations 14/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the bilateral MCP and PIP joints. Laboratory tests show:

Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 8,200/mm3
Platelet count: 250,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 48 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor: positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies: positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and swelling in both her hands and wrists for the past 2 years. She notices morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has difficulty gripping objects. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated on her elbows. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are detected. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain that has progressively worsened over the past year. She describes the pain as dull and aching, with stiffness that is most severe in the morning, improving as the day progresses. She reports occasional fatigue but denies any joint swelling or redness. She has a history of psoriasis, which has been well-controlled with topical treatments. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility, particularly in the lumbar region. There is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints but no peripheral joint involvement. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An x-ray of the pelvis is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 2-week history of weakness in her right foot and difficulty walking. She describes a sensation of tripping over her right foot, especially on uneven surfaces. She also reports a tingling sensation in her right leg but denies back pain, fever, or trauma. Her medical history is significant for mild bilateral knee pain that she attributes to her daily running routine. She is otherwise healthy and does not take any medications. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the right leg. Reflexes are normal, and there is no swelling or tenderness of the joints. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to a rash and blood in her urine. The rash appeared two days ago and is located on her lower legs and buttocks. She also reports abdominal pain and mild swelling in her knees over the past week. Her mother mentions that the patient had a mild upper respiratory infection two weeks ago. The patient's temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. On physical examination, there are palpable purpura on the lower extremities and mild swelling of the knees. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 33-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past month. She reports stiffness in her hands and wrists that is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She denies fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands and bilateral wrist swelling. Her laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr, and normal renal function. A rheumatoid factor test is ordered and returns as positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of difficulty walking and progressive weakness in her left foot. She describes tripping frequently due to an inability to lift the foot properly. She denies any recent trauma or significant past medical history. Her job as a software developer involves long hours of sitting, and she reports some mild back discomfort. Physical examination reveals decreased dorsiflexion of the left foot with normal sensation, but there is muscle weakness noted in the left anterior compartment of the lower leg. The left Achilles reflex is diminished compared to the right. There is no joint swelling or erythema, and her gait is a steppage type. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue, generalized weakness, and joint pain. She reports the joint pain is episodic, involving her wrists and knees, and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She also describes easy bruising and frequent nosebleeds over the past few weeks. Her medical history is unremarkable. She is not on any medications except for occasional acetaminophen for pain. On physical examination, she appears pale and has petechiae on her lower extremities. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin: 8 g/dL, Leukocyte count: 2,500/mm^3, Platelet count: 70,000/mm^3, Serum: Na+: 138 mEq/L, Cl-: 100 mEq/L, K+: 4.6 mEq/L, HCO3-: 22 mEq/L, BUN: 20 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL. Bone marrow biopsy is performed, revealing hypocellular marrow with decreased hematopoietic cells. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis for her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent joint pain and swelling, predominantly affecting her wrists and fingers. She notes that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting over an hour. She denies any recent trauma or overuse. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3, Platelet count 250,000/mm3. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. The pain is associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour and improves as the day progresses. She reports difficulty in performing daily activities due to pain and swelling in her fingers. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, and several small subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with complaints of joint pain in her hands and wrists that has persisted for the last 4 months. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, accompanied by stiffness that lasts for about an hour. She also reports occasional swelling in her fingers but denies any history of trauma to the joints. She notes that her symptoms improve as the day progresses. Past medical history is significant for a family history of autoimmune diseases. She currently takes NSAIDs with partial relief. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally with some warmth over the joints. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and improves throughout the day. She has also noticed swelling in her finger joints and occasional fatigue. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, with her mother having been diagnosed with lupus. Her past medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. A rheumatoid factor test is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain. She reports that the joint pain initially started in her ankles and knees and has now spread to her wrists. She also mentions feeling fatigued and having a low-grade fever. She denies any recent travel, new medications, or change in appetite. Physical examination reveals multiple tender, erythematous nodules on both shins and mild swelling in her wrists and knees. Her vital signs are stable, and there is no rash or joint deformity. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.H: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to difficulty walking and a sensation of weakness in her right foot that started 3 weeks ago. She mentions that she has been tripping over her right foot and finds it difficult to lift her toes while walking. Additionally, she reports mild lower back pain that has occasionally radiated down her right leg. She has a history of occasional fatigue and morning stiffness in her hands, which resolves after about an hour. She denies any numbness or tingling in her feet or any recent injuries. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 118/78 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Neurological examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and a high-stepping gait. Reflexes are 2+ throughout, and sensation is intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's foot drop?

A 52-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with concerns about skin changes and episodes of color changes in her fingers. She reports that her fingers turn white and then blue in response to cold or stress, followed by redness and tingling upon rewarming. These episodes have been occurring for the past year. In addition to these symptoms, she notes that the skin on her fingers has become tight and shiny. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, the skin over her fingers and hands appears taut with reduced mobility. Capillary nailfold examination reveals dilated capillaries. Basic laboratory tests show mild anemia but are otherwise unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis in this patient?

A 54-year-old female presents to the clinic with a two-year history of persistent bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has noticed small, firm nodules on her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for controlled hypertension. She denies recent infections or new medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, as well as several subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers and a positive rheumatoid factor test. An X-ray of her hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain in her hips and legs for the past 6 months. She denies any recent trauma and has no significant past medical history except for hypertension, which is controlled with lisinopril. She has been experiencing fatigue and some difficulty walking due to the pain. On physical examination, she has tenderness on palpation over the pelvis and femur. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, normal calcium and phosphate levels, and no significant changes in liver function tests. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of persistent fever, fatigue, and generalized malaise. She reports intermittent headaches and occasional joint stiffness in the mornings that improves throughout the day. She has no significant past medical history. Her vital signs include: temperature 101.8°F (38.8°C), blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, heart rate 92/min, and respirations 16/min. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 100 mm/hr and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 9 mg/dL. Blood cultures are pending. Physical examination reveals no obvious source of infection. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Conduct a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
options.F: Refer for a temporal artery biopsy

question: A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing her daily activities due to pain. She has noticed slight weight loss and occasional fevers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody tests are positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities due to pain in her hands and wrists. There is no history of trauma. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels are elevated. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions on the ulnar side of several metacarpal heads. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists that has persisted for several months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving throughout the day. Her past medical history includes mild hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is evidence of swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her laboratory workup reveals a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show soft-tissue swelling along with joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 2-month history of persistent swelling in her neck and under her arms. She also reports feeling increasingly fatigued, experiencing occasional night sweats, and has unintentionally lost 5 kg (11 lb) over the past three months. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections, travel, or sick contacts. On physical examination, there are multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,700/mm3
Platelet count 310,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 42 mm/h
Serum
Glucose 94 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL
AST 16 U/L
ALT 18 U/L
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Administer empirical antibiotics for possible infection
options.B: Order a chest X-ray to evaluate for thoracic lymphadenopathy
options.C: Schedule a follow-up appointment in 6 months
options.D: Perform an excisional lymph node biopsy
options.E: Start prednisone for suspected autoimmune disease
options.F: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question:
A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness that is more pronounced in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving. She reports that the pain affects her wrists, knees, and the small joints of her hands. Her symptoms began approximately six months ago and have gradually worsened. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. On physical examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal anemia of chronic disease and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and stiffness in her hands and wrists that has persisted for the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving as the day progresses. She denies any recent infections, trauma, or fever. Her medical history includes hypertension and seasonal allergies. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,900/mm3
Platelet count 290,000/mm3
ESR 60 mm/h
CRP 15 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-CCP antibody positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a complaint of symmetric joint swelling and pain in both hands and wrists over the past two months. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day. She also reports occasional fatigue and low-grade fevers. Her medical history is significant for asthma, which is well-controlled with an inhaler. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show: ESR: 42 mm/hr, CRP: 15 mg/L, Positive Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (Anti-CCP) antibody, and rheumatoid factor: 120 IU/mL. Radiographs of the hands show mild joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?

A 28-year-old female visits her family physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain over the past three months. She notes that the stiffness in her hands is most pronounced upon waking and gradually improves as the day progresses. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, she appears pale and has mild tenderness and swelling in the small joints of her hands. Laboratory investigation reveals Hb 9.5 g/dL, WBC 3.8 x 10^9/L, and platelets 110 x 10^9/L. Liver and renal function tests are normal. Further testing shows a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) at a low titer, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely next step to confirm a diagnosis in this patient?

A 29-year-old woman visits her doctor complaining of joint pain in her hands and feet for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and seems to improve as the day progresses. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. She takes no regular medications. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, tender, and warm bilaterally. There are no skin rashes or nodules. Laboratory studies show a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide test. Which of the following is most likely to be found in this patient?

options.D: Low C3 and C4 complement levels
options.F: High levels of serum creatine kinase

question: A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hand pain and swelling for the past 3 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has difficulty performing daily tasks that involve gripping or lifting objects. Her medical history includes asthma, for which she uses an inhaler occasionally. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. She also mentions swelling and discomfort around her jaw area, which she attributes to her parotid glands. She denies any significant joint pain but reports feeling more fatigued than usual. She has a history of hypertension well-controlled on lisinopril. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including her mother who had lupus. On examination, her vital signs are normal, but there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Schirmer's test shows reduced tear production. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive ANA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour in her wrists, knees, and several small joints of her hands. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She denies any recent infections or travel. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory results show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 8,000/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of increasing joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning her clothes. She has a history of chronic fatigue and has noticed some swelling in her fingers. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory workup reveals elevated inflammatory markers and serology tests positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions at the affected joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and a rash on her face. She describes the rash as appearing after spending time in the sun and notes that it resembles a butterfly shape over her cheeks and nose. She reports joint pain primarily in her hands and knees, which is often worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies any recent infections or significant changes in her health but mentions feeling increasingly fatigued. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, she has a malar rash and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies and an ESR of 30 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic neck pain and tingling sensation in her arms for the past 5 months. The symptoms are worse at the end of the day, especially after working long hours on her computer. She also reports occasional headaches and stiffness in her shoulders, but denies any fever, weight loss, or significant joint swelling. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the cervical paraspinal muscles and reduced range of motion in the neck. Neurological examination shows intact strength and reflexes in the upper extremities, but decreased sensation to light touch in the fingers. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and thyroid function tests. MRI of the cervical spine shows mild cervical spondylosis with foraminal narrowing at C5-C6. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of intermittent fever and generalized fatigue.
