She reports that the fever spikes occur daily and are accompanied by chills and night sweats. She denies any recent travel, insect bites, or sick contacts. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. She has also experienced unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds and occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 102.2°F (39°C), blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse of 88/min, and respiratory rate of 16/min. There is mild tenderness in the joints without visible erythema or swelling. Laboratory findings show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after tripping over her dog and landing on her outstretched hand. She reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist area. She denies any numbness or tingling in the fingers but mentions having occasional morning stiffness in her joints for the past few months. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the distal radius with a limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered. Which of the following is most likely to be found?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting approximately 2 hours after waking. She has also experienced fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is synovial swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with tenderness on palpation. The joints are warm but not erythematous. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. ESR and CRP are elevated. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain. She describes stiffness in her fingers and wrists that is worse in the morning and lasts for more than an hour. She also reports swelling in these joints. She denies fever, skin rashes, or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 11 g/dL, indicating normocytic anemia, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent bilateral hand and wrist pain, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She also notes firm, painless nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable on her forearms. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent fatigue and diffuse bone pain over the past few months. She reports that the pain is most noticeable in her lower back and legs. Recently, she has also experienced nausea and has been feeling unusually thirsty. On review of systems, she denies joint swelling or morning stiffness. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and she takes lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On examination, she is afebrile, and vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.2 mg/dL and a parathyroid hormone level of 80 pg/mL. X-rays show subperiosteal bone resorption in the phalanges. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting her wrists and hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and feeling fatigued. She has a family history of thyroid disease and type 1 diabetes but no personal history of these conditions. On examination, her wrists, MCP, and PIP joints are warm and swollen bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP, positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-CCP antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows soft tissue swelling and early joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She notices stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour, improving as the day progresses. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serological testing shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Which of the following is most likely associated with this patient's condition?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of severe headaches, fatigue, and swelling of her ankles. She reports that she has felt more tired than usual and has been experiencing occasional joint pain, particularly in her fingers and wrists. Her medical history is significant for asthma, controlled with inhalers. She denies any skin rashes, oral ulcers, or fever, and reports no changes to her medications. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. She has periorbital edema and 2+ pitting edema in her lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine and mild proteinuria. Blood tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal complements. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She has noticed that the rash covers the bridge of her nose and cheeks but spares the nasolabial folds. Additionally, she reports intermittent mouth ulcers that make eating uncomfortable. She denies any recent travel or new medications. Her medical history is significant for recurrent episodes of joint pain involving the wrists and knees but no history of trauma or swelling. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Vital signs are stable. Physical examination reveals a malar rash and tenderness in the wrists and knees without swelling. Laboratory tests are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint swelling and stiffness in both hands for the past 3 months. She notes that the stiffness is especially pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory findings are as follows:

Complete blood count:
Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Leukocytes: 7,200/mm^3
Platelets: 250,000/mm^3

Inflammatory markers:
ESR: 55 mm/hr
CRP: 10 mg/L

Autoantibody tests:
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-CCP: Positive
ANA: Negative

Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past month. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which gradually improves as she moves around during the day. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are positive. Radiographs of her hands reveal juxta-articular osteopenia and no erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and several fingers, particularly in the morning, subsiding as the day progresses. She denies any recent injury or fever. Her family history is notable for a sister with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman visits her physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been gradually worsening over the past 6 months. The pain is described as a symmetric polyarthritis affecting her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands. She reports morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour and fatigue throughout the day. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she is taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 58 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing tasks like buttoning her clothes. She has also noticed small, firm nodules on her elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies recent fevers, weight loss, or rash. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for about two hours, and improving throughout the day. She has noticed small, firm nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and she has no known drug allergies. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with limited range of motion. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated on the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly in her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity as the day progresses. She has difficulty opening jars and reports fatigue. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, tender, and warm. Laboratory findings show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, nasal congestion, and a cough producing blood-tinged sputum. She also reports experiencing joint pain and swelling predominantly in her wrists and small joints of her hands. Over the past month, she has noticed increasing difficulty with breathing on exertion and swelling around her eyes and ankles. Her medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections. On examination, her blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 20/min. She has bilateral crackles on lung auscultation and periorbital edema. Laboratories reveal an elevated serum creatinine level. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her lower legs and intermittent joint pain. She describes the rash as small, red spots that do not blanch with pressure. She also reports blood-tinged urine and mild abdominal pain. She denies any recent illnesses or new medications and has no history of kidney problems. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on her lower extremities and mild swelling of her knees. Lab results show hematuria and mild proteinuria. Her vitals show a blood pressure of 120/78 mm Hg, a heart rate of 76/min, and a temperature of 99.1°F (37.3°C). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting several hours. She notes that her symptoms improve slightly with activity but worsen significantly by the end of the day. She also reports fatigue and weight loss over the past few months. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, and an X-ray of the hands demonstrates juxta-articular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both wrists and hands for the past 3 months. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has also experienced fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies and rheumatoid factor are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with worsening joint pain and stiffness over the past year. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and can last for over an hour. She reports swelling and tenderness in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. She also notes recent difficulty opening jars and occasionally drops objects. She has a history of hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, ulnar deviation and swan neck deformities of the fingers are noted. Her laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibody. X-rays of her hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain, swelling, and stiffness in both hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve with activity as the day progresses. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory investigations reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her anti-nuclear antibodies are positive, and she is found to have a high rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse bone pain, fatigue, and recurrent episodes of constipation. She has a history of hypertension and osteopenia for which she takes hydrochlorothiazide and calcium supplements, respectively. Her mother had osteoporosis, and her sister has rheumatoid arthritis. Laboratory tests show a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL and a parathyroid hormone level of 98 pg/mL. Her serum creatinine and complete blood count are within normal limits. On examination, tenderness is noted over her sternum and lower back. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to hand pain and deformity that has been worsening over the past 3 years. She reports stiffness in her fingers every morning that lasts for over an hour before subsiding. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers and swan-neck deformities of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 22-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain for the past 3 weeks. She describes the pain as migratory, affecting different joints at different times, including her knees, wrists, and ankles. The pain is accompanied by swelling and warmth over the affected joints. She mentions a sore throat a month ago, but she did not seek medical attention at that time. She denies any recent travel or changes in medication but reports increased fatigue. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 110/72 mmHg, pulse is 84/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, she appears well but has mild swelling and tenderness in her wrists and knees. Laboratory testing reveals elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with swelling and pain in both hands that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and notes that her symptoms improve as the day progresses. She has fatigue and has recently noticed nodules on her elbows. Her medical history is unremarkable. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, White blood cell count 7,200/mm³, Platelet count 250,000/mm³. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is 45 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein (CRP) is 25 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy. She notes intermittent joint pain, particularly in her knees and wrists, but reports no morning stiffness. She has a history of frequent sore throats but denies recent infections. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 118/75 mm Hg. On examination, multiple enlarged cervical and axillary lymph nodes are palpated. The nodes are non-tender and mobile. She has mild splenomegaly but no hepatomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal a leukocyte count of 11,000/mm3 with 60% neutrophils, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of stiffness and swelling in both hands for the past two months. She notes the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, sometimes lasting over an hour. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes per day for 30 years. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally, with warmth but no erythema. Laboratory results show:

- Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 45 mm/h
- C-reactive protein: 12 mg/L
- Rheumatoid factor: positive
- Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies: positive

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain in her hands and wrists for the past 18 months. She reports that the pain is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and osteoarthritis. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive hand deformity and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports difficulty in performing daily activities due to joint pain and swelling, especially in the wrists and fingers. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (anti-CCP). X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She reports swelling and deformity in her fingers, particularly in the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints. She also mentions fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. She denies any recent infections or significant weight loss. Physical examination reveals synovitis with ulnar deviation of the hands and tenderness at the wrists and knees. Laboratory findings show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detected. Hand X-rays demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman visits the clinic with a complaint of recurrent painful oral ulcers for the past 8 months. She reports having similar ulcers on her genital area that resolve spontaneously but keep recurring. She also mentions having episodes of joint pain affecting her knees and ankles accompanied by fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraine headaches. She denies any history of sexually transmitted infections. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis, and her older sister has psoriasis. On examination, there are multiple aphthous ulcers in her oral cavity and a healing ulcer on her left labia. No skin rashes are noted on her extremities or trunk. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory findings reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her hands, for the last 8 months. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about an hour before improving as the day progresses. She has also noticed swelling in her fingers and wrists. Her medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or overuse of her hands. She works as a librarian and reports difficulty with gripping books and typing. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 4 months. She reports that the pain is symmetric and affects her hands and wrists, and it is worse in the morning, lasting about 2 hours before easing somewhat. She also notes occasional low-grade fevers and has noticed some weight loss despite a normal appetite. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she is taking levothyroxine. On physical examination, she has tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and a positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as opening jars and turning doorknobs. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, managed with lisinopril and levothyroxine respectively. Physical examination reveals bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with slight ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Hand X-rays demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes a burning sensation in her soles, particularly at night, and states that her symptoms worsen with activity. She reports occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, which she attributes to her job as a data entry specialist. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or changes in vision. She has a family history of diabetes mellitus. On examination, she has decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities. Her vital signs are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her hands and wrists. She notes that her symptoms are most severe in the morning, lasting for about 2 hours before improving throughout the day. She describes swelling and occasional warmth over the affected joints. She has been taking ibuprofen with partial relief. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation and nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions.

What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of recurrent fever, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. She also notes diffuse joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about an hour, but she reports no significant joint swelling. Her past medical history is significant for recurrent urinary tract infections. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals low-grade fever of 100.8°F (38.2°C) and mild tenderness of the wrists and ankles without swelling. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. Blood cultures are pending. Further imaging studies show no specific abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with fatigue and joint pain over the past six months. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles, and notes stiffness that is worse in the morning and lasts for more than an hour. Her medical history is significant for a cesarean section three years ago and seasonal allergies. She denies any recent infections or travel. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits, but she has swelling and tenderness in multiple metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a normal complete metabolic panel. Rheumatoid factor is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 4-month history of worsening pain and stiffness in both of her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning shirts and opening jars. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is synovitis in both wrists and several metacarpophalangeal joints. Palpable subcutaneous nodules are present over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful red nodules on her shins and joint pain. She describes the joint pain as migratory, affecting her knees, wrists, and ankles. The nodules are tender and erythematous and have been increasing in number. She reports recent malaise and low-grade fevers but denies eye pain or respiratory symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medications. Physical examination reveals multiple erythematous nodules on her lower extremities and tenderness of the affected joints without swelling or warmth. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes the joint pain as bilateral and symmetric, mainly affecting her wrists, hands, and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender to palpation. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and recurrent sinus infections. She also reports a persistent cough and hematuria over the last few weeks. She has a history of allergic rhinitis and seasonal asthma. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), blood pressure is 135/85 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 20/min. Auscultation of the lungs reveals bilateral crackles, and there is mild tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 10,000/mm3, and an elevated C-reactive protein level. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral patchy infiltrates. Urinalysis is positive for red blood cell casts and proteinuria. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA) are positive with a cytoplasmic pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers. She also reports a history of intermittent joint pain, particularly in her knees and ankles. The joint pain is often accompanied by swelling and stiffness lasting for about 30 minutes in the morning. The patient has no significant past medical history and is not taking any regular medication. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, there are multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and erythematous lesions on the genitalia. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg. Laboratory tests show a leukocyte count of 8,500/mm³, hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 30 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and diffuse bone pain over the past several months. She describes the pain as a deep ache, especially in her hips and spine, which worsens with activity. She has a history of hypertension and hypercholesterolemia, and her medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies recent trauma or any significant change in her physical activity. On further questioning, she reports occasional nausea and constipation. She has no family history of autoimmune diseases. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness on palpation over the lumbar spine and pelvic girdle. Laboratory results show:
Serum calcium 11.5 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL)
Phosphate 2.5 mg/dL (normal: 2.5-4.5 mg/dL)
Parathyroid hormone 85 pg/mL (normal: 15-65 pg/mL)
Alkaline phosphatase 140 U/L (normal: 44-147 U/L)
25-hydroxyvitamin D 15 ng/mL (normal: 20-50 ng/mL)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of tingling and burning sensations in her feet, which have gradually worsened. She describes the sensation as feeling like needles are pricking her feet, especially at night. She also reports occasional numbness in her toes and difficulty in feeling the ground while walking. She denies any trauma to the area but has had intermittent joint pain and morning stiffness in her hands for the past few years. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is managed with metformin. On examination, her feet are warm with diminished sensation to light touch and vibration. Ankle reflexes are reduced bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, fasting blood glucose of 140 mg/dL, and HbA1c of 7.8%. What is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and unintended weight loss of about 7 kg (15 lbs). She also reports occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, but denies any significant swelling. Her medical history includes hypertension and she takes lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol socially. On examination, she appears pale and has a temperature of 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure of 120/76 mm Hg, and heart rate of 88/min. Her abdomen is soft with no organomegaly, and there is no lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 82 fL, and normal white blood cell and platelet counts. A serum ferritin is low. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue and a 10-pound unintentional weight loss. She also reports occasional joint pain, particularly in the mornings, but denies swelling. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears pale, and her vital signs are within normal ranges. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume 72 fL
Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3
Platelet count 290,000/mm3
Serum
Ferritin 8 ng/mL
Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL
AST 22 U/L
ALT 24 U/L
What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent nasal congestion and facial pain for the past 3 months. She reports recent onset of shortness of breath and a productive cough with streaks of blood. She also mentions fatigue and joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings. Her past medical history is significant for recurrent ear infections and she states that she has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the past 6 weeks. Physical examination reveals bilateral wheezing and tenderness over her maxillary sinuses. A urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following laboratory tests would be most helpful in confirming the underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and skin lesions. She reports stiffness in the morning that improves with movement, and notices that her fingers and toes are swollen, especially on the right side. She also has scaly, red patches on her scalp and knees that are itchy and occasionally bleed when scratched. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Her father has a history of psoriasis. She takes ibuprofen occasionally but denies any regular medications. Physical examination shows asymmetric swelling of the distal interphalangeal joints and scaly plaques on the scalp and knees. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 8 months of gradually worsening bilateral hip pain. She describes the pain as dull and aching, worse with activity, and somewhat relieved by rest. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion and tenderness in both hips, but no swelling or erythema. The patient's gait is slightly antalgic. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of the pelvis reveal joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation at both acetabulofemoral joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain affecting both her hands, wrists, and knees. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, taking more than an hour to improve, and it is associated with swelling and stiffness. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/hr. The rheumatoid factor is positive at a high titer. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past 3 months. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting for several hours and has difficulty with daily activities due to her hand pain. Her medical history is notable for hypertension managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor test is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are detected. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of dry mouth and irritation in her eyes. She mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing joint pain, primarily in her wrists and knees, but denies any morning stiffness. She also notes a gradual swelling in front of her ears. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Family history is significant for her mother having lupus. On physical examination, bilateral parotid gland enlargement is observed, and there are no signs of synovitis. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and positive antinuclear antibodies. Schirmer's test is performed and shows reduced tear production. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-year history of joint pain involving her hands and wrists, associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports fatigue and has noticed subcutaneous nodules over her elbows. Her family history is significant for autoimmune conditions. She is on no medications except occasional ibuprofen for pain. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with synovitis and tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Radiographic imaging of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the physician because of generalized fatigue and joint pain in her wrists and fingers that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes stiffness in her joints, especially in the morning, lasting over an hour. She has a history of mild hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg. Physical examination shows synovial thickening and tenderness in her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, leukocyte count of 6,500/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. A rheumatoid factor assay returns positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks of gestation presents to the obstetrics clinic with complaints of persistent lower back and pelvic pain for the past month. The pain is dull and aching, often exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking, and is partially relieved by rest. She has no prior history of back injuries, and there are no neurological symptoms such as numbness or weakness. She denies fevers, rashes, or joint swelling. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated until now. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, but no signs of joint effusion or deformity in her hands and wrists. Laboratory tests show mild leukocytosis, normal C-reactive protein, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of worsening joint pain and stiffness over the last 18 months. She describes the stiffness as most pronounced in the mornings and lasting for several hours. She reports difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars and typing due to swelling and pain in her fingers. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and she is on lisinopril. She denies any fevers, rash, or recent infections. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of multiple small joints in both hands and wrists with ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. ESR and CRP levels are also elevated. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with complaints of skin tightening and color changes in her fingers that worsen when exposed to cold. She describes episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon warming, associated with mild discomfort. She also reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is noticeable thickening and tightening of the skin over her fingers, known as sclerodactyly. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) at a titer of 1:320 with a centromere pattern. Her physician suspects a connective tissue disorder.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of chronic pain in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as a dull ache with morning stiffness lasting over an hour, improving with activity throughout the day. She reports occasional swelling and warmth in her joints. She has a history of hypertension managed with hydrochlorothiazide. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and limited range of motion in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are noted over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of frequent headaches and fatigue. She has also noticed swelling in her legs and occasional shortness of breath. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her blood pressure is 165/100 mmHg, heart rate is 82/min, and she has bilateral pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory workup reveals a serum creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL (elevated from a previous level of 0.9 mg/dL six months ago) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Urinalysis shows proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Considering her clinical presentation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Initiate antihypertensive therapy with a calcium channel blocker

question: A 55-year-old woman comes to her primary care physician with complaints of ongoing pain in both her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as dull and reports swelling and stiffness, particularly in the morning lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, which is well-controlled on levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with firm subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her vital signs are normal, and she exhibits no signs of acute distress. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, weight gain, and constipation for the past 6 months. She also reports feeling cold frequently, even when others are comfortable. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals dry skin and a slight swelling in her neck region. Her vital signs are: temperature 97.8°F (36.6°C), blood pressure 130/85 mm Hg, heart rate 58/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Initial laboratory tests show a low serum sodium and elevated TSH level with low free T4. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient?

options.E: Referral to endocrinology for thyroid ultrasound

question: A 54-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain and deformity in her hands and feet. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty gripping objects. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies any fevers, rashes, or recent infections. On physical examination, the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally, with notable ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 7-month history of progressive joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 1 hour, which improves throughout the day. She has noticed deformities developing in her fingers and wrist. She has a history of hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is ulnar deviation of the fingers, swan-neck deformities, and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-week history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using her hands. She denies any fever or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr and C-reactive protein of 7 mg/L. Antinuclear antibody testing is positive at a titer of 1:160 with a speckled pattern, and rheumatoid factor is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and worsening bone pain. She reports that the pain is mostly in her lower back and hips, and sometimes radiates to her legs. She denies recent trauma but mentions increased thirst and frequent urination over the past few weeks. Her medical history includes hypertension and depression. She takes lisinopril and sertraline. Her physical examination is notable for tenderness over the lumbar spine and hips without swelling or erythema. Laboratory evaluation shows:
Calcium 11.8 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL)
Phosphate 2.6 mg/dL (normal: 2.5-4.5 mg/dL)
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) elevated
25-hydroxyvitamin D normal
Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with neck pain and numbness in her hands for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as dull and constant, worsened by movement and improved with rest. She also reports tingling and a pins-and-needles sensation in her arms, particularly after using a computer for extended periods. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for mild osteoarthritis managed with acetaminophen. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in the neck and decreased sensation to light touch in the thumb and index finger. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric in the upper extremities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,400/mm3
C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor 22 IU/mL (normal <20 IU/mL)
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: CT scan of the cervical spine

question:
A 43-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 6-month history of pain and stiffness in her wrists and fingers. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also reports several firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the forearms. Laboratory tests show: 
Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 45 mm/h
C-reactive protein: 18 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor: High
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (Anti-CCP) antibodies: Positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is particularly worse in the morning, lasting about two hours before improving with movement. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the arms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 7-day history of joint pain and swelling. She recently returned from a trip to Southeast Asia. The pain affects her wrists, ankles, and knees and is accompanied by a mild fever and fatigue. She reports no prior history of joint issues. Examination reveals warm, swollen joints with decreased range of motion. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg. Laboratory tests show a leukocyte count of 7,500/mm^3 and elevated inflammatory markers. A viral serology panel is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes that her symptoms have progressively worsened. She has noted the development of firm nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender with reduced range of motion. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

options.C: Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She reports weakness in her legs, making it difficult for her to climb stairs and stand up from a seated position. She also notes occasional numbness and tingling in her feet. She has experienced fatigue and intermittent joint pain in her hands and knees over the last year. Her medical history includes hypertension and she takes lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is noticeable muscle weakness in the lower extremities, diminished deep tendon reflexes in the knees and ankles, and decreased sensation to pinprick and temperature in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 13 g/dL, leukocytes 6,200/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 42 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness and swelling in her wrists, knees, and fingers that lasts for over an hour. She reports fatigue and has difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled on lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, fevers, or weight loss. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are tender and swollen bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h and C-reactive protein of 2.5 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody tests are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty gripping objects. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She has no known drug allergies and takes no medications. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Vital signs reveal temperature 37.2°C (99°F), pulse 85/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 122/78 mmHg. Physical examination shows symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally, with no deformities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her wrists, fingers, and knees. She notes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She reports increased fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, as her mother was diagnosed with lupus. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol sparingly. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as mild swelling of both knees. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that have been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as symmetrical, affecting her wrists, hands, and knees. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, which is well-controlled on levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.8 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL), indicative of normocytic anemia, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given the clinical and laboratory findings?

A 64-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurrent episodes of right first metatarsophalangeal joint pain and swelling over the past 5 years. The pain is severe, peaks within 24 hours, and resolves spontaneously over several days. She has noticed firm nodules forming near her elbows and on her finger joints. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She consumes wine occasionally and denies any recent trauma or fever. On examination, the affected joint is warm, erythematous, and exquisitely tender to touch. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and elevated serum uric acid level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of diffuse body aches and fatigue. She recently recovered from a viral illness characterized by fever and sore throat. She reports difficulty sleeping due to the aches and feels exhausted during the day. She denies joint swelling or redness. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals tender spots over the shoulders, neck, and lower back but no joint inflammation or fever. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old woman presents to the clinic due to progressive difficulty walking over the past two months. She describes weakness in her lower extremities that worsens by evening. She also reports occasional numbness and tingling in her feet. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Family history reveals that her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 132/84 mm Hg. Neurological examination shows 3/5 strength in the hip flexors and knee extensors bilaterally, with intact sensation and reflexes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of lower back pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also reports fatigue and occasional pain in her hips and knees. She denies any significant trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her family history is significant for a brother with ankylosing spondylitis. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited lumbar spine flexion. Laboratory tests show an elevated C-reactive protein level, but rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibody tests are negative. Pelvic X-ray reveals bilateral sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand stiffness and swelling, especially in the morning, which lasts for over an hour. She reports difficulty in performing daily tasks such as buttoning her shirt and opening jars. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and the proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about an hour. She also mentions feeling fatigued throughout the day. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her wrists and hands. She reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. She has never experienced similar symptoms before and has no significant medical history. Physical examination shows symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Antinuclear antibody testing is positive, and the rheumatoid factor is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent bone pain in her lower back and ribs that has been worsening over the past three months. She describes the pain as dull and constant, and it is not relieved by over-the-counter analgesics. She also reports increased fatigue, frequent urination, and occasional nausea. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, her blood pressure is 138/86 mmHg, pulse 82/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Lab tests reveal an elevated serum calcium level of 12.1 mg/dL (normal range: 8.5-10.2 mg/dL) and decreased serum phosphate. X-rays show osteopenia with subperiosteal bone resorption. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of dry eyes, dry mouth, and occasional swelling of the glands near her jawline. She also notes mild joint pain in her hands and wrists, which she attributes to aging, and reports feeling tired more often than usual. There is no history of fever, weight loss, or recent infections. On examination, her parotid glands are enlarged bilaterally, and there is mild tenderness over the joints of her hands without significant swelling. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with diffuse body aches and fatigue that began two weeks ago following a viral upper respiratory infection. She reports that the aches are present throughout her body but are most pronounced in her shoulders, hips, and thighs. The patient denies any significant joint swelling or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. She also notes occasional low-grade fevers and generalized malaise. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. Her vital signs are stable, and she has mild tenderness to palpation over her proximal muscles but no joint deformities or synovitis. Laboratory studies reveal normal complete blood count and normal inflammatory markers, including C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past three months. She reports that her joint pain is mostly in the wrists and knees and is associated with morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She also mentions frequent nosebleeds and bleeding gums. She denies recent infections, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for a positive family history for autoimmune diseases. On examination, she appears pale, and there is tenderness in the wrists and knees with mild swelling, but no erythema. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), leukocyte count of 2600/mm³ (reference range: 4500-11000/mm³), and platelet count of 80,000/mm³ (reference range: 150,000-350,000/mm³). Peripheral blood smear shows normocytic normochromic anemia with decreased platelets and white blood cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic sinus congestion and intermittent episodes of nasal discharge for the past 6 months. She also reports a persistent cough and shortness of breath. Recently, she has experienced increased fatigue and swelling in her ankles. She denies any fever or recent infections. The patient has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals bilateral lung crackles and mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory results indicate elevated creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. A chest X-ray shows multiple nodular infiltrates in both lungs. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressively worsening joint deformities over the past year. She describes stiffness and swelling in her hands and knees that is most severe in the morning and improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is currently taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is notable ulnar deviation of the fingers, swelling, and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Order an MRI of the hands

question: A 32-year-old female, currently 28 weeks pregnant, presents to your clinic with complaints of increasing lower back and pelvic pain over the past month. She describes the pain as dull and aching, exacerbated by prolonged standing and alleviated by rest. She denies any trauma or injury. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine, and a family history of rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and a waddling gait. She reports no morning stiffness or joint swelling. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fever, malaise, and generalized joint pain. She reports that the fevers are more pronounced in the evening and are associated with night sweats. She also mentions feeling unusually tired and having lost weight unintentionally. Her medical history is unremarkable, with no chronic conditions or recent travel. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness in the wrists and knees but no significant swelling or warmth. Laboratory tests show a leukocyte count of 11,200/mm³, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 80 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 60 mg/L. Blood cultures are negative, and a chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with episodes of severe pain in her right big toe, which occur every few months and are accompanied by swelling and redness. She mentions that the pain often wakes her from sleep and is most intense within the first 24 hours of onset. She has noticed small, white nodules on her ear lobes and elbows. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She does not consume alcohol, and her only medication is hydrochlorothiazide. On examination, the affected toe is erythematous, swollen, and exquisitely tender. Laboratory tests reveal hyperuricemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, easy bruising, and recurrent nosebleeds over the past several weeks. She also reports generalized joint pain, particularly in her knees and wrists, but denies any swelling or morning stiffness. She has no significant past medical history and is currently taking no medications. On physical examination, she appears pale, and there are petechiae noted on her lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal the following: hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), white blood cell count 2800/mm³ (reference range: 4500-11,000/mm³), and platelet count 70,000/mm³ (reference range: 150,000-350,000/mm³). Her peripheral smear shows normocytic, normochromic red blood cells and reduced platelet clumps. Additional testing is ordered to further evaluate her pancytopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of fatigue and joint pain affecting both her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting over an hour, and improving throughout the day. She denies any recent infections, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and the presence of anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to her family physician with complaints of chronic pain and swelling in her hands and wrists, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her symptoms have gradually worsened over the past two years. She also reports noticing firm nodules under the skin on her elbows. Her medical history is significant for hyperlipidemia and seasonal allergies. Current medications include atorvastatin and loratadine. On examination, there is synovitis in the bilateral wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. A radiograph of the hands demonstrates joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 2-year history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as being worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting for several hours. Over the last few months, she has also noticed firm lumps under the skin near her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or new medications. On physical examination, there is evidence of synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, along with subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of both elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and a rash that worsens with sun exposure. She reports experiencing fatigue and mouth ulcers over the past two months. She denies any fever or recent illness. Her medical history is significant for occasional migraines. She takes ibuprofen as needed for her joint pain. On examination, there is a malar rash and tenderness in the small joints of the hands and wrists without synovitis. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests reveal leukopenia and mild anemia. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on her cheeks and nose that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports joint pain predominantly affecting her wrists and knees, along with mild fatigue. She denies any recent illnesses or new medications. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. On physical examination, there is a symmetric erythematous rash over the cheeks and nasal bridge sparing the nasolabial folds. There is mild swelling and tenderness over the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 3,200/mm3, and a platelet count of 140,000/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of numbness and tingling in her hands and feet. She also reports intermittent shooting pains in her forearms and calves. Her past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes and hypothyroidism, both well-controlled with medication. She does not report any recent trauma or infections. She has been experiencing fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past few months. On physical examination, the patient has decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, but elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely contributing factor to this patient's symptoms?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers for the past several months. She also reports experiencing episodes of fingers turning white and blue when exposed to cold, which are followed by redness and throbbing pain once rewarmed. She sometimes feels joint pain and stiffness in her hands, especially in the morning, and has fatigue that worsens throughout the day. She has no significant personal medical history but mentions that her grandmother had a similar condition. She is a nonsmoker and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals sclerodactyly and decreased hand range of motion. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show mild anemia, elevated ESR, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her hands, wrists, and knees. She has a history of hypertension controlled with amlodipine and is a non-smoker. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–15.5 g/dL), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) at 45 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr), and normal liver and kidney function tests.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on this patient’s presentation?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain that has developed over the past week. She describes the nodules as initially small but becoming larger and more painful. She has a history of recent upper respiratory infection two weeks ago. She does not smoke or consume alcohol, and her family history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals multiple erythematous, tender nodules on both shins.
