There is tenderness and mild swelling in the ankles and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and CRP. A chest X-ray is ordered and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to joint pain and swelling over the past two weeks. The pain began in her knees and ankles and has since moved to her wrists and elbows. Two weeks ago, she had a sore throat and fever that resolved with over-the-counter medications. On examination, she has temperature of 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 96/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. She exhibits tenderness and swelling in several joints, but no deformities are noted. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue and a 3-month history of unintentional weight loss of 7 kg (15.4 lbs). She has also been experiencing shortness of breath on exertion and palpitations. She reports feeling unusually cold and notes that her skin has become pale. She denies any recent infections, fever, or joint pain. Her medical history includes migraine headaches and gastroesophageal reflux disease, for which she takes sumatriptan and omeprazole. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is significant for autoimmune thyroid disease in her mother. On physical examination, her conjunctivae are pale and her pulse is 105/min, with a blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 8.6 g/dL and a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 76 fL. Serum ferritin is low, and transferrin saturation is decreased. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-week history of fatigue, fever, and a rash that appeared after a camping trip. She reports joint pain in her knees and wrists. She recalls being bitten by a tick during the trip. Her temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash with a central clearing on her right thigh and tenderness in the knees and wrists. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 9,200/mm³ with a normal differential. ESR is elevated. Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-year history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain, which is accompanied by swelling and stiffness. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about 1.5 hours before improving slightly as the day progresses. On examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, and several small subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory workup shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that she notices worsens in the morning and improves with movement throughout the day. She also reports the presence of firm subcutaneous nodules over her elbows and occasional fatigue. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands are swollen and tender. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with concerns about joint deformities and persistent hand pain. She reports that for the past three years, she has experienced joint stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour and has noticed deformities developing in her fingers, which have significantly decreased her manual dexterity. She has a history of smoking and hypertension. Her current medications include lisinopril and over-the-counter ibuprofen. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swan-neck deformities, and tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand radiography shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, with pain primarily affecting her wrists and fingers. Her past medical history is significant for chronic hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent travel or insect bites. On examination, her hands show swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A hand X-ray reveals joint space narrowing and erosion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 5-month history of progressive weakness in her arms and thighs. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She feels more fatigued than usual and has noticed a few tender joints, particularly in her fingers and knees, though she denies morning stiffness. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, she has 4/5 strength in the deltoid and quadriceps muscles bilaterally and mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints without visible swelling. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm^3
Platelet count 230,000/mm^3
CK 850 U/L (elevated)
ESR 25 mm/hr
ANA negative

Considering this patient’s presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with concerns of fatigue and unintended weight loss of 10 pounds over the past 3 months. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent changes in diet or exercise routine. She also notes occasional shortness of breath and palpitations. On physical examination, she appears pale, and her conjunctivae are slightly anemic. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, hematocrit of 28%, and mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Her ferritin level is decreased. Further questioning reveals no significant joint pain or swelling, and her family history is non-contributory. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports stiffness in her joints, particularly in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving. She has noticed swelling and pain in her wrists, hands, and knees bilaterally. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with hydrochlorothiazide. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints, wrists, and knees. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume 85 fL
Leukocyte count 6,500/mm3
Platelet count 350,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor positive at 1:160

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 38-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 4-month history of joint stiffness and swelling. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning, lasting for about 2 hours before improving throughout the day. She denies any trauma or overuse of her joints. She also notes fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Family history is notable for her mother having lupus. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 35-year-old female presents with fatigue and multiple subcutaneous nodules on her forearms, particularly over the extensor surfaces. She reports generalized malaise and low-grade fevers over the past few weeks. She denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness. Physical examination reveals several firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of both arms. There is no synovitis or joint deformity noted. Laboratory tests show a mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP. Rheumatoid factor is negative, but the patient has a history of smoking. A biopsy of one of the nodules reveals granulomatous inflammation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports that her symptoms are particularly severe in the morning, with stiffness lasting more than an hour. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including her mother who has rheumatoid arthritis. The patient denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP levels, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Joint radiographs reveal juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of increasing difficulty in climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head, which started about 3 months ago. She also notes mild joint pain in her wrists and fingers that seems to be worse in the morning. She denies any numbness or tingling in her extremities. She reports fatigue and has noticed a symmetrical rash on her cheeks and eyelids. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is a non-smoker. On examination, there is proximal muscle weakness in the shoulders and hips, but no significant joint swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old African American woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of fatigue and unintended weight loss of 10 pounds. She also reports experiencing dizziness and palpitations. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional joint pain in her hands, which she attributed to overuse. She denies any changes in appetite, fever, or recent travel. She works as a bank manager and has noticed increased difficulty concentrating at work. On physical examination, she appears pale. Her vital signs show a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse of 100/min, and a respiratory rate of 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal: Hemoglobin 9 g/dL, Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 72 fL, Reticulocyte count 1%, and a positive anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with right shoulder pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. She reports difficulty lifting her arm above her head and finds it challenging to perform activities such as brushing her hair and reaching for objects on high shelves. There is no history of trauma, but she mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing mild joint stiffness in the mornings. Her medical history includes hypertension, and she is currently taking lisinopril. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a restricted range of motion in all directions. The pain is exacerbated by passive external rotation and abduction. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of fatigue and joint pain for the past 4 months. She notes that her hands and knees feel stiff, especially in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving. She reports difficulty using her hands for daily tasks due to pain and swelling. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL (normal: 12-15 g/dL), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr (normal: <20 mm/hr), and a normal mean corpuscular volume (MCV). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the office with complaints of progressive deformity of her fingers over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and significant difficulty with daily tasks such as opening jars. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and seasonal allergies. She takes lisinopril and loratadine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are present over the elbows. Laboratory studies reveal positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions at the proximal interphalangeal joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute right knee pain and swelling that started two days ago following a weekend hike. She reports that the knee pain began after a mild twisting injury while descending a trail. The pain is sharp, exacerbated by bending the knee, and she has difficulty bearing weight on the affected leg. She denies any fever or a history of inflammatory joint disease. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, her right knee is swollen with a moderate effusion, and there is tenderness along the medial joint line. The range of motion is limited due to pain, and a mild warmth is noted over the joint. Lachman test is negative. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 21-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain for the last two weeks. She describes the pain as migratory, affecting her knees, wrists, and elbows, and notes that the pain is worse in the morning. She recalls having a sore throat about three weeks ago but did not seek medical attention at that time. She denies any history of trauma or previous similar episodes. On examination, she has a temperature of 37.9°C (100.2°F), blood pressure of 110/68 mm Hg, heart rate of 76/min, and respiratory rate of 18/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling in the affected joints, but no erythema. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and normal white blood cell count. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman visits the clinic due to skin rashes and joint pain for the past 5 months. The rashes are described as red, scaly patches primarily affecting her elbows and knees. She reports joint pain in her fingers and knees, which is asymmetric in nature and tends to fluctuate in severity, often worsening after periods of inactivity. She denies morning stiffness lasting over 30 minutes. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. She is not on any regular medications, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales on extensor surfaces and tenderness in the distal interphalangeal joints of her right hand. No subcutaneous nodules are present. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of gradual onset right shoulder pain and restricted range of motion. She describes the pain as aching and worsens with overhead activities. She has difficulty putting on clothes and reaching for items on high shelves. The patient denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals limited active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder with pain on abduction and external rotation. Supraspinatus and infraspinatus strength are normal. There is no tenderness over the acromioclavicular joint. Laboratory tests show mild elevation of C-reactive protein, but normal rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-ray of the right shoulder shows mild degenerative changes but no acute findings. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. She also reports intermittent joint pain, predominantly in her knees and ankles, but denies any morning stiffness. Over the past month, she has noticed enlarged lymph nodes in her neck and under her arms, which are non-tender. She denies any significant weight loss, cough, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. On physical examination, she is febrile to 38.5°C and has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Her laboratory findings show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but normal white blood cell count and differential. A chest X-ray is unremarkable. A lymph node biopsy is planned. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Order autoantibody panel including ANA and anti-dsDNA
options.G: Refer to oncology for further evaluation

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She works as a software engineer and has no recent travel history. On examination, her vital signs are normal. There is symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes that the pain has started to affect both her wrists and several small joints in her hands. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen with minimal relief. On examination, there is visible swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show:
Hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3
Platelet count 230,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
C-reactive protein 5.2 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has difficulty with tasks such as turning doorknobs and opening jars. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies fever, rash, or weight changes. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with warmth and erythema. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting for over an hour and has noticed small bumps under her skin near her elbows and fingers. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and osteoarthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated near the elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the symptoms are particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving. The pain primarily affects her hands, wrists, and knees, and she has difficulty with daily activities. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is 38 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given this clinical presentation?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. She reports stiffness in her fingers, wrists, and knees that lasts for about an hour each morning. She also notes periodic swelling in these joints. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, which are both well controlled with medication. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. There is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a C-reactive protein level of 15 mg/L. Serologic tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with progressive fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the past 3 months. She also reports feeling dizzy at times and has been experiencing occasional headaches. She denies any abdominal pain or changes in bowel habits. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Vital signs are as follows: temperature 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure 128/74 mmHg, pulse 88/min, respirations 14/min. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild tachycardia. Laboratory tests show:

Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 29%
MCV: 80 fL
Ferritin: 15 ng/mL
Vitamin B12: 315 pg/mL (normal)
Folate: 10 ng/mL (normal)

Given these findings, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past three months. She describes the pain as symmetric and affecting her wrists, fingers, and knees, with stiffness lasting for more than an hour in the morning. She has difficulty performing daily activities such as opening jars and buttoning her clothes. Her past medical history is significant for mild hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally, along with a slight ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past 2 weeks. She reports a recent episode of upper respiratory symptoms including sore throat, cough, and low-grade fever, which resolved a week ago. The body aches began shortly after, affecting her muscles and joints, and are particularly bothersome in the mornings. She does not have any significant past medical history and is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness in multiple muscle groups and mild swelling in the wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies show mild leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores over the past year. She also describes episodes of joint pain, particularly in her knees and ankles, which are worse after periods of inactivity. She denies any new rashes or eye symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, multiple shallow ulcers are noted in the oral cavity, and there is tenderness over her knees and ankles without swelling. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain and swelling. She reports recent travel to the Caribbean and mentions multiple mosquito bites during the trip. She complains of fatigue, low-grade fever, and polyarticular arthralgia affecting her wrists, ankles, and knees. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the mentioned joints with no deformities. Laboratory tests show a mild leukopenia and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that her symptoms are primarily in her hands and wrists and are worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving throughout the day. She also notes increased fatigue and some unexplained weight loss. She denies any recent infections or injuries. Her family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Vital signs are stable. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 1-year history of pain in her hands and wrists. The pain is described as a dull ache that is present throughout the day and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting approximately 1 hour. She reports swelling of the joints and difficulty making a fist. On examination, there are palpable subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. The metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman is evaluated for a 3-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and new-onset hypertension. She reports mild swelling in her hands and occasional morning stiffness that resolves within an hour. Her blood pressure is 162/98 mm Hg, and laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatinine level of 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL) with proteinuria on urine analysis. Her past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and a 5-year history of well-controlled hypothyroidism. On physical examination, she has pitting edema in her lower extremities and mild tenderness in the small joints of her hands. Autoimmune serologies show a positive ANA titer. What is the most likely cause of her renal dysfunction?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with lower back pain that has worsened over the past month. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her mid-back, exacerbated by movement and relieved with rest. She denies any history of trauma. Her medical history includes osteoporosis, diagnosed 5 years ago, for which she takes alendronate. She has not had a recent bone density scan. She also reports occasional fatigue and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T8 and T9 vertebrae. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory studies show:

Calcium: 9.8 mg/dL
Vitamin D: 22 ng/mL (low)
C-reactive protein: 1.0 mg/L

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles symmetrically. She reports difficulty with daily activities due to joint swelling and tenderness. Her past medical history includes mild asthma. She denies recent infections, rashes, or changes in bowel habits. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL) with a mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL (reference range: 80–100 fL), and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of lower back pain and stiffness. The pain is most severe in the morning, improving with mild activity throughout the day. She has experienced fatigue and occasional pain in her knees and ankles. She has difficulty bending and reaching for objects on low shelves. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. She has a history of depression managed with sertraline. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. Physical examination shows limited spinal mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of joint pain and stiffness that has persisted for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving throughout the day. The pain affects her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and knees symmetrically. She has noticed swelling in these areas and reports occasional fatigue. She denies any recent infections, and she works as a software engineer, spending long hours at a desk. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, her wrists and fingers are swollen and tender, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, nasal congestion, and recurrent sinusitis over the past 4 months. She also reports experiencing a persistent dry cough and shortness of breath. She has noticed dark urine and swelling in her ankles. Her medical history is significant for repeated episodes of sinus infections and a recent diagnosis of hypertension. On examination, her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 140/95 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 20/min. Respiratory examination reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory studies show elevated serum creatinine and proteinuria. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely finding in a biopsy of her renal tissue?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the emergency department due to sudden onset of severe back pain after lifting a light box at home. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region. She has a history of osteopenia diagnosed 5 years ago and is on calcium and vitamin D supplements. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension treated with lisinopril. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T8 vertebra and limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is normal, and there are no signs of spinal cord compression. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis or step to confirm the diagnosis?

options.A: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in both her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. She also notes the presence of several firm, non-tender nodules under the skin on her elbows. She denies recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surface of the forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. An X-ray of the hands shows juxta-articular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following findings is most likely to be present?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of profound fatigue and joint pain affecting her wrists and knees bilaterally. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She denies any recent infections or fevers. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and knees. Laboratory investigations reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hour, and a normal thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain. Over the last 6 months, she has experienced gradually worsening morning stiffness lasting over an hour and pain affecting her wrists and fingers symmetrically. She also reports feeling unusually tired despite adequate sleep. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medication. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 52 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain involving her hands and wrists. The pain is especially severe in the morning and tends to improve as the day progresses. She has noted swelling in her fingers and difficulty with gripping objects. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Her mother had a similar condition in her 50s. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 12.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 8,000/mm3, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-CCP antibody levels are significantly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive muscle weakness over the past 3 months. She describes difficulty climbing stairs and lifting heavy objects. She denies any joint pain, swelling, or morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness in both the upper and lower extremities, without atrophy or fasciculations. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatine kinase levels. Electromyography demonstrates myopathic changes. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with her presentation?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour and difficulty performing tasks requiring fine motor skills. She has noticed small, firm nodules under her skin around the elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroxine. On physical examination, her wrists and several metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of both forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping. She describes the pain as a constant ache affecting her neck, shoulders, back, and hips. She reports feeling unrefreshed even after a full night's sleep and has noticed increased forgetfulness and difficulty concentrating. Her medical history is notable for treated hypothyroidism. The patient has tried over-the-counter pain medications with little relief. On physical examination, there are tender points at the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and medial knee bilaterally. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. Laboratory tests show normal thyroid function, a normal complete blood count, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 18 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.F: Obtain magnetic resonance imaging of the spine

question:
A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks of gestation presents to the clinic complaining of persistent back and pelvic pain for the past month. The pain is described as a dull ache that intensifies with prolonged standing or walking and is relieved by lying down. She denies any prior history of back pain before her pregnancy. Her past medical history is significant for mild anemia managed with oral iron supplements. She is otherwise healthy and her pregnancy has been uncomplicated thus far.

On physical examination, her vital signs are stable with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, heart rate of 82/min, and temperature of 36.8°C (98.2°F). Palpation reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints but no swelling or erythema. Neurological examination of the lower extremities is normal, and there are no signs of radiculopathy. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate, with a negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most appropriate management for this patient's condition?

options.A: Physical therapy focusing on core strengthening
options.B: Acetaminophen as needed for pain relief
options.E: Referral to a rheumatologist for further evaluation
options.G: Use of a maternity support belt

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the office with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores that have persisted for the past 6 months. She also describes episodes of joint pain predominantly affecting her knees and wrists, which are worse in the morning, improving as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for iritis 2 years ago. She denies any recent travel or new sexual partners and reports feeling fatigued. On examination, there are multiple shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and erosive lesions on the labia. Her joints are without obvious swelling but are tender to palpation. Recent laboratory tests reveal the following: hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, ESR 30 mm/hr, and negative rheumatoid factor. A pathergy test is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that the pain is symmetric, primarily affecting her wrists and knees. Morning stiffness lasts for about two hours and improves with activity. She has tried over-the-counter analgesics with minimal relief. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, her wrists and knees are swollen and tender, with decreased range of motion. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour, which improves throughout the day. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 12 mg/L, and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Radiographs of the hands show early erosive changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores. She reports episodes of mouth ulcers occurring every few weeks, accompanied by deep, painful genital ulcers. She also experiences occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, but denies any morning stiffness or significant swelling. She feels fatigued and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the last 2 months. Her family history is unremarkable. On examination, there are multiple aphthous ulcers in her oral cavity and tender nodules on her lower legs. A genital examination reveals ulcerative lesions. Laboratory tests show an ESR of 50 mm/hour and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that the joint pain is symmetric, primarily affecting her wrists and fingers, and is worse in the morning, improving throughout the day. She also notes increased fatigue and has observed swelling in her hands. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no medications and denies alcohol or tobacco use. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is most consistent with her diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain that starts in the morning and is associated with stiffness. She mentions that the pain improves with physical activity and is worse with rest. She denies any trauma or overuse injury. She has no significant medical history but mentions occasional fatigue and some mild pain in her hips. Her family history is notable for a cousin with psoriasis. On examination, her vitals are stable, and she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Radiographs of the pelvis show sclerosis and erosion at the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.G: Refer for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the sacroiliac joints

question: A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to ongoing fatigue and joint pain. She reports that her symptoms began about 3 months ago, starting with fatigue that she initially attributed to her busy work schedule as a lawyer. Over the past month, she developed swelling and pain in her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands. She describes the joint pain as worse in the morning, taking over an hour to improve with activity. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-15.5 g/dL), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), and a negative antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressively worsening skin tightness over her fingers and hands over the past year. She also reports color changes in her fingers upon exposure to cold, transitioning from white to blue, and then red. She feels generally fatigued and has experienced occasional joint pain in her hands. She denies any history of fevers, weight loss, or respiratory symptoms. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows shiny, tight skin on her fingers and mild digital ulcerations. Nailfold capillary examination reveals dilated capillary loops. Laboratory tests show a positive anti-centromere antibody. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of fatigue, unintentional weight loss, and swollen glands in her neck and armpits for the past two months. She also reports occasional joint pain and night sweats. The patient denies any recent infections or travel history. She has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, she has multiple cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 83/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A lymph node biopsy is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain. She describes stiffness lasting for more than an hour in the morning, which improves as the day progresses. The pain predominantly affects her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints symmetrically. Her medical history includes occasional tension headaches. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the affected joints. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), and vital signs are otherwise normal. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An x-ray of her hands shows periarticular osteopenia and erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Start a biologic agent like etanercept
options.C: Perform joint aspiration for synovial fluid analysis

question: A 50-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with worsening hand pain and deformity over the past 3 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her fingers and wrists. Her medical history is significant for a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis one year ago. She is currently taking methotrexate with partial relief of symptoms. Physical examination reveals subluxation of the metacarpophalangeal joints, swan neck deformities, and ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and positive anti-CCP antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis given these findings?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is most severe in the morning and affects her wrists, knees, and fingers bilaterally. She also notes that she has been feeling more tired than usual and has had difficulty performing her daily activities due to the stiffness and pain. Her past medical history is significant for mild hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. She denies any recent travel or infections. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies demonstrate a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, with a mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, particularly in her wrists and fingers, which improves as the day progresses. Her mother had a similar condition in her later years. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally and mild ulnar deviation of the hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Hand X-rays demonstrate juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions.

What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of persistent low back pain. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. The patient also mentions feeling fatigued and describes occasional episodes of pain in her hips and shoulders. She denies any trauma to the area. Her family history is significant for a father with psoriasis. On physical examination, there is limited range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint swelling and pain that has been progressively worsening for the past 3 months. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting her wrists and fingers, and notes that it is particularly severe in the morning but improves throughout the day with activity. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor (RF) test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of chronic pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. The symptoms are most severe in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour. She reports difficulty in activities like opening jars and buttoning her shirt. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine. On examination, her vital signs are normal. There is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with subcutaneous nodules palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, joint pain, and occasional fevers for the past three months. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes swelling in her wrists and fingers. She has also experienced unintended weight loss and night sweats. Her past medical history includes recurrent oral ulcers and photosensitivity. She does not smoke or drink alcohol and has no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals pallor, mild hepatosplenomegaly, and tenderness in the small joints of the hands with visible swelling. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 3,200/mm³, and platelet count of 112,000/mm³. Antinuclear antibody test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and joint pain in her hands and wrists for the past 8 weeks. She notes that the pain is symmetric and improves with activity. Her past medical history includes osteopenia and seasonal allergies. She does not smoke and denies alcohol use. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints on both hands. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 65 mm/h, and a positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She describes the morning stiffness as taking more than an hour to improve. She has also noticed small, firm bumps under the skin near her elbows. She has a family history of rheumatoid arthritis, as her mother was diagnosed in her late 50s. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, and she is on levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with decreased range of motion. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 3-month history of unexplained weight gain, facial puffiness, and joint stiffness, particularly in the morning. She reports feeling unusually fatigued and has noticed her shoes and rings are tighter than usual. She denies any changes in diet or physical activity, and her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and mild swelling of the hands, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 11.8 g/dL, white blood cell count of 8,500/mm3, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/h. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of lower back pain that has progressively worsened. She describes the pain as dull and aching, primarily located in the lower back and buttocks, and it sometimes radiates down her thighs. She notes that the pain is worse in the morning, improving with activity throughout the day, and occasionally wakes her up at night. She denies any recent injuries, fevers, chills, or weight loss, but reports occasional stiffness in her hips. Her family history is significant for a father with psoriasis. Physical examination reveals limited movement of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Pelvic X-ray reveals bilateral sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and fatigue. She mentions developing a red rash on her cheeks after spending some time in the sun. She also reports intermittent mouth sores and joint stiffness lasting more than an hour every morning. Her vital signs are stable, and physical examination reveals a malar rash and joint tenderness in her wrists and fingers. Laboratory results show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
